ca-fro-civpro:affirmative-defenses.html:
The defendant must assert affirmative defenses or they are deemed waived. Common affirmative defenses include:

  • Assumption of the risk
  • Release
  • Contributory negligence
  • Statute of frauds
  • Fraud
  • Statute of limitations
  • Duress
Deadlines to Serve Answer

Federal

California

General rule

  • An answer is due within 21 days after the defendant receives service of process
  • An answer is due within 30 days after being served

Service waived

  • Defendant in the U.S.: Answer is due within 60 days after the waiver request was sent
  • Defendant in foreign country: Answer is due within 90 days after the waiver request was sent
  • Waiver of service does not extend the time to respond to a complaint

FRCP 12 motion filed

  • Answer is due within 14 days after notice of the court’s decision on the motion
  • If the defendant files a demurrer or a motion to strike that is then denied, an answer must be filed within 10 days of service of notice of the denial
CA distinction

Note that a defendant must seek affirmative relief in a cross-complaint—not in an answer. The term “cross-complaint” encompasses counterclaims, crossclaims, and third-party complaints. A related cause of action against the plaintiff must be brought as a compulsory cross-complaint or it will be barred.

ca-fro-civpro:amount-in-controversy.html:
A plaintiff’s good-faith assertion in the complaint that the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000 is sufficient unless there is a legal certainty that the plaintiff cannot recover that amount.

Aggregation of claims is permitted for:

  • A single plaintiff against a single defendant
  • A single plaintiff against multiple defendants that are jointly liable or
  • Multiple plaintiffs against a single defendant if the plaintiffs have a common and undivided interest.

Counterclaims are generally not counted in determining whether the plaintiff has satisfied the amount- in-controversy requirement. Instead, a permissive counterclaim must meet the amount-in-controversy requirement, but a compulsory counterclaim need not because supplemental jurisdiction will apply to that claim.

ca-fro-civpro:bases-for-in-personam-juri.html:
Voluntary presence: A state has personal jurisdiction over a defendant who is voluntarily present in the state and served with process while there unless the person was (1) in the state only to answer a summons or (2) brought to the state by force or fraud.

CA distinction

There is no immunity from service of process for nonresidents who come to California to temporarily participate in a trial or deposition.

Domicile: If authorized by statute, a state has personal jurisdiction over a person domiciled in the state. Domicile is where one resides with the intent to remain indefinitely.

Consent: A defendant can consent to personal jurisdiction in three ways:

1. Expressly by contract, by stipulation, or by authorizing an agent

2. Impliedly through conduct

3. Voluntarily by appearing in court, unless the defendant appears to object to jurisdiction

Long-arm statute: A long-arm statute is a state statute that gives courts within the state personal jurisdiction over out-of-state defendants. Most of these statutes authorize personal jurisdiction over nonresidents who engage in some activity in the state or cause some action to occur within the state to the extent permissible under the Due Process Clause.

CA distinction

The California long-arm statute allows a state court to exercise jurisdiction on any basis that is in accord with the California or U.S. Constitution.

Because California’s long-arm statute is not more restrictive than the constitutional requirements for due process, the due process analysis for personal jurisdiction in a California state court mirrors the analysis that would be conducted in federal court.

Attachment of property: If a claim is not related to ownership of attached property, then there must be minimum contacts between the defendant and the forum state to establish personal jurisdiction.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ MULTIPLE PARTIES AND CLAIMS
ca-fro-civpro:basic-requirements.html:
• Numerosity: The class must be so numerous that joinder of all members is impracticable.
  • Commonality: There must be questions of law or fact common to the class.
  • Typicality: The claims or defenses of the class representatives must be typical of the class.
  • Adequacy: The class representatives must fairly and adequately protect the interests of the class.

ca-fro-civpro:certification-of-pleadings.html:
Most pleadings are not, and are not required to be, verified. But pleadings and other documents must be certified, i.e., signed by an attorney of record and provide the attorney’s contact information. The signature certifies that there is an appropriate factual and legal basis for the filing and that to the best of the attorney’s knowledge:

  • There is no improper purpose (e.g., to harass or needlessly increase costs)
  • The legal contentions are warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for a change in the law and
  • The factual allegations have evidentiary support or are likely to have such support after an opportunity for discovery.

Violation of the certification requirement can be raised by the court or by a party in a motion for sanctions.

ca-fro-civpro:change-of-venue.html:
If venue is improper, then a court must dismiss the case or transfer it to a proper district if it is in the interest of justice. A case can be transferred to any district where the case might have originally been brought or to which all parties consent. A court lacking personal jurisdiction over the defendant may also transfer the case to a different venue.

receiving court

CA distinction

Venue can be transferred when the court designated in the complaint is the wrong court. And even if venue is proper, the court may transfer venue if:

  • A fair trial cannot be had in the original county
  • The convenience of the witnesses and the ends of justice so require or
  • There is no judge of the court qualified to act.

Venue-selection clauses are enforceable in California unless the proposed forum is unavailable or enforcement of the clause would be unreasonable.

ca-fro-civpro:chapter-iv-choice-of-law-t.t: CHAPTER IV ▪ CHOICE OF LAW: THE ERIE DOCTRINE
ca-fro-civpro:chapter-iv-choice-of-law-t.html:
Conflict of laws is the part of each state’s law used in cases with connections to multiple states to identify which state’s laws will determine the parties’ rights. In contrast, choice of law more commonly refers to a federal court sitting in diversity determining whether state or federal law controls a given issue.

Choice of Law in Federal Court

Jurisdiction

Substantive Issues

Procedural Issues

Federal question

Federal law

Federal law

Diversity

State law

Federal law

Supplemental

State law (for state-law claims)

Federal law

ca-fro-civpro:claim-preclusion-vs-issue-.html:
Claim Preclusion (Res Judicata) Issue Preclusion (Collateral Estoppel)

CA distinction

Claim Preclusion:

  • A judgment is not final until the conclusion of the appeal or the time for appeal has expired.
  • California courts do not apply the modern “transactional” approach in analyzing the “identical claims” prong (as the federal rule does). Instead, California courts apply the minority “primary rights” doctrine, under which causes of action are based on individual harm and redress may be sought for one harm in each suit.

Issue Preclusion:

  • Default and consent judgments are given issue-preclusive effect as to material issues that were actually raised in the plead.
Exam tip

Discuss claim preclusion and issue preclusion separately and in order: 1. Is the claim in the second suit precluded by prior adjudication? If yes, the inquiry ends. 2. If not, are there any issues in the second suit precluded by the prior adjudication?

ca-fro-civpro:complete-diversity.html:
There is generally no diversity of citizenship if any plaintiff is a citizen of the same state as any defendant. But minimal diversity (i.e., when at least one plaintiff is diverse from at least one defendant, even if the citizenship of other plaintiffs and defendants overlaps) is sufficient in the following circumstances:

  • Federal Interpleader Act (i.e., statutory interpleader actions)
  • Class Action Fairness Act (i.e., actions with at least 100 class members and claims worth more than $5 million)
  • Interstate mass torts if at least 75 natural persons have died in one accident and the plaintiffs and defendants are from many different states (e.g., airline crash)
Citizenship of Parties

Individual

Citizen of the state where the individual is domiciled (i.e., physically present with the intent to remain indefinitely)

  • Can have only one domicile at a time
  • A foreign citizen admitted to the U.S. as a permanent resident is treated as a citizen of the state of domicile

Corporation

Citizen of every state where the corporation:

  • Is incorporated
  • Has its principal place of business (i.e., its “nerve center” or corporate headquarters)

Unincorporated association

Citizen of every state where its members are domiciled

  • Applies to partnerships, limited partnerships, trade associations, and unions

Legal representative

Citizenship of the legal representative controls except:

  • The citizenship of a decedent controls for litigation involving the decedent’s estate and
  • The citizenship of an infant or incompetent person controls for guardianship of the minor or incompetent person

Class action members

Citizenship of the named class representative(s) controls, and unnamed class members may join without regard to citizenship

Exam tip

When a corporation is a party, complete diversity between plaintiffs and defendants is still required. Therefore, you must consider EVERY state where the corporation has citizenship.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ MULTIPLE PARTIES AND CLAIMS
CHAPTER VI ▪ MULTIPLE PARTIES AND CLAIMS
ca-fro-civpro:counterclaims.html:
A counterclaim is a claim for relief against an opposing party after an original claim has been asserted. An answer to a counterclaim is due within 21 days of service. There are two types of counterclaims:

  • Compulsory counterclaims arise out of the same transaction or occurrence that is the subject matter of the opposing party’s claim and do not require adding another party over whom the court has no jurisdiction. A court will have supplemental jurisdiction over a compulsory counterclaim, so it need not satisfy federal question or diversity jurisdiction.
  • Permissive counterclaims are any counterclaims that are not compulsory, and a party has discretion whether to assert it in the current action. Subject matter jurisdiction over a permissive counterclaim must be established by federal question or diversity jurisdiction.

Third parties can assert counterclaims against the original plaintiff or defendant, and these counterclaims are governed by the above requirements for counterclaims and joinder.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ MULTIPLE PARTIES AND CLAIMS
ca-fro-civpro:crossclaims.html:
A crossclaim is a claim made against a coparty (e.g., a claim by a defendant against another defendant). An answer to a crossclaim is due within 21 days of service.

A crossclaim may be asserted if it arises out of the same transaction or occurrence as the original action or a counterclaim. Any new parties are subject to the joinder rules.

Personal jurisdiction will be satisfied because the parties are already before the court. And because venue will be determined by the original claim, a party cannot object to venue over a crossclaim.

Exam tip

Crossclaims will often fall under supplemental jurisdiction—but not always. Remember to analyze whether the crossclaim satisfies federal subject matter jurisdiction on its own or through supplemental jurisdiction.

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ CHOICE OF LAW: THE ERIE DOCTRINE
ca-fro-civpro:determining-applicable-sta.html:
A federal district court exercising diversity jurisdiction over a case is bound by the conflict-of-laws rules of the state in which the court is located, but only to the extent that the state’s rules are valid under the Full Faith and Credit Clause and the Due Process Clause. States apply their own procedural laws and sometimes apply the substantive laws of another jurisdiction.

CA distinction

Conflicts of law are resolved under the following principles:

  • Real property disputes: Apply California law, except where title is held by the U.S. government.
  • Tort claims: Apply the government-interest approach when a conflict exists.
  • Contract claims: Apply a choice-of-law provision if it is enforceable and does not conflict with California public policy. Otherwise, apply the government-interest approach.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ PRETRIAL PROCEDURE AND DISCOVERY
ca-fro-civpro:discovery-conference.html:
Except in a proceeding exempted from the initial-disclosure rules or if the court orders otherwise, Rule 26(f) requires that the parties confer as soon as practicable. “As soon as practicable” means at least 21 days before a scheduling conference is to be held or a scheduling order is due under Rule 16(b). The purpose of a Rule 26(f) discovery conference is to:

  • Consider the nature and basis of the parties’ claims and defenses and the possibilities for promptly resolving the case
  • Make or arrange for the required initial disclosures
  • Discuss any issues about preserving discoverable information and
  • Develop a proposed discovery plan.
CA distinction

Parties are not required to “meet and confer” or otherwise provide a proposed discovery plan. Instead, the court must hold an initial case management conference within 180 days of the filing of a complaint.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ PRETRIAL PROCEDURE AND DISCOVERY
ca-fro-civpro:discovery-devices.html:
Methods of Discovery

Federal

California

Deposition

  • Written or oral examination of a party or a witness under oath that can occur any time after the discovery conference
  • Each party is limited to 10 depositions unless good cause is shown
  • A subpoena duces tecum requires a nonparty deponent to bring specified documents or things to the deposition
  • No limit on the number of depositions a party may take
  • 20-day time limit on notice of the deposition

Interrogatories

  • Written questions served on a party that must be fully and separately answered under oath unless timely objected to with specificity
  • A party may serve no more than 25 written interrogatories on another party
  • Written responses are due within 30 days of service
  • Unlimited number of form interrogatories
  • Up to 35 special interrogatories

Request for production or inspection

  • Request served on a party to produce and allow inspection of documents, electronic information, tangible items, or land
  • No limit on the number of requests
  • Written response is due within 30 days of service or 30 days from the parties’ first discovery conference if the request was served prior to that conference

No distinction

Requests for admission

  • Requests served on a party to admit the truth of facts within the scope of discovery
  • Once admitted, the fact is conclusively established
  • No limit on the number of requests
  • Written responses are due within 30 days of service
  • Limited to 35 requests

Physical or mental exam

  • Examination of a party whose physical or mental condition is in controversy
  • Ordered by the court or agreed to by the parties
  • An attorney must be permitted to attend the physical exam
Exam tip

Remember that California imposes no limits on the number of depositions a party may take, form interrogatories a party may serve, or requests for production or inspection a party may serve.

ca-fro-civpro:diversity-jurisdiction.html:
Federal courts have diversity jurisdiction when:

  • The opposing parties are citizens of different states or citizens of a state and citizens of a foreign state and
  • The amount in controversy exceeds $75,000, i.e., the plaintiff must seek at least $75,000.01.

ca-fro-civpro:diversity-jurisdiction-2.html:
When a federal court has diversity jurisdiction over a claim, the court can exercise supplemental jurisdiction over additional claims if the “common nucleus of operative fact” test is met and the claims are not asserted by:

  • Existing plaintiffs (not defendants) against persons made parties through impleader, compulsory joinder, permissive joinder, or intervention or
  • Persons compulsorily joined or seeking to intervene as plaintiffs.

The following rules also apply:

  • An additional claim asserted by a permissively joined plaintiff cannot violate the complete diversity rule (even though the plaintiff need not satisfy the jurisdictional amount).
  • Compulsory counterclaims need not satisfy the jurisdictional amount, but permissive counterclaims must satisfy both diversity jurisdiction requirements.
  • Crossclaims may be asserted by a defendant against another defendant or by a plaintiff against another plaintiff if the crossclaim arises out of the same transaction or occurrence as the initial claim.

ca-fro-civpro:due-process-requirements.html:
Due process is satisfied if a basis for in personam jurisdiction exists or if a nonresident defendant has sufficient minimum contacts with the forum state such that maintaining the action does not offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice.

Minimum contacts can be established through purposeful availment, which occurs when the defendant’s contacts with the forum state are so purposeful and substantial that the defendant should reasonably anticipate being taken to court there. The scope of the contacts necessary to assert personal jurisdiction depends on the relationship that the cause of action has with the forum state:

  • Specific jurisdiction: Such jurisdiction may be warranted over a cause of action that arises out of or closely relates to a defendant’s contact with the forum state—even if that is the defendant’s only contact with the forum state.
  • General jurisdiction: Such jurisdiction may be warranted over a party—even when the cause of action has no relationship to the defendant’s contacts with the forum state—if that party is domiciled in, or has continuous and systematic contacts with, the forum state.

Once minimum contacts are established, a court must examine the facts to determine if maintenance of the action would offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice. Factors include the interest of the forum state in adjudicating the matter, the burden on the defendant of appearing in the case, the interest of the judicial system in the efficient resolution of controversies, and the shared interests of the states in promoting common social policies.

Exam tip

When analyzing a personal jurisdiction question, the focus is on (1) the contacts that the defendant has or had with the forum state and (2) whether the court’s assertion of jurisdiction would comport with fair play and substantial justice. Personal jurisdiction will depend on the facts of each case.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ PRETRIAL PROCEDURE AND DISCOVERY
ca-fro-civpro:experts.html:
Testifying experts may be deposed, and their reports are discoverable. However, drafts of expert reports and Rule 26(a) disclosures are protected—as are any communications between the party’s attorney and the expert witness—unless they:

  • Relate to the expert’s compensation or
  • Identify facts, data, or assumptions provided by the party’s attorney and used or relied upon by the expert in forming her opinion.

If an expert is not testifying, then discovery is prohibited absent exceptional circumstances.

CA distinction

A party in California can request (1) that all parties simultaneously disclose expert witness lists, details of prospective trial testimony, and all writings/reports and (2) that experts be available for deposition.

ca-fro-civpro:federal-question-jurisdict.html:
Federal courts have original (subject matter) jurisdiction over all civil actions arising under the Constitution, laws, or treaties of the United States.

State courts and federal courts have concurrent jurisdiction over federal question claims—except when Congress expressly provides that the jurisdiction of the federal courts is exclusive.

When a federal court has federal question jurisdiction over a claim, the court can exercise supplemental jurisdiction over additional claims against the same party and/or additional parties if the “common nucleus of operative fact” test is met.

ca-fro-civpro:federal-rule.html:
In federal court, a complaint must contain:

  • A short and plain statement of the court’s subject matter jurisdiction
  • A short and plain statement of the claim establishing entitlement to relief and
  • A demand for judgment for the relief sought.
CA distinction

Federal rules allow notice pleading, while California requires fact pleading (i.e., all material, operative facts that give rise to a cause of action must be alleged). Therefore, in California state courts, all pleadings must contain a statement of facts constituting each cause of action and a demand for relief. If damages are sought, then the amount must be stated (except in personal injury or wrongful death cases).

ca-fro-civpro:final-judgment-rule.html:
Federal appellate courts generally only have jurisdiction to hear appeals from a final judgment, i.e., a district court decision that fully resolves the dispute on the merits and leaves nothing for the court to do but enforce the judgment. However, if a case has multiple claims or parties, a district court may direct entry of a final judgment as to fewer than all claims or parties—but only if the court expressly determines that there is no just reason for delay.

Exam tip

Remember that California’s definition of a final judgment is broader than the federal definition. In California, any judgment against one of multiple parties that resolves all issues related to that party qualifies as a final judgment. But under the federal rules, such a judgment is only final if the court orders and expressly states as such.

ca-fro-civpro:general-venue-rule.html:
Venue in a federal civil action is proper in any of the following judicial districts:

  • Where any defendant resides, if all defendants reside in the same state in which the district is located
  • Where a “substantial part of the events or omissions” on which the claim is based occurred
  • Where a “substantial part of the property” that is the subject of the action is located
  • If venue cannot be established in a district on any of the above grounds, where any defendant is subject to personal jurisdiction with respect to the action (i.e., the “fallback” alternative)
Residency for Venue Purposes

Status

Plaintiff

Defendant

Individual

Resides in the federal district where the individual is domiciled

Business entity

Resides in the federal district where it has its principal place of business

Resides in a federal district where it is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction

CA distinction

In California, venue refers to the county in which an action may be tried. In actions in which the claim arose elsewhere (i.e., transitory actions), venue is proper in the county where the defendant resides at the time the action commences or, if no defendant is from California, any county.

There are also specific venue rules for the following actions:

  • Contract actions: Venue lies where the contract was executed or performed.
  • Personal injury/wrongful death actions: Venue lies where the injury occurred.
  • Mixed actions: Venue generally lies where any defendant resides.
  • Against corporations, associations, and partnerships: Venue lies in the county where the contract was executed or performed, the obligation or liability arose, or the breach occurred. For corporations only, venue also lies where the corporation has its principal place of business.
Exam tip

Remember that venue in federal court focuses on whether the case was brought in a proper judicial district, while venue in California state court focuses on whether the case was brought in a proper county.

ca-fro-civpro:in-general.html:
Subject matter jurisdiction refers to a court’s competence to hear and determine cases of a general class and subject. Unlike personal jurisdiction, subject matter jurisdiction cannot be waived or agreed to by the parties.

CA distinction

Federal subject matter jurisdiction is limited to enumerated categories of cases (i.e., federal question and diversity). In contrast, California state courts have general jurisdiction over all civil cases, which fall into two classifications:

  • Unlimited: The amount in controversy is more than $35,000
  • Limited: The amount in controversy is $35,000 or less
    • Small claims: Limited civil actions in which the amount in controversy is $12,500 or less for actions by individuals and $6,250 or less for actions by other parties

If a civil case was initially or later becomes misclassified, subject matter jurisdiction is not lost. For an initially misclassified case, the court may either:

  • Reclassify the case from unlimited to limited if the verdict will necessarily be $35,000 or less or
  • Reclassify the case from limited to unlimited if the verdict might exceed $35,000.

Additionally, the clerk will automatically reclassify the case if (1) the plaintiff amends the complaint and changes the amount in controversy above or below $35,000 or (2) a cross- complaint increases the amount in controversy of the entire case above $35,000.

Remember that the term “amount in controversy” has different meanings in California and federal courts. In federal court, the amount in controversy must be satisfied to establish diversity jurisdiction. But in California, the amount in controversy simply affects how the case is classified— not whether jurisdiction is established.

ca-fro-civpro:in-general-2.html:
Personal jurisdiction refers to a court’s ability to exercise judicial power over the persons (namely, the defendants) or property involved in the case or controversy before it.

A party may expressly, impliedly, or voluntarily consent to personal jurisdiction. Therefore, a defendant’s right to challenge a court’s exercise of personal jurisdiction is waived if the defendant fails to object to personal jurisdiction in a pre-answer motion or in the defendant’s answer.

The Due Process Clause precludes a federal court from exercising personal jurisdiction over a defendant unless the defendant has “minimum contacts” with the forum state and the exercise of jurisdiction would be fair and reasonable.

ca-fro-civpro:interlocutory-review.html:
Although most interlocutory orders are not immediately appealable, a court of appeals has the discretion to permit an appeal of such an order if the district court certifies in writing that:

  • The order involves a controlling question of law on which there is substantial ground for difference of opinion and
  • An immediate appeal may materially advance termination of the litigation.

Appellate courts also have discretion to hear interlocutory appeals from orders certifying or refusing to certify a class action.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ MULTIPLE PARTIES AND CLAIMS
ca-fro-civpro:interpleader.html:
Interpleader allows a person holding property to force all potential claimants to that property into a single lawsuit. There are two types in federal court: rule interpleader and statutory interpleader.

Rule Interpleader vs. Statutory Interpleader

Requirement

Rule Interpleader

Statutory Interpleader

Subject matter jurisdiction

Complete diversity between stakeholder and claimants + amount in controversy > $75,000 or Federal question jurisdiction

Minimal diversity between claimants + amount in controversy ≥ $500

Personal jurisdiction

Governed by the general requirements for in personam jurisdiction in federal court

Nationwide personal jurisdiction and service of process permitted

Venue

Governed by traditional venue rules

Proper in any district where a claimant resides

Exam tip

Statutory interpleader is meant to make federal court more accessible. All usual roadblocks are much lower—the amount in controversy need only be $500, there is nationwide service of process, venue is proper where any claimant resides, and only minimal diversity is required.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ MULTIPLE PARTIES AND CLAIMS
ca-fro-civpro:intervention-as-of-right.html:
A nonparty has a right to intervene in an ongoing action if a federal statute confers that right or the nonparty timely moves to intervene when:

  • The nonparty has an interest in the property or transaction that is the subject matter of the action
  • The disposition of the action may impair the nonparty’s interest and
  • The existing parties do not adequately represent the nonparty’s interest.

ca-fro-civpro:judgment-as-a-matter-of-la.t: JUDGMENT AS A MATTER OF LAW
ca-fro-civpro:judgment-as-a-matter-of-la.html: A motion for judgment as a matter of law (i.e., JMOL or directed verdict) challenges the sufficiency of the evidence in a civil jury trial. Prior to submission to the jury, a party may file a motion for JMOL. After the verdict and entry of judgment, a party may renew its motion for JMOL (i.e., judgment notwithstanding the verdict or JNOV).

ca-fro-civpro:methods-of-service-on-indi.html:
Process may be served on an individual located within the United States by:

  • Following the service-of-process rules of the forum state or the state in which the service occurred
  • Serving the individual personally
  • Leaving process at the individual’s usual place of abode with a person of suitable age and discretion who resides there or
  • Delivering process to the individual’s appointed agent.

Persons in foreign countries may be served by mail requiring a signed receipt.

CA distinction

When substituted service (i.e., service made by leaving the summons and complaint at the defendant’s home or office with a person over the age of 18) is made in California, a follow-up mailing to the defendant is required.

Service by publication is allowed if the defendant cannot be served by reasonable diligence.

Service by first class mail/return receipt is allowed for out-of-state defendants. Jurisdiction may only be exercised if the nonresident has minimum contacts with California.

Exam tip

Watch for fact patterns in which (1) a case is filed in federal court in California or (2) a case is filed in any federal court and the defendant is served in California. In either of these scenarios, service may be made by any method permitted under the federal rules OR California law.

ca-fro-civpro:motion-for-judgment-as-a-m.t: Motion for Judgment as a Matter of Law
crumb: JUDGMENT AS A MATTER OF LAW
ca-fro-civpro:motion-for-judgment-as-a-m.html: A motion for JMOL may be made any time before the case is submitted to the jury. The court should view the evidence in the light most favorable to the opposing party and draw all reasonable inferences from the evidence in favor of that party.

ca-fro-civpro:motion-for-judgment-on-the.t: Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings
ca-fro-civpro:motion-for-judgment-on-the.html: After the pleadings are closed, a motion for judgment on the pleadings allows a court to dispose of a case when the material facts are not in dispute and a judgment on the merits can be achieved based solely on the content of the pleadings.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ PRETRIAL PROCEDURE AND DISCOVERY
ca-fro-civpro:motion-to-compel.html:
A party can move to compel disclosure or discovery against a party who failed to make automatic disclosures or to respond to discovery requests (including evasive or incomplete disclosures).

Exam tip

A party must make a reasonable, good-faith effort to informally resolve the issue before filing a motion to compel.

ca-fro-civpro:motion-to-strike.html:
When a pleading contains any insufficient defense—or redundant, immaterial, impertinent, or scandalous material—a court may order such defense or material stricken upon a party’s motion or on its own initiative. This allows the court to avoid wasting time and money litigating invalid issues.

CA distinction

Within 30 days after being served with a pleading, a party can move to strike any irrelevant, false, or improper matter included in the pleading. Before filing the motion, the moving party must meet and confer in person or by telephone with the party who filed the pleading at issue to determine if an agreement can be reached that resolves the objections to be raised in the motion.

A defendant may, within 60 days after service of the complaint, file an anti-SLAPP motion to strike a complaint when the plaintiff has filed a “strategic lawsuit against public participation” (used only in response to certain public-interest lawsuits, class actions, and actions arising from commercial statements/conduct).

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ MULTIPLE PARTIES AND CLAIMS
ca-fro-civpro:permissive-intervention.html:
A court may allow a nonparty to intervene in an ongoing action—after the court considers any undue delay or prejudice to the original parties that may result—if:

  • The nonparty has a conditional right to intervene under a federal statute or
  • The nonparty’s claim or defense and the original action share a common question of law or fact.
CA distinction

California requires that the intervenor’s interest be “direct and immediate.”

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ MULTIPLE PARTIES AND CLAIMS
ca-fro-civpro:permissive-joinder.html:
Plaintiffs and defendants may join and be joined in one action if:

  • Any right to relief is asserted jointly, severally, or with respect to or arising out of the same transaction, occurrence, or series thereof and
  • A question of law or fact that is common to all plaintiffs or defendants will arise.

Subject matter jurisdiction must exist over the action with the joined plaintiffs or defendants. And in a diversity jurisdiction action, supplemental jurisdiction:

  • Does not apply for joined defendants, so there must be complete diversity between the plaintiffs and the defendants and each claim must exceed $75,000 but
  • Is permitted for joined plaintiffs even if the amount in controversy is not established (but there still must be complete diversity).
CA distinction

Plaintiffs may be joined if:

  • They are asserting a right to relief that arises from the same transaction/occurrence and a common question of law or fact will arise or
  • They have a claim adverse to the defendant.

Similar rules apply to permissive joinder of defendants.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ MULTIPLE PARTIES AND CLAIMS
ca-fro-civpro:permissive-joinder-2.html:
A party may join independent or alternative claims of whatever nature against an opposing party. However, subject matter jurisdiction over permissively joined claims includes special rules:

  • Diversity jurisdiction: The plaintiff may aggregate all claims to satisfy the amount-in-controversy requirement against a single defendant.
  • Federal question jurisdiction: Nonfederal claims can be joined only if the requirements for diversity jurisdiction or supplemental jurisdiction are satisfied.
CA distinction

The California rule is substantially similar to the federal rule, except when several defendants have been joined. In those cases, California requires that there be at least one question of law or fact common to all defendants, and the court may sever claims to avoid undue delay or prejudice.

ca-fro-civpro:relation-back.html:
The relation-back doctrine applies when the statute of limitations has run and a party wants to amend the pleading.

  • New claim asserted: The amendment relates back to the date of the original pleading if the amendment asserts a claim or defense that arose out of the same conduct, transaction, or occurrence as the original pleading.
CA distinction

California has two additional requirements—that the amended pleading (1) involve the same accident and injuries and (2) refer to the same offending instrumentality.

  • New party: The amendment relates back to the date of the original pleading if the amendment asserts a claim or defense that arises out of the same conduct, transaction, or occurrence as the original pleading plus the new party:
    • Receives notice of the action within 90 days after the original pleading was filed and
    • Knew or should have known about the action but for the mistake concerning the proper party’s identity.
CA distinction

California does not have a parallel provision, but it does allow a plaintiff to correct a “misnomer” in naming a defendant, even when the statute of limitations has run. California also allows substitution of the true name of a defendant for a fictitious “Doe” label in the complaint if:

  • The original complaint was timely filed and contained sufficient factual allegations against all defendants
  • The plaintiff was genuinely ignorant of the defendant’s identity, of facts giving rise to a cause of action, or that the law allows a cause of action and
  • The plaintiff’s ignorance was pleaded in the complaint.

The plaintiff has three years from the filing of the complaint to identify and serve the former “Doe” defendant.

When considering an amendment to a California pleading after the statute of limitations has run, remember that there are three possible scenarios in which the amendment will relate back to the date of the original pleading: (1) the assertion of a new claim arising out of the same conduct, transaction, or occurrence as the original pleading, (2) correcting a misnomer, and (3) substituting the true name of a “Doe” defendant.

ca-fro-civpro:remand.html:
removal is filed.

Issues Triggered by a Notice of Removal

Procedure

  • Was the notice of removal timely filed within 30 days after receipt or service of the initial pleading?
  • Do all of the defendants consent to removal?
  • Does any defendant reside in the forum state (for diversity cases)?

Subject matter jurisdiction

  • Would the federal court have federal question or diversity jurisdiction (diversity is more common)?

Applicable law

  • Do the facts present competing laws that trigger a choice-of-law or Erie doctrine analysis?

Venue

  • Is venue proper (i.e., in the federal district where the state court sits)?

ca-fro-civpro:removal-jurisdiction.html:
A defendant may generally remove a case from a state court to a federal district court with subject matter jurisdiction. Removal must be to the federal district court for the district and division in which the state court action is pending. Removal to the wrong district court is subject to a motion to remand the case back to the state court (or transfer it to the proper federal court).

Exam tip

“Was the motion to remand properly granted?” is one way of testing subject matter jurisdiction on the bar exam.

ca-fro-civpro:renewed-motion-for-judgmen.t: Renewed Motion for Judgment as a Matter of Law
crumb: JUDGMENT AS A MATTER OF LAW
ca-fro-civpro:renewed-motion-for-judgmen.html:
If the court does not grant JMOL, then the movant can renew its motion for JMOL no later than 28 days after the entry of judgment or the jury is discharged (if the issue was not decided by a verdict). A renewed motion for JMOL can be granted only on the grounds that were raised in the JMOL. The court may (1) allow judgment on the verdict, (2) order a new trial, or (3) direct the entry of judgment as a matter of law.

CA distinction

California uses the same standards as the federal rules, but there is no requirement that a party move for a JMOL or directed verdict before moving for a renewed JMOL.

ca-fro-civpro:right-to-jury-trial.html:
An action at law (e.g., for damages) is generally tried on demand to a jury, while an action in equity (e.g., injunction, specific performance, admiralty) is not. When legal and equitable issues overlap in one lawsuit, the court will try the legal issues first.

CA distinction

The right to a jury trial exists for all actions that are legal in nature.

For state-law claims in diversity actions, federal law will determine whether there is a right to a jury trial. And for all civil cases in federal court, there is a right to a jury trial if the amount in controversy exceeds $20. Additionally, a party must serve a demand for a jury trial within 14 days after service of the last pleading directed to the issue that is to be tried by jury.

ca-fro-civpro:rule-12-b-6-motion-to-dism.html: A claim will be dismissed if it fails to assert a legal theory of recovery or allege facts sufficient to support a cognizable claim. In deciding whether to grant a motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim, a court treats well-pleaded facts as true, resolves all doubts and inferences in the plaintiff’s favor, and views the complaint in the light most favorable to the plaintiff.

ca-fro-civpro:rule-12-b-defenses.html:
Lack of subject matter jurisdiction can be raised at any time. This defense is never waived.

Failure to state a claim upon which relief can be granted and failure to join necessary or indispensable parties can be raised in any pleading, in a motion for judgment on the pleadings, or at trial.

Lack of personal jurisdiction, improper venue, and insufficient process or service of process must be raised in a pre-answer motion or answer or within the time to amend an answer as of right—otherwise these defenses are waived. Note that, under the omnibus motion rule, a pre-answer motion that raises some other Rule 12(b) defense—but omits these defenses—waives the omitted defenses.

Complaint Pre-answer motion served Answer served/amended Trial ends filed

No subject matter jurisdic

ction

Failure to state a claim /

Failure to join a required party

No personal jurisdiction / Insufficient process or se

/ Improper venue / ervice of process

If no pre-answer motion was served

CA distinction

A defendant who objects to the court’s exercise of personal jurisdiction must do so at the earliest opportunity, usually by a motion to quash service of summons. The motion should be filed before filing an answer; otherwise, the defendant will be deemed to have made a general appearance.

A defendant may object to the allegations in a complaint by filing (1) an answer or (2) either a general or special demurrer to the entire complaint or any of its causes of action. A demurrer is similar to a Rule 12(b) motion.

CA distinction

A defendant may object to the allegations in a complaint by filing (1) an answer or (2) either a general or special demurrer to the entire complaint or any of its causes of action. A demurrer is similar to a Rule 12(b) motion.

Grounds for general demurrer:

  • The pleading fails to state facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action
  • The court lacks subject matter jurisdiction

Grounds for special demurrer:

  • Lack of legal capacity
  • Existence of another pending action
  • Defect in joinder or misjoinder of parties
  • Uncertain pleading
  • Failure to plead whether a contract is written or oral
  • Failure to file certain certificates

ca-fro-civpro:rule-for-amendments.html:
A party may amend a pleading once as of right no later than 21 days after serving it if the pleading is one to which no responsive pleading is required. If a responsive pleading is required, then a party may amend within 21 days after being served with an answer or Rule 12(b) motion. Otherwise, a party may amend a pleading during and after trial if it conforms to the evidence and the opposing party has an opportunity to prepare.

A court should freely give leave to amend a pleading when justice so requires and the opposing party will not suffer undue prejudice.

CA distinction

A party may amend a pleading once as a matter of right at any time:

  • Before an answer, demurrer, or motion to strike is filed or
  • After a demurrer or motion to strike is filed but before the demurrer or motion to strike is heard if the amended pleading is filed and served no later than the date for filing an opposition to the demurrer or motion to strike.

A party may amend the pleading after the date for filing an opposition to the demurrer or motion to strike upon stipulation by the parties. And a court may grant leave to amend after sustaining a demurrer or granting a motion to strike. Otherwise, a party may amend a pleading only by leave of court or with the written consent of the adverse party.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ PRETRIAL PROCEDURE AND DISCOVERY
ca-fro-civpro:sanctions.html:
The court may immediately impose sanctions for a party’s failure to attend their own deposition, respond to interrogatories, or respond to a request for production or inspection. In addition, if a party fails to obey a court order regarding discovery, the court may impose sanctions. Any sanctions are subject to the abuse- of-discretion standard on appeal.

CA distinction

The California rules are similar to the federal rules, except that:

  • Initial misuse of the discovery process will result in a monetary sanction unless the party acted with substantial justification or the sanction would be unjust and
  • Unless they acted with substantial justification or the sanction would be unjust, an additional $1,000 sanction will be imposed against any party, person, or attorney who:
    • Did not respond in good faith to a request for production or an inspection demand
    • Failed to produce requested documents until seven days before the court was scheduled to hear a motion to compel production or
    • Failed to confer in a reasonable and good-faith attempt to resolve any dispute concerning the request.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ PRETRIAL PROCEDURE AND DISCOVERY
ca-fro-civpro:scope.html:
Discovery is generally permitted with regard to any nonprivileged matter that is (1) relevant to any party’s claim or defense and (2) proportional to the needs of the case. The information sought need only be relevant—not admissible—to be discoverable.

Privileged information is not discoverable. Privilege is determined under federal common law for federal question claims and state law for diversity jurisdiction or supplemental jurisdiction claims.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ MULTIPLE PARTIES AND CLAIMS
ca-fro-civpro:settlement-dismissal-or-ju.html:
The claims, issues, or defenses of the certified class may be settled or dismissed only with the approval of the court. The court must direct notice to all class members who would be bound by a proposed settlement or dismissal.

A valid judgment generally binds all members of the class except those who opted out.

CA distinction

A class action can be brought when (1) the question is one of common/general interest, of many persons, or the parties are numerous and (2) it is impracticable to bring them all before the court. Class action certification requires an ascertainable class and a well-defined community of interest. When considering the community of interest, the court must address whether:

  • Common questions of law/fact predominate
  • The class action will benefit the parties and the court and
  • The named class representative(s) will adequately represent the class.

ca-fro-civpro:special-appearance.html:
Federal

California

Special appearances have been abolished.

A defendant in California may challenge personal jurisdiction in a special appearance without submitting to the jurisdiction of the court. However, a defendant who objects to personal jurisdiction for the first time in an answer is deemed to have made a general appearance and may not then object to personal jurisdiction.

ca-fro-civpro:special-pleading.html: Claims alleging fraud or mistake and claims for special damages must be pleaded with particularity.

ca-fro-civpro:special-rules-for-service-.t: Special Rules for Service of Process
ca-fro-civpro:special-rules-for-service-.html:
• Infant: Service must be made on the infant and the infant’s guardian.
  • Adjudicated incompetent: Service must be made on the person deemed incompetent and the person’s guardian.
  • Partnership: Service can be made on a general partner, an attorney in fact, or an authorized agent.
  • Corporation: Service must be effectuated by (1) delivering process to a corporate officer, director, managing agent, or agent appointed for receiving service of process or (2) following state law.
  • Nonresident motorist: For claims arising out of in-state accidents, service can be made on a state official who forwards a copy to the out-of-state motorist.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ MULTIPLE PARTIES AND CLAIMS
ca-fro-civpro:subject-matter-jurisdictio.html: For diversity jurisdiction, the named class representatives must be diverse from the adverse party, and at least one class member must satisfy the amount-in-controversy requirement.

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ CHOICE OF LAW: THE ERIE DOCTRINE
ca-fro-civpro:substance-vs-procedure.html: It can sometimes be difficult to determine whether an issue involves substance or procedure. The general approach is to consider whether there is a conflict between state and federal law. If there is no conflict, then the court can apply both laws harmoniously. If there is a conflict, then the court will consider whether a valid federal law—i.e., a federal statute, rule, or constitutional provision—addresses the issue.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ PRETRIAL PROCEDURE AND DISCOVERY
ca-fro-civpro:summary-judgment.html:
Motion for Summary Judgment

Federal

California

Time to File

  • The motion may be filed until 30 days after the close of discovery or a time set by a local rule or court

The motion can be filed 60 days after the nonmovant’s general appearance, and the movant must serve all papers 81 days before the hearing date

Burden of Proof

  • The movant must establish the standard for granting through pleadings, affidavits, declarations, discovery, or other materials containing admissible facts
  • If this initial burden is met, the burden then shifts to the nonmovant to:
    • Show specific disputed facts through affidavits, declarations, discovery, or other materials containing admissible facts (cannot rely on pleadings) or
    • Request postponement for additional discovery and support this request with affidavits or declarations

The movant can also present evidence negating the nonmovant’s cause of action or defense

Standard for Granting

  • No genuine dispute as to any material fact, and the movant is entitled to judgment as a matter of law
  • The evidence must be viewed in the light most favorable to the nonmovant, and all doubts must be resolved in the nonmovant’s favor

ca-fro-civpro:supplemental-jurisdiction.html: A federal court with original subject matter jurisdiction over a claim may exercise supplemental jurisdiction over additional claims even though the court would not independently have subject matter jurisdiction over those claims. However, the additional claims must arise out of a “common nucleus of operative fact,” such that all claims should be tried in a single judicial proceeding.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ MULTIPLE PARTIES AND CLAIMS
ca-fro-civpro:third-party-claims-i-e-imp.html:
Impleaded claims are made by a defending party (i.e., third-party plaintiff) against a nonparty (i.e., third-party defendant) for all or part of the third-party plaintiff’s potential liability on an original claim.

Impleader can be asserted any time after the complaint is filed. However, a third-party plaintiff must obtain court permission to file more than 14 days after service of the original answer.

If the original claim is based only on diversity jurisdiction, then claims by a plaintiff against a third-party defendant must meet the requirements for diversity or federal question jurisdiction on their own.

In personam personal jurisdiction must be established over the third parties.

CA distinction

If a cause of action in a cross-complaint adds a new party, then the cause of action must:

  • Arise out of the same transaction/occurrence as original the plaintiff’s cause of action or
  • Allege a claim in the plaintiff’s action against a third-party plaintiff (who can then join new third- party defendants or causes of actions against them).

ca-fro-civpro:well-pleaded-complaint.html: Federal question jurisdiction exists only when the federal issue is presented on the face of the plaintiff’s complaint. Jurisdiction must be determined based on whether the plaintiff has pleaded the necessary elements of the plaintiff’s cause of action—not on defenses, answers, or counterclaims raised by the defendant.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ PRETRIAL PROCEDURE AND DISCOVERY
ca-fro-civpro:work-product-rule.html:
A party may not discover documents and tangible things prepared in anticipation of litigation or for trial unless the party shows that it:

  • Has a substantial need for the materials to prepare its case and
  • Cannot obtain a substantial equivalent by other means without undue hardship.

If a court orders discovery of work product, then the court must protect against the disclosure of mental impressions, conclusions, opinions, or legal theories of a party’s attorney or other representative.

CA distinction

An attorney’s impressions, conclusions, opinions, and legal research are absolutely privileged and never subject to discovery.

crumb: CHAPTER III ▪ AGREEMENTS AFFECTING PROPERTY DIVISION
ca-fro-commprop:agreements-during-marria.html:
A transmutation refers to changing the nature of property (e.g., changing separate property into community property). Transmutations made on or after January 1, 1985, must be in writing with an express declaration by the adversely affected spouse that the characterization or ownership of the property is being changed. Because of the “express declaration” requirement, extrinsic evidence and statements in wills are not considered.

These transmutation requirements are absolute and bar the ordinary exceptions to the Statute of Frauds. However, certain tangible gifts are excluded from the writing requirement so long as they are between spouses, not substantial in value, and used solely or principally by the receiving spouse.

Exam tip

The relevant time is the date of the transmutation—not the date the property was acquired. That is because the legislature intended that the transmutation rules apply to all property owned by spouses, regardless of when the property was acquired.

ca-fro-commprop:apportioning-community-p.t: Apportioning Community Property and Separate Property Interests
crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ JOINT DEEDS AND TITLES
PROPERTY PURCHASED WITH COMMUNITY-PROPERTY AND SEPARATE-PROPERTY FUNDS
ca-fro-commprop:apportioning-community-p.html:
When separate property is used as a down payment or contribution to acquire community or quasi- community property, the presumption of community property controls. This presumption can be rebutted by a writing indicating that the separate-property interest in the property is intact.

  • If this presumption is rebutted, then under the Aufmuth formula, the separate property will acquire a pro-rata interest in the property in the proportion that the down payment bears to the purchase price.
  • If this presumption is not rebutted, then the separate property is limited to a right of reimbursement after tracing any payments to a separate-property source.

Anti-Lucas legislation does not apply to community-property contributions for separate real property. The community property will acquire a pro-rata interest in the separate real property in the proportion that the community-property payments bear to the total purchase price, including any appreciation in value during the time of the community-property payments.

Payments toward interest, taxes, and insurance are not “contributions” in the above formulas because they do not contribute to the equitable interest.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ JOINT DEEDS AND TITLES
PROPERTY PURCHASED WITH COMMUNITY-PROPERTY AND SEPARATE-PROPERTY FUNDS
ca-fro-commprop:as-of-1984-anti-lucas-le.html:
The community-property presumption was expanded to include all jointly held property, rebuttable by an express statement in the deed or a collateral writing that the property is separate property. The spouse making the separate-property contribution has a right to reimbursement, without interest.

When no vested property rights are at risk (e.g., a survivorship interest in joint tenancy), the Anti-Lucas statutes may be retroactively applied.

Exam tip

Remember that the Anti-Lucas legislation applies only to divorce or legal separation—not death.

ca-fro-commprop:chapter-i-definitions.html:
Exam tip

Community property is frequently tested on the California Bar Exam, usually in its own essay question. However, it has also been tested alongside other subjects (e.g., professional responsibility, trusts).

Community-property assets and debts form the marital community estate, which must be divided equally at divorce unless an enforceable property settlement agreement provides otherwise.

Basic Definitions

Marital Community

The marital economic community begins at marriage and ends at divorce, the death of one spouse, or permanent separation (i.e., a complete and final break in the marital relationship).

Community Property

Presumed to include all property acquired (and debts incurred) by a spouse during marriage while domiciled in California, regardless of which spouse takes title to the property.

Separate Property

Property acquired before marriage or after permanent separation, as well as property acquired at any time by gift or inheritance.

Quasi-Community Property

Property acquired by a spouse while living in a non-community-property state that would be characterized as community property if the couple had been living in California when the property was acquired.

This classification is only triggered by divorce or the death of the titled spouse.

Quasi-Marital Property

Property acquired by putative spouses during their putative marriage.

Transmutation

Changing the classification of property by agreement or transfer.

Tracing

The process of determining the source of funds used to acquire an asset to establish its classification as community property or separate property.

Tracing an asset acquired during a marriage back to a separate-property source can be used to rebut the presumption that the asset is community property. A change in the form of an asset does not change the asset’s characterization.

ca-fro-commprop:chapter-iii-agreements-a.t: CHAPTER III ▪ AGREEMENTS AFFECTING PROPERTY DIVISION
ca-fro-commprop:chapter-iii-agreements-a.html:
Spouses may remove themselves from California’s community-property system, in whole or in part, by entering into a contract before or during marriage that specifies their own property distribution system.

Exam tip

When evaluating a spousal agreement:

  • First, determine whether it was made before the marriage (i.e., premarital or antenuptial) or during the marriage (e.g., to characterize community or separate property).
  • Next, analyze the validity of the agreement (e.g., contract defenses) before addressing how the agreement may impact each asset involved in the problem.

If the facts are insufficient to establish whether the agreement was valid, then argue both sides and state a conclusion, then analyze the allocation of each asset under a valid agreement and an invalid agreement.

ca-fro-commprop:chapter-ix-division-of-p.t: CHAPTER IX ▪ DIVISION OF PROPERTY AT DEATH
ca-fro-commprop:chapter-ix-division-of-p.html:
At death, the character of property is presumptively as expressed in the title, under the special title presumption (e.g., a joint tenancy property is presumed to be a joint tenancy, and community property is presumed to be community property).

Exam tip

Compare this rule with the presumption that applies to a dissolution of marriage or legal separation— property acquired during the marriage in joint form is presumed to be community property.

ca-fro-commprop:chapter-vi-management-an.t: CHAPTER VI ▪ MANAGEMENT AND CONTROL OF COMMUNITY PROPERTY
ca-fro-commprop:chapter-vi-management-an.html:
Exam tip

When you discuss equal rights of management in an essay, always discuss fiduciary duties under a separate heading. Remember to mention that the innocent spouse can ask for a greater share of community property due to the other spouse’s breach.

crumb: CHARACTERIZATION OF SPECIAL TYPES OF PROPERTY
ca-fro-commprop:commingled-bank-accounts.html:
Exam tip

“Commingling” occurs when the separate property of one spouse is mixed or combined with community property or the separate property of the other spouse (e.g., joint checking account)

The burden of proof is on the separate-property proponent to show that separate-property funds were used to purchase assets. This is done by tracing the funds back to a separate-property source using one of the following methods:

  • Direct tracing – The proponent must prove that there were sufficient separate-property funds available at the time of purchase and that the proponent intended to use them.
  • Indirect tracing (exhaustion) – The proponent must prove that community-property funds in the account were already exhausted by the payment of family expenses (e.g., food, clothing, recreation, housing) at the time of purchase. Certain presumptions apply to family expenses paid from a commingled account:
    • Community-property funds are used to pay family expenses
    • Separate-property funds are used only when community-property funds are exhausted and
    • Any separate-property funds used for family expenses are a gift to the community.

crumb: CHARACTERIZATION OF SPECIAL TYPES OF PROPERTY
ca-fro-commprop:credit-purchases-and-loa.html: Property acquired on credit during the marriage is presumed to be community property unless the lender “primarily relied” on the purchaser’s separate property in extending credit. The proceeds of a loan made on a spouse’s personal credit are also community property.

crumb: CHAPTER VIII ▪ DIVISION OF PROPERTY AT DIVORCE
ca-fro-commprop:division-of-liabilities.html: At divorce, debts incurred before marriage are assigned to the spouse who incurred the debt. Debts incurred during marriage must be divided equally or assigned to one of the spouses. And debts incurred during marriage but after separation can be characterized as separate property if the debt was not incurred for common necessities or for the benefit of the community.

crumb: CHARACTERIZATION OF SPECIAL TYPES OF PROPERTY
ca-fro-commprop:educational-degrees-and-.html:
Educational degrees and professional licenses are not community property, but the community estate may be entitled to reimbursement for certain costs. These costs are normally limited to direct educational or training expenses (not living expenses), including interest. The community estate will be reimbursed if:

  • Community-property or quasi-community-property funds were used to pay the education or training costs
  • The educated spouse’s earning capacity was substantially improved and
  • The spouses did not contractually waive the right to reimbursement in writing.

Note that the right to reimbursement is in lieu of any right to a percentage of the enhanced earning ability of the spouse who earned the degree or license.

The educated spouse can raise the following defenses to the right to reimbursement:

  • The community substantially benefited from the education or training
    • Contributions made more than 10 years ago = Rebuttably presumed that the community has substantially benefited
    • Contributions made less than 10 years ago = Rebuttably presumed that the community has not substantially benefited
  • The other spouse also received an education or training paid for with community-property funds during the marriage or
  • The education or training reduces the need for spousal support.

Outstanding educational loans are still assigned to the spouse who incurred them, but the community is entitled to reimbursement for contributions to the repayment of the loans.

crumb: CHAPTER VIII ▪ DIVISION OF PROPERTY AT DIVORCE
ca-fro-commprop:equal-division-rule.html:
California requires an equal division of community and quasi-community property at divorce, with the following exceptions:

  • Deliberate misappropriation of community property by one spouse to the exclusion of the interest of the other spouse
  • Personal injury damages, which are generally assigned to the injured spouse, but at least 1/2 must be assigned to the injured spouse
  • If the community debts exceed the assets, the excess debt may be assigned in an equitable manner
  • Debts for education loans may be assigned to the spouse who received the education
  • The court may assign sole or joint ownership of a community-property pet animal
  • Liability for a tort judgment may be assigned first to the tortfeasor’s separate property if she was not acting for the benefit of the community
  • A civil judgment to pay damages for domestic violence against one spouse may be enforced against the abusive spouse’s community-property share

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ MANAGEMENT AND CONTROL OF COMMUNITY PROPERTY
ca-fro-commprop:fiduciary-duty.html:
The highest duty of good faith and fair dealing is imposed on each spouse in the management and control of community property. The managing spouse also owes three specific fiduciary duties: the duties to disclose, to account, and to obtain consent.

Establishing that the managing spouse breached a fiduciary duty requires proof of deliberate misappropriation, gross negligence, or reckless conduct. Remedies for a breach that impairs the non-managing spouse’s interest in the community estate include (1) an accounting, (2) adding a name onto a title, (3) a greater share of the community property, and (4) attorney’s fees and costs. The non- managing spouse must bring the action within three years from the date that the spouse knew of the breach, or otherwise upon legal separation, divorce, or a spouse’s death.

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ PARTIES SUBJECT TO COMMUNITY PROPERTY
ca-fro-commprop:general-requirements.html:
A valid marriage requires the consent and legal capacity of two persons to enter into a marriage contract. The age of consent to marry is 18 years. Anyone younger must obtain a court order and written consent of a parent or guardian. The consent must be followed by formal legal procedures.

California abolished common-law marriage, so a valid marriage cannot be created in California solely by the parties’ consent or mere cohabitation. However, California does recognize common-law marriages validly created in other states.

Exam tip

Keep in mind that persons may contract a marriage in any jurisdiction, even if they are not domiciled in that jurisdiction. Therefore, a California resident may be able to prove that he entered into a common-law marriage while temporarily residing in another jurisdiction.

ca-fro-commprop:generally.html: A “debt” includes liability in contract or tort and other obligations before or during marriage and is considered “incurred” at the time a contract is made, a tort occurs, or an obligation arises.

crumb: CHAPTER IX ▪ DIVISION OF PROPERTY AT DEATH
ca-fro-commprop:intestate.html:
The surviving spouse of an intestate decedent is entitled to the decedent’s 1/2 of community property and 1/2 of quasi-community property. When combined with the surviving spouse’s own 1/2, this means that the surviving spouse will receive ALL of the community and quasi-community property.

In contrast, the distribution of the decedent’s separate property depends on who survives the decedent.

Intestate Distribution of the Decedent Spouse’s Separate Property

Who Survived the Decedent?

Spouse’s Share of Separate Property

Spouse only

Spouse takes ALL

Spouse + One lineal descendant, parent, or parent’s issue

Spouse takes 1/2

Spouse + Two or more lineal descendants

Spouse takes 1/3

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ JOINT DEEDS AND TITLES
ca-fro-commprop:joint-titles.html:
Under the special title presumption, ownership of property is determined by the title of the property. Therefore, if the title indicates that the spouses hold property as joint tenants, each spouse holds an undivided half separate-property interest in the property for the duration of the marriage.

However, as of 1987, all jointly titled property acquired during marriage is presumed to be community property upon divorce. This presumption can be rebutted by an agreement stating that the property is not held jointly.

ca-fro-commprop:liability-of-a-spouse-s-.t: Liability of a Spouse’s Separate Property
ca-fro-commprop:liability-of-a-spouse-s-.html: A spouse’s separate property is liable for debts that the spouse incurred before or during marriage but is not liable for the other spouse’s debts.

ca-fro-commprop:liability-of-community-e.html:
Until the date of separation, the community is liable for debts incurred by either spouse before or during marriage—including preexisting child and spousal support obligations. Upon divorce, the community has a right to reimbursement.

To protect community-property earnings from liability for pre-marriage debts, the non-debtor spouse must deposit them into a separate bank account from which the debtor spouse has no right of withdrawal.

ca-fro-commprop:necessaries-of-life.html:
Before separation, a married person is personally liable for the “necessaries of life,” meaning living expenses consistent with the spouses’ station in life (e.g., food, clothing, shelter, medical expenses).

After separation, a married person is personally liable only for the “common necessaries of life,” meaning the expenses required to sustain life. If the debtor spouse had separate- or community-property funds at the time the debt was paid, then the non-debtor spouse can be reimbursed for any separate- property funds used.

crumb: CHARACTERIZATION OF SPECIAL TYPES OF PROPERTY
ca-fro-commprop:personal-injury-recovery.html:
The characterization of tort recovery upon divorce depends on when the third-party tortfeasor injured the spouse.

  • Injury before marriage – Tort recovery is separate property. However, the injured spouse must reimburse the community and the other spouse’s separate property for any injury-related expenses.
  • Injury during marriage – Tort recovery is community property, but the recovery will be awarded to the injured spouse upon divorce if it has not been spent or commingled with community-property funds. Some of the recovery (no more than one-half) may be awarded to the non-injured spouse in the interest of justice.

In contrast, personal injury recovery is characterized as community property at the death of either spouse.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ MANAGEMENT AND CONTROL OF COMMUNITY PROPERTY
ca-fro-commprop:personal-property.html:
Each spouse has equal management and control over major personal property transactions. “Equal” means that both spouses must participate in decisions regarding such transactions. However, this rule is subject to the following exceptions:

  • Bank accounts: Access to bank accounts in one spouse’s name is limited to that spouse.
  • Gifts to third parties: Gifts require the other spouse’s written consent. If consent is not given, the non-consenting spouse can ratify the gift or revoke it and sue to recover the property. After the donor spouse’s death, the non-consenting spouse can ratify the gift or void up to half of its value.
  • Household goods: A spouse is not allowed to sell, convey, or encumber the family dwelling, household furniture/furnishings, or the clothing of the other spouse or minor children without the other spouse’s written consent.
  • Community business: The managing spouse has primary management and control, but the managing spouse must give the other spouse prior written notice of major actions. The non-managing spouse can seek a remedy if the managing spouse failed to do so and detrimentally impacted the non-managing spouse’s community-property interest.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ JOINT DEEDS AND TITLES
PROPERTY PURCHASED WITH COMMUNITY-PROPERTY AND SEPARATE-PROPERTY FUNDS
ca-fro-commprop:pre-1984-lucas-ruling.html: Single-family residences acquired in joint tenancy with community-property and separate-property funds was presumptively community property, and the separate-property funds were deemed to be a gift to the community. This presumption could be rebutted by clear and convincing evidence.

crumb: CHAPTER III ▪ AGREEMENTS AFFECTING PROPERTY DIVISION
ca-fro-commprop:premarital-agreements.html:
Premarital agreements fall within the Statute of Frauds, so they:

  • Must be in writing
  • Must be signed by both parties and
  • Are enforceable without consideration.

However, an oral agreement may be enforceable if (1) the promise was fully executed by the promisor or (2) the promisee has detrimentally relied on the oral agreement.

Premarital agreements can alter property and ownership rights as well as spousal support. However, premarital agreements cannot include child support and should not promote divorce.

Defenses to Enforceability

Defense

Requirements

Agreement was not voluntarily executed

The proponent of the agreement must show that:

  • The opponent was represented by independent legal counsel at the time of signing or, after being advised to seek such counsel at least seven days before signing, expressly waived counsel
  • The opponent had at least seven days to sign
  • The opponent (if unrepresented by counsel) was fully informed of the terms/ effect of the agreement and
  • The agreement was not executed under fraud, duress, or undue influence.

Agreement was unconscionable when executed

The opponent of the agreement must show that, at the time the agreement was executed, the opponent:

  • Was not provided a fair, reasonable, and full disclosure of the other party’s property or financial obligations
  • Did not voluntarily and expressly waive disclosure in writing and
  • Did not have (or reasonably could not have had) adequate knowledge of the other party’s property or financial obligations.

Additionally, a spousal support provision in a premarital agreement will not be enforceable (1) against a party who was not represented by independent counsel when the agreement was signed or (2) if the provision is unconscionable at the time enforcement is sought.

ca-fro-commprop:presumptions.html:
There is a general presumption that all property acquired during marriage is community property. However, the general community-property presumption can be rebutted in various ways, including:

  • Written title evidencing that a gift was intended
  • Evidence that the property was acquired by gift, bequest, devise, or descent or after permanent separation
  • Tracing the funds used to purchase the asset to a separate-property source or
  • An agreement designating property as separate property.

The general separate-property presumption applies to anything acquired before marriage, by inheritance during marriage, or after death/divorce or legal separation.

Once a presumption applies, the party opposing the presumption has the burden to rebut the presumption. The general presumption must be rebutted by a preponderance of the evidence, while special presumptions must be rebutted by clear and convincing evidence.

(general presumption) (special presumptions)

Special Presumptions

Presumption

Applicability

Effect

Special Title Presumption

Only at death

Presumes that the form of ownership on the title represents the form of the spouses’ ownership interests

Special Community- Property Presumption

Property acquired during marriage in joint form

Presumed to be community property unless rebutted by evidence of contrary intent (after 1984, the evidence must be in writing)

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ PARTIES SUBJECT TO COMMUNITY PROPERTY
ca-fro-commprop:putative-spouse-doctrine.html:
The putative spouse doctrine seeks to protect the “innocent” party in an invalid marriage from losing community-property rights. It also extends to the innocent party in an invalid domestic partnership.

A putative spouse is one who subjectively believed in good faith, based on objectively reasonable grounds, that an invalid marriage was valid. In determining good faith, the trial court must consider the totality of the circumstances.

All property that would have been considered community or quasi-community property had the marriage been valid is considered quasi-marital property in a putative marriage (but this property right stops accruing once the putative spouse discovers that the marriage is invalid). Therefore, the putative spouse has a 1/2 interest in all quasi-marital property at annulment. And though still an undecided issue, it seems that the non-good-faith spouse may obtain an ownership interest in the quasi-marital property of the putative spouse.

A putative spouse also has the same rights as a surviving spouse to the decedent’s separate property. If the decedent had both a putative spouse and a legal spouse, then the decedent’s estate is split equally between them.

When both parties know that the marriage is invalid, one party can use the doctrine of estoppel against the other party if both are still engaged in the benefits of cohabitation.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ MANAGEMENT AND CONTROL OF COMMUNITY PROPERTY
ca-fro-commprop:real-property.html:
Equal management and control also applies to community real property. However, both spouses must join in (1) executing an instrument leasing for longer than one year or (2) selling, conveying, or otherwise encumbering community real property.

When the title to community real property is held in the names of both spouses and the title indicates that they are married, both spouses must participate in any transaction. But when the community real property is held in only one spouse’s name, the transaction is presumed to be valid if the purchaser, in good faith, did not know that the seller was married.

ca-fro-commprop:reimbursement-of-debt.html:
Unless waived, a right of reimbursement exists regarding payments for the necessities of life, preexisting child or spousal support, and tort obligations. Either spouse can exercise this right:

  • In divorce proceedings involving the division of community or quasi-community property
  • In proceedings upon the death of a spouse or
  • If earlier, within three years from the date that the spouse has knowledge of the debt satisfaction.

crumb: CHARACTERIZATION OF SPECIAL TYPES OF PROPERTY
ca-fro-commprop:separate-property-busine.html:
Income from a separate-property business is generally characterized as separate property. But when the value of a separate-property business has grown during the marriage, then a court has discretion to apply the most appropriate and equitable approach to calculate the community-property and separate- property shares. The most common approaches are the Pereira approach and the Van Camp approach.

SPB’s total fair market value (FMV)

If the increase in value is primarily due to community-property labor (i.e., spousal labor and skills), then apply the Pereira approach:

If the increase in value is primarily due to the character of the separate property itself (rather than the labor of the spouse), then apply the Van Camp approach. This approach can be broken into two steps:

Step 1: Apply the Van Camp formula to calculate the value of the community portion of the business.

no community share remains.

Step 2: Determine whether the community has been compensated for its share of the value of the business through a salary or other distributions.
