If the community estate has already received the full value calculated in Step 1 (e.g., by a high salary or large bonuses), then the entire business is treated as separate property, and the analysis ends with no judgment against the value of the business.

Value added during marriage

If the community estate has not received the full value calculated in step 1, then the court may award equitable distribution to the community estate (e.g., the difference between the reasonable value of the services provided and the amount actually received by the community estate).

Value added during marriage

Exam tip

Be sure to address both the Pereira and Van Camp formulas in an essay, but focus on the one that will most likely be applied. Here is an easy way to remember which formula to apply:

  • Pereira – Think “P” for “person,” or “personal” services and effort
  • Van Camp – Think “V” for inherent “value,” or “valuable” business or asset

To earn full credit, you must use the numerical values provided in the fact pattern to explain how the community-property and separate-property shares will be calculated. But as long as you explain the formulas and show your work, you will not lose points for mathematical errors in calculating the final dollar values of the shares.

crumb: CHARACTERIZATION OF SPECIAL TYPES OF PROPERTY
ca-fro-commprop:stock-options.html:
Stock options that are granted and vest during the marriage are community property. Stock options that are granted during the marriage but vest after separation are part community property and part separate property. The community-property share of such options is determined by applying one of the following formulas, depending on why the options were granted:

  • Hug formula – Calculate the amount of time between the date of hire (DOH) and the date of separation (DOS). That number will be divided by the amount of time between the DOH and the date the options vest (DOV). The result, multiplied by the number of shares, is the community-property share of the stock options.
  • Nelson formula – Calculate the amount of time between the date of the grant (DOG) and the date of separation (DOS). That number will be divided by the amount of time between the DOG and the DOV. The result, multiplied by the number of shares, is the community-property share of the stock options.
Determining Community-Property Share of Stock Options

Formula

Why Option Was Granted

Calculation

Hug formula

Compensate an employee for past service (or to attract a new employee)

CP = (DOH - DOS) / (DOH - DOV) × # of shares

SP = # of shares − CP

Nelson formula

Incentivize continued employment

CP = (DOG - DOS) / (DOG - DOV) × # of shares

SP = # of shares − CP

CP = Community Property; DOH = Date of Hire; DOS = Date of Separation; DOV = Date Options Vest; SP = Separate Property; DOG = Date of Grant.

ca-fro-commprop:summary-tips-for-communi.html:
Community-property essays often involve (1) a timeline of events and transactions related to a marriage and (2) a call of the question asking you to discuss the parties’ rights and liabilities regarding a list of items, assets, or debts. When drafting these answers, do the following:

  • State the basic definitions of community property and separate property (and quasi-community property, if relevant):
    • Property, earnings, or debt acquired during marriage is presumed to be community property.
    • Property acquired before marriage or after permanent separation, as well as property acquired at any time by gift or inheritance, is separate property.
    • Property acquired by a married couple while living in a non-community-property state that would be characterized as community property if the couple had been living in California when it was acquired is, upon death or divorce, quasi-community property.
  • Identify any issues that affect all assets (e.g., invalid marriage, premarital agreement, property settlement agreement, transmutation).
  • Address each item or asset as community property, separate property, or quasi-community property under a separate heading. Consider the following:
    • When, where, and how was the asset acquired?
    • If the asset was purchased, what funds (separate or community) were used?
    • What presumption applies, and will the presumption be rebutted?
    • What actions took place after the asset was acquired?
    • Do any special rules apply (e.g., due dates, titles, death)?

crumb: CHAPTER IX ▪ DIVISION OF PROPERTY AT DEATH
ca-fro-commprop:testate.html:
The testator can bequeath only her own separate property and 1/2 of the community property.

When a testator includes an “election clause” in a will or attempts to bequeath the surviving spouse’s 1/2 interest in the community property, the surviving spouse is required to choose between his community-property ownership rights and his rights under the will. Otherwise, the surviving spouse could assert both rights.

ca-fro-commprop:tort-obligations.html:
When one spouse is the tortfeasor and causes injury to property or a person, the court determines the order of satisfaction for any tort obligation as follows:

  • If the tortfeasor spouse was acting for the benefit of the community (e.g., taking the couple’s child to school), then liability is first satisfied from the community estate and then from the tortfeasor’s separate property.
  • If the tortfeasor spouse was not acting for the benefit of the community, then liability is first satisfied from the tortfeasor’s separate property and then from the community estate.

If the order is not satisfied, then the non-tortfeasor spouse may seek reimbursement from the tortfeasor spouse.

Primary liability Secondary liability

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ PARTIES SUBJECT TO COMMUNITY PROPERTY
ca-fro-commprop:unmarried-cohabitants.html:
Distribution of property acquired during unmarried cohabitation is governed by contract principles—not community-property statutes. Express contracts between unmarried cohabitants will be enforced unless they are based on prostitution. If there is no express contract, then courts will examine the parties’ conduct for an implied contract or understanding.

Exam tip

Watch for couples who cohabitate before a valid marriage occurs. If unmarried cohabitants later marry, contract principles still apply to any conduct or property acquired before the marriage occurred.

crumb: CHAPTER V ▪ PRIVILEGES AND OTHER POLICY EXCLUSIONS
ca-fro-evidence:attorney-client-privileg.html:
A client and attorney cannot be forced to testify about their confidential communications with each other that were made for the purpose of obtaining or providing legal advice or representation. Note that the attorney need not actually give advice or agree to the representation for this privilege to exist. However, only the communication is privileged—not preexisting documents or underlying facts/evidence that the client has or knows.

The client holds the privilege, which exists until the client (or her guardian or successor in interest) waives it. The privilege can survive termination of the attorney-client relationship—and even the client’s death. But the client must make reasonable efforts to keep the communication confidential to avoid waiver, and the attorney must protect the privilege by refusing to talk about such confidential communications.

Exceptions to this privilege exist for any communications that are:

  • Made to enable the commission of what the client knew or should have known was a crime or fraud
  • Relevant to a dispute between an attorney and a client or former client
  • Relevant to a dispute between parties who claim through the same deceased client or
  • Between former co-clients who are now adverse to each other.
CA distinction

No privilege exists when (1) the communication relates to the future commission of what the client knew or should have known was a crime or fraud or (2) the lawyer reasonably believes that disclosure is necessary to prevent a criminal act that may result in death or substantial bodily harm to another.

Waiver of the attorney-client privilege can arise from:

  • Failure to assert the privilege in a timely manner
  • Voluntary disclosure of the information
  • Express waiver (e.g., by contract) or
  • The attorney’s failure to take precautions (i.e., careless conduct).
CA distinction

The privilege exists until it is waived or the client has died and her estate no longer exists. The client’s guardian/conservator can waive the privilege on the client’s behalf.

ca-fro-evidence:authentication.html: All tangible evidence must be authenticated with sufficient evidence to support a finding that the thing is what its proponent claims it to be. There are different methods for authenticating physical objects, documentary evidence, and oral statements.

ca-fro-evidence:character-evidence.html: Character evidence refers to generalized information about a person’s (typically, a defendant’s) behavior. Such evidence is generally inadmissible to prove that the person acted in conformity with that character (or trait) on the occasion at issue.

ca-fro-evidence:character-for-truthfulne.html:
Evidence of a witness’s truthful character cannot be used to bolster the witness’s credibility. It is only admissible after a direct attack on the witness’s truthful character through reputation or opinion testimony.

CA distinction

In criminal cases, the defendant can bolster his testimony for honesty/veracity without any impeachment attempt by the prosecution through reputation and opinion evidence as well as specific acts.

A specific act is generally not admissible to attack or support a witness’s character for truthfulness. However, on cross-examination, a witness may be asked about specific instances of conduct if they are probative of the (un)truthfulness of the witness or another witness about whose character the witness being cross-examined has testified.

CA distinction

Only in a criminal case may a witness be impeached with specific instances of dishonest conduct, and those instances may be proved by extrinsic evidence.

Although an arrest cannot be used to impeach a witness’s character for truthfulness, a criminal conviction can be used, subject to certain limitations.

ca-fro-evidence:civil-cases.html:
In a civil case, character evidence cannot be used to prove a person’s conduct. For example, evidence that the defendant is a reckless driver cannot be used to prove that the defendant drove recklessly on the day in question.

However, such evidence is admissible when character is an essential element of a claim or defense. This usually arises in claims involving defamation (character of the plaintiff), negligent hiring or entrustment (character of the person hired or entrusted), or child custody (character of the parent or guardian). Character evidence is also admissible for impeachment purposes and in civil cases involving allegations of sexual misconduct.

ca-fro-evidence:competence.html:
A person who has personal knowledge of the matter and gives an oath or affirmation to testify truthfully is competent to testify as a witness (with some exceptions).

A person who is unable to understand the requirement to tell the truth is incompetent to be a witness. But questions regarding a witness’s mental abilities—including the effect of drugs and alcohol on the witness’s ability to perceive, recall, or recount a matter and the witness’s degree of certainty about the matter—go to the witness’s credibility rather than competency to testify.

CA distinction

California applies an exception to the above rule regarding the effects of hypnosis on the witness’s ability to recall a matter:

  • In a civil action, a witness’s testimony is generally inadmissible if it relates to events for which the witness has undergone hypnosis to aid the witness’s recollection. But such testimony is admissible if it is recounting information revealed before the hypnosis took place.
  • In a criminal action, this restriction does not apply to the defendant.

crumb: CHAPTER V ▪ PRIVILEGES AND OTHER POLICY EXCLUSIONS
ca-fro-evidence:compromise-offers-and-ne.html:
Compromise offers and negotiations are generally inadmissible. However, they are admissible to prove bias or prejudice of a witness, negate a claim of undue delay, or prove obstruction of a criminal investigation or prosecution. They are also admissible in a subsequent criminal case if made during compromise negotiations with a governmental agency exercising its regulatory, investigative, or enforcement authority.

Settlement agreements are not admissible in a suit on the original claim to prove the validity of the claim. Nor may they be offered by third parties to prove or disprove the validity or amount of a disputed claim that the third person has asserted against the involved party.

ca-fro-evidence:criminal-cases.html:
The prosecution cannot introduce evidence of a defendant’s bad character to prove that the defendant has a propensity to commit crimes and is therefore likely to have committed the crime in question.

However, under the mercy rule, the defendant can introduce reputation or opinion testimony regarding his own good character as being inconsistent with the crime charged if that evidence is pertinent to the crime charged. But if the defendant “opens the door” by offering evidence of his good character, then the prosecution can rebut the defendant’s claims with evidence of the defendant’s bad character.

CA distinction

California’s rule is broader, allowing a criminal defendant to introduce evidence of a relevant character trait or the defendant’s character in general.

A defendant may also introduce reputation or opinion testimony regarding the victim’s character when it is relevant to the defense asserted (though evidence of a victim’s sexual behavior is generally inadmissible in cases involving sexual misconduct). But if the defendant does so, then the prosecution can rebut that evidence by offering evidence of the victim’s good character or the defendant’s bad character.

If defendant introduces: Prosecution may rebut with:

Additionally, any party may use character evidence for impeachment purposes (e.g., to show a witness’s character for untruthfulness).

CA distinction

A criminal defendant can attack the victim’s character with reputation or opinion testimony as well as specific acts.

Note that in California, a self-defense claim is not considered an attack on the victim’s character. Therefore, the prosecution cannot rebut such a claim with character evidence of the victim’s peaceful nature unless the defendant has introduced evidence of the victim’s violent nature.

ca-fro-evidence:criminal-cases-2.html:
In contrast, when character evidence is admissible in a criminal case, it may be proved by reputation or opinion testimony—but generally not by specific acts. However, specific acts are admissible when:

  • Character is an essential element of a crime or defense or
  • The acts are being used for a nonpropensity (or noncharacter) purpose, which can be remembered with the mnemonic “MIMIC.”
Noncharacter Purposes for Admitting a Specific Act

Motive

To show the purpose for committing the crime charged

Intent

To establish a guilty mind or negate good faith

Absence of Mistake

To negate mistake or accident and prove a deliberate act

Identity

To connect the defendant to the crime with a unique pattern of behavior or criminal signature

Common Plan or Scheme

To show preparation or planning

Other

To show knowledge of the crime, an opportunity to commit the crime, consciousness of guilt, etc.

The prosecution must provide a criminal defendant with reasonable notice of its intent to offer evidence of the defendant’s specific acts at trial and articulate the permitted purpose (i.e., MIMIC) for which the evidence will be offered. Such notice must be in writing before trial, unless the court excuses this requirement for good cause.

ca-fro-evidence:cross-examination.html:
When a character witness is being cross-examined, the court may allow a party to inquire into relevant specific acts committed by the person about whom the witness is testifying. But if the character witness denies the specific act, then extrinsic evidence is not admissible to prove the specific act. The party is stuck with that answer.

Exam tip

Extrinsic evidence is evidence from sources other than the witness’s own testimony (e.g., documents, another witness’s testimony).

ca-fro-evidence:declarant-s-availability.html: Other hearsay exceptions do not require that the declarant be unavailable because the circumstances under which the statements were made suggest that they are inherently trustworthy.

ca-fro-evidence:declarant-unavailable-as.html:
Some hearsay exceptions apply only if the declarant is unavailable as a witness. Unavailability arises when the declarant:

  • Is exempt from testifying on the grounds of privilege
  • Refuses to testify despite a court order to do so
  • Lacks memory of the subject matter of the statement
  • Is unable to testify due to death, infirmity, or physical or mental disability or
  • Is absent and cannot be subpoenaed or otherwise made to be present.

However, if the unavailability is due to the procurement or wrongdoing of the proponent to prevent the declarant from testifying at or attending the trial, then the declarant is not “unavailable as a witness.”

CA distinction

Lack of memory does not render the declarant unavailable, and a refusal to testify does not render the declarant unavailable unless she is found in contempt.

Hearsay Exceptions for Unavailable Declarants

Federal

California

Former Testimony

Prior witness testimony is excepted if the party against whom the testimony is offered had an opportunity and similar motive to develop the testimony

No privity requirement

Dying Declaration

In homicide and civil cases, a statement is excepted if: The declarant believed death was imminent

  • (the declarant need not have actually died) and The statement pertained to the cause or
  • circumstances of death

The declaration is admissible in all

  • criminal and civil cases The declarant must have actually died,
  • and the statement must be about the cause of death

Statement Against Interest

A statement is excepted if: It was against the declarant’s proprietary or

  • pecuniary interest at the time it was made A reasonable person would not have made
  • the statement unless it was true and For a statement that would subject the declarant
  • to criminal liability, corroborating circumstances clearly indicate its trustworthiness

A statement is excepted if: It was against the declarant’s proprietary/

  • pecuniary interest, subjected the declarant to civil/criminal liability, rendered a claim by the declarant invalid, or risked making the declarant an object of hatred/ ridicule/social disgrace and A reasonable person would not have
  • made the statement unless true

Statement of Personal or Family History

A statement about birth, adoption, marriage, divorce, or a similar fact of personal or family history is excepted

No distinction

Statement Against Party hat Caused Unavailability

A statement offered against a party that wrongfully caused the declarant’s unavailability is excepted

No distinction

Exam tip

A statement against interest should not be confused with a party admission. Party admissions need not be “against interest,” and a statement against interest is not limited to parties.

ca-fro-evidence:declarant-witness-s-prio.html:
The Federal Rules of Evidence identify three types of prior statements by a declarant-witness that are nonhearsay:

  • A prior statement of identification of a person after perceiving that person is admissible as nonhearsay substantive evidence even if the witness has no memory of the identification.
  • A prior consistent statement is admissible to rebut an express or implied charge that the declarant recently fabricated the statement or acted with an improper motive. However, the prior statement must have been made before the declarant had a reason to fabricate it.
  • A prior inconsistent statement made under penalty of perjury is admissible to impeach the declarant’s credibility and as substantive evidence.
Exam tip

Any prior inconsistent statement can be used for impeachment because impeachment is a nonhearsay purpose.

However, for such statements to be admissible, the declarant must (1) testify at the present trial or hearing and (2) be subject to cross-examination about the statement. Therefore, this hearsay exclusion cannot apply if the declarant is dead or otherwise unable to testify.

CA distinction
  • Prior statement of identification – The person’s memory must be recent.
  • Prior consistent statements – A statement made by a witness that is consistent with her testimony and was made before an inconsistent statement that has been admitted to attack the witness’s credibility is also admissible as substantive evidence.
  • Prior inconsistent statements – The statement need not be under oath.

ca-fro-evidence:exclusion-of-relevant-ev.html:
Relevant evidence may be excluded if its probative value substantially outweighed by certain dangers:

  • Unfair prejudice
  • Confusing the issues
  • Misleading the jury
  • Undue delay
  • Waste of time
  • Needlessly cumulative

This rule favors admissibility. Therefore, evidence may be admissible even if the danger of unfair prejudice (or another danger) outweighs its probative value, so long as the danger does not do so “substantially.”

CA distinction

Proposition 8 states that relevant evidence shall not be excluded in any criminal proceeding unless subject to an exemption (e.g., California Evidence Code privileges, hearsay exceptions).

On Evidence essays, always state Proposition 8 at the outset.

ca-fro-evidence:expert-witness-testimony.html:
A witness who is qualified as an expert by knowledge, skill, experience, training, or education may testify in the form of an opinion if the proponent demonstrates that:

  • The expert has scientific, technical, or other specialized knowledge
  • The expert’s knowledge will help the trier of fact understand the evidence or determine a fact in issue
  • The expert’s testimony is based on sufficient facts or data
  • The expert’s testimony is the product of reliable principles and methods and
  • The expert’s opinion reflects a reliable application of those principles and methods to the facts of the case.

When an expert’s opinion is based on inadmissible facts or data, the opinion is admissible if experts in that particular field would reasonably rely on those kinds of facts or data in forming an opinion on the subject. The proponent may disclose the otherwise inadmissible facts to the jury if their probative value substantially outweighs their prejudicial effect. The opposing party can then challenge the adequacy of the expert’s knowledge on cross-examination.

Additionally, an expert must have a reasonable degree of certainty in her opinion. And though an expert may express an opinion on an ultimate issue in most cases, an expert cannot state an opinion about whether a criminal defendant had the requisite mental state for a crime.

CA distinction

California courts apply the Frye test, which requires a preliminary showing that the scientific theory is generally accepted as valid and reliable in the relevant scientific field.

ca-fro-evidence:face-to-face-confrontati.html:
The Confrontation Clause also protects criminal defendants’ right to be confronted with the witnesses against them, though face-to-face confrontation may be denied if an important public interest is at stake.

Exam tip

Think of the Confrontation Clause as a separate hurdle for prosecutors.

ca-fro-evidence:form-of-questions.html:
Leading questions suggest the answer within the question. Leading questions are not permitted on direct examination unless (1) the witness is hostile, (2) a leading question is needed to develop the witness’s testimony, or (3) the witness struggles with communication. However, leading questions are permitted and unrestricted on cross-examination.

Leading questions are distinct from improper questions, which are generally prohibited. A question is improper if it has any of the following characteristics:

Improper Questions

Characteristic

Definition

Argumentative

Intended to present an argument rather than elicit a factual response

Assumes facts not in evidence

Assumes as true certain unestablished facts

Calls for a conclusion or an opinion

Requires the witness to draw a conclusion or state an opinion the witness is not qualified to give

Compound

Requires answers to multiple questions

Lacks foundation

Examining counsel failed to establish a necessary predicate (e.g., authenticating tangible evidence)

Repetitive

Question was already asked and answered (but a question asked on direct may be repeated on cross or redirect to clarify confusion)

ca-fro-evidence:general-considerations.html:
All relevant evidence is admissible unless otherwise excluded by a specific rule, law, or constitutional provision. Evidence is relevant if it is probative (i.e., tends to make a fact more or less probable than it would be without the evidence) and material (i.e., concerns a fact that is of consequence in determining the action).

Federal

California

Evidence is considered probative if it tends to make a fact more or less probable than it would be without the evidence.

Evidence must have a tendency to prove or disprove a disputed fact to be considered probative.

ca-fro-evidence:general-rule.html:
An original document or reliable duplicate must be produced to prove the contents of a writing when:

  • The contents are at issue (e.g., contents prove an event, have a legal effect, provided facts learned by witness) or
  • The witness is relying on the contents when testifying (was not an eyewitness).

Note that a duplicate is admissible unless the original’s authenticity is questioned or it would be unfair to admit the duplicate.

CA distinction

Duplicates and other written evidence of the original are allowed, including handwritten copies.

ca-fro-evidence:habit-evidence.html:
Habit evidence is admissible to prove that a person acted in accordance with a habit—or that an organization acted in accordance with a routine—on a particular occasion. Such evidence is admissible without corroboration and without an eyewitness.

Exam tip

Habit is more specific than character evidence. On the bar exam, words like “always” or “every time” generally refer to habit, while “often” or “frequently” likely refers to character evidence.

ca-fro-evidence:impeachment.html:
Any party, including the party that called the witness to testify, may impeach a witness. Impeachment is the process of challenging a witness’s testimony based on the witness’s:

  • Character for untruthfulness
  • Bias or interest (e.g., reason to lie, relationship with a party)
  • Ability to perceive or testify accurately or
  • Prior contradictory statements.

ca-fro-evidence:impeachment-of-a-hearsay.html:
When a hearsay statement is admitted into evidence, the declarant’s credibility may be attacked (and, if attacked, supported) by any evidence that would be admissible if the declarant had testified as a witness. If the declarant is called as a witness, then the declarant may be examined as if under cross-examination.

CA distinction

Leading questions cannot be used when cross-examining a hearsay declarant.

ca-fro-evidence:lay-witness-opinion.html:
A lay (nonexpert) witness’s opinion testimony is admissible if it is:

  • Rationally based on the witness’s perception (e.g., appearance, emotion, intoxication, vehicle speed)
  • Helpful to clearly understand a witness’s testimony or to determine a fact in issue and
  • Not based on scientific, technical, or specialized knowledge.

crumb: CHAPTER V ▪ PRIVILEGES AND OTHER POLICY EXCLUSIONS
ca-fro-evidence:liability-insurance.html:
Evidence that a person was or was not insured against liability is:

  • Not admissible to prove whether the person acted negligently or wrongfully but
  • Admissible to prove agency, ownership or control, or a witness’s bias or prejudice.

ca-fro-evidence:medical-diagnosis-or-tre.html:
A statement describing a patient’s medical history or past/present symptoms is excepted from hearsay if it is made for the purpose of medical diagnosis or treatment. The statement need not be made by the patient, and this exception extends to statements made to a physician, other medical personnel, and family members.

CA distinction

This rule only applies to a victim’s statement that (1) was made when the victim was under 12 years of age and (2) describes child abuse or neglect.

ca-fro-evidence:opposing-party-s-stateme.html:
A statement made by a party to the current litigation is nonhearsay when it is offered by an opposing party. This exclusion includes statements made without personal knowledge, opinions, and statements that are not against the party’s interest. It also includes:

  • Adoptive admissions, which are statements made by another person that a party expressly or impliedly adopts as the party’s own. Silence in response to a statement is considered an adoptive admission if (1) the party understood the statement, (2) the party had the ability and opportunity to deny the statement, and (3) a reasonable person similarly situated would have denied the statement.
  • Vicarious statements, which are statements made by a person (e.g., employee, agent, authorized speaker) that are imputed to an opposing party based on the relationship between them.
  • Statements made by conspirators during and in furtherance of a conspiracy that are offered against co-conspirators.
  • Judicial admissions, which are conclusive evidence when the admission is made in a pleading, during the discovery process, or during a proceeding. However, withdrawn guilty pleas are generally inadmissible in subsequent proceedings.

crumb: CHAPTER V ▪ PRIVILEGES AND OTHER POLICY EXCLUSIONS
ca-fro-evidence:physician-patient-privil.html:
The patient holds this privilege, which is recognized by many states. It applies to statements made by a patient to a physician for the purpose of obtaining medical treatment. However, this privilege does not exist if:

  • A dispute exists between the patient and the physician
  • It is asserted in a federal question case (because federal law does not recognize this privilege)
  • The information was acquired for reasons other than treatment
  • The patient contractually waived the privilege
  • The patient’s physical condition is at issue or
  • The statement is part of a crime.
CA distinction

The statement must be made to a licensed physician, and the privilege applies whether the patient is seeking treatment or diagnosis. However, the privilege does not apply in criminal cases.

ca-fro-evidence:present-sense-impression.t: Present Sense Impression and Excited Utterance
ca-fro-evidence:present-sense-impression.html:
The following statements are deemed reliable and therefore excepted from hearsay because they are made without time to fabricate:

  • Present sense impression – a statement explaining or describing an event or condition that is made while the declarant perceives it or immediately after
  • Excited utterance – a statement about a startling event or condition that is made while the declarant is under the stress of the excitement it caused.
CA distinction

A present sense impression is called a “contemporaneous” statement. It is a statement offered to explain or make understandable the declarant’s conduct (more akin to a verbal act).

An excited utterance is called a “spontaneous” statement (the terms are often used interchangeably). It is a statement that (1) purports to narrate, describe, or explain an act, condition, or event perceived by the declarant and (2) was made spontaneously while the declarant was under the stress of excitement caused by such perception.

ca-fro-evidence:prior-inconsistent-state.html:
A prior statement can be used to impeach a witness if it is inconsistent with the witness’s present testimony. However, unless otherwise ordered by the court, extrinsic evidence of the prior inconsistent statement cannot be admitted until:

  • The witness is given the opportunity to explain or deny the statement and
  • The opposing party can examine the witness about it.

Note that the opportunity to explain or deny does not apply when the statement impeaches a hearsay declarant or qualifies as an opposing party’s statement.

CA distinction

All prior inconsistent statements are admissible for their truth and may be introduced as substantive evidence. Additionally, extrinsic evidence made in writing is admissible without actual showing of the document.

crumb: CHAPTER V ▪ PRIVILEGES AND OTHER POLICY EXCLUSIONS
ca-fro-evidence:psychotherapist-patient-.html:
The patient holds this privilege, which is recognized by federal courts and most states. It applies to confidential communications between a psychiatrist, psychologist, or licensed social worker and a patient for the purpose of treatment. But the privilege does not exist if:

  • The patient’s mental condition is at issue
  • The statement was a result of a state-ordered exam or
  • The case is a commitment proceeding against the patient.
CA distinction

In addition, there is no privilege if the patient is (1) a danger to himself/others or (2) under 16 years old and the possible victim of a crime.

Other California privileges include:

  • Clergy-penitent – The clergy member must customarily receive such communications, and both the penitent and the clergy member hold the privilege.
  • Professional journalist – Newspersons and other journalists may not be held in contempt for refusing to disclose any news source or any unpublished information obtained or prepared in the course of their job. However, a court can compel disclosure in limited circumstances.
  • Government – A public entity is privileged to refuse to disclose information that was acquired in confidence by a public employee and not open or officially disclosed to the public before the privilege is claimed.

ca-fro-evidence:recollection-refreshed.html:
Present Recollection Refreshed vs. Past Recollection Recorded

Rule

Admissibility

Use by Opponent

Present recollection refreshed

Item may be used to refresh a witness’s memory before or during testimony if:

  • The witness once knew but can no longer recall a fact
  • The item will help the witness remember and
  • The witness’s testimony is based on the refreshed recollection—not the item itself
  • Inspect item
  • Compel production
  • Use on cross- examination
  • Introduce into evidence

Past recollection recorded

A record may be used as substantive proof and read into evidence if:

  • The witness once knew but can no longer recall a fact
  • The witness made or adopted the record when the fact was fresh in mind and
  • The record accurate reflects the witness’s knowledge
  • Offer record as exhibit

Federal

California

When the item used to refresh a witness’s recollection is a writing, the adverse party is entitled to compel production of the item.

Failure to produce a writing used to refresh a witness’s memory regarding a matter will generally result in any testimony concerning that matter being stricken.

ca-fro-evidence:recorded-recollection.html:
If a witness is unable to testify on a matter for which a record exists, the record is excepted from hearsay if:

  • The record concerns a matter the witness once knew about
  • The record was made when the matter was fresh in the witness’s memory
  • The record accurately reflects the witness’s knowledge and
  • The witness testifies that he cannot recall the matter well enough to testify about it even after consulting the record on the stand.

The witness may read the record to the jury, but the record may be received as an exhibit only if offered by an adverse party.

Exam tip

Remember that a present recollection refreshed refers to an item that can be used to refresh a witness’s memory regardless of admissibilty, while a recorded recollection refers to admissible hearsay.

ca-fro-evidence:rehabilitation-of-a-witn.html:
An impeached witness may be rehabilitated by:

  • Providing an explanation or clarification on redirect examination
  • Offering opinion or reputation evidence of the witness’s character for truthfulness, if character was attacked on that ground or
  • Offering a prior consistent statement to rebut an express or implied charge that the witness lied due to an improper motive or influence.
CA distinction

A witness may also be rehabilitated by a prior consistent statement that counters and was made before an inconsistent statement that has already been introduced.

ca-fro-evidence:sixth-amendment-confront.html:
In a criminal trial, the Confrontation Clause precludes the admission of testimonial hearsay evidence against the defendant unless (1) the hearsay declarant is unavailable and (2) the defendant had a prior opportunity to cross-examine the declarant.

A statement is testimonial if it was made under circumstances that would cause an objective witness to reasonably conclude that the statement would be available for use at a later trial (e.g., made in an affidavit).

The Confrontation Clause also limits the hearsay exception for statements against a party that wrongfully caused the declarant’s unavailability to cases where the defendant acted with the particular purpose of making the witness unavailable.

crumb: CHAPTER V ▪ PRIVILEGES AND OTHER POLICY EXCLUSIONS
ca-fro-evidence:spousal-privileges.html:
Spousal Immunity vs. Marital Communications

Elements

Spousal Immunity

Marital Communications

Applicability

Criminal cases only

Criminal and civil cases

Timing

Assert during valid marriage (expires upon divorce or annulment)

No time limit to assert (applicable after divorce or death)

Protected Communications

Applies to testimony about events that occurred before or during marriage

Applies to confidential communications made between spouses during marriage

Privilege Holder

  • Majority rule – witness-spouse can choose (but not be compelled) to testify
  • Minority rule – party-spouse can prevent witness-spouse from testifying
  • Majority rule – either spouse can refuse to testify or prevent the other spouse from testifying
  • Minority rule – communicating spouse can assert the privilege

Protection

  • Protects criminal defendant’s spouse from being called by the prosecution as a witness
  • Protects person from being compelled to testify against current spouse in any criminal proceeding
  • Protects statements made privately between spouses during marriage

Exceptions

Cases in which one spouse is:

  • Suing the other spouse
  • Charged with a crime against the other spouse
  • Charged with a crime against the child of either spouse
Spousal Immunity vs. Marital Communications Elements Spousal Immunity Marital Communications Applicability Criminal and civil cases Assert during valid marriage (expires upon No time limit to assert (applicable after Timing divorce or annulment) divorce or death) Applies to confidential communications Protected Applies to testimony about events that between spouses during marriage, unless Communications occurred before or during marriage the communication was made to enable or aid anyone to commit or plan a crime or fraud Either spouse can refuse to testify or Privilege Holder Witness-spouse can choose to testify prevent the other spouse from testifying Protects criminal defendant’s spouse from being called by the prosecution as a witness Prevents anyone from testifying about Protection Protects person from being compelled statements made privately between to testify against current spouse in any spouses during marriage criminal proceeding Cases in which: One spouse is suing the other spouse One spouse is charged with a crime against the other spouse Exceptions One spouse is charged with a crime against the child, relative, or cohabitant of either spouse or The crime of bigamy is involved

Before Marriage

During Marriage

After Marriage

Spousal Immunity

Martial Communications

= protected communications = time to assert

crumb: CHAPTER V ▪ PRIVILEGES AND OTHER POLICY EXCLUSIONS
ca-fro-evidence:subsequent-remedial-meas.html:
Remedial measures taken after an accident or event are not admissible to prove negligence, other culpable conduct, a defective product or design, or the need for a warning or instruction. But they are admissible for other purposes (e.g., impeachment, proving ownership or control, establishing the feasibility of precautionary measures).

CA distinction

The exclusion only extends to negligence/culpable conduct—not strict liability. Additionally, benevolent gestures are not admissible.

ca-fro-evidence:summary-spotting-evidenc.html:
Remember to use only the Federal Rules of Evidence when answering multiple-choice questions on the bar exam. But on essay questions, be prepared to use the Federal Rules of Evidence and the California Evidence Code.

Try the following approach when tackling evidence essays on the bar exam:

  • Determine the applicable law based on the call of the question. It may instruct you to apply the federal rules or the California rules. If the call of the question is silent, apply both rules.
  • Organize your essay in accordance with the sequence presented by the call of the question. Most evidence questions will ask you to address the admissibility of each item of evidence. In those instances, label each item of evidence (e.g., “1. W’s testimony”) before discussing the relevant issues (e.g., “A. Logical Relevance”; “B. Hearsay”).
  • When analyzing the admissibility of an evidentiary item, always begin with logical relevance (i.e., whether the evidence is probative and material).

Use the table on the next page to help you spot issues that often appear in the multiple-choice portion of the exam.

Relevance

Who is offering the evidence?

Is this a civil or criminal proceeding?

What is the purpose for introducing the evidence?

Impeachment or substantive?

How and in what form is the evidence being offered?

Character evidence: typically inadmissible

Bad acts: admissible for MIMIC but inadmissible for propensity

Habit evidence: admissible

Proper Witness

Is the witness competent?

Requirements: personal knowledge, oath/affirmation

Can the witness be impeached?

Based on character for untruthfulness, bias, inability to perceive/testify accurately, contradictory prior statement, or another witness

Is an expert witness qualified?

Knowledge, skill, experience, training, or education

Proper Testimony

Is the witness a lay witness?

Opinion testimony is admissible if it is:

  • Rationally based on the witness’s perception
  • Helpful to clear understanding and
  • Not based on scientific, technical, or specialized knowledge

Is the witness an expert?

Opinion testimony is admissible if:

  • The subject matter is scientific, technical, or specialized and
  • It will help the trier of fact understand evidence/determine a fact at issue

Privilege

What is the privilege?

Consider: spousal privileges, attorney-client, physician-patient, psychotherapist-patient, self-incrimination

Who holds the privilege?

  • Does the witness hold the privilege?
  • Can the witness assert the privilege on behalf of someone else?

Are there any exceptions or waivers?

  • Communication must be confidential
  • Privilege is waived if the holder fails to timely assert it, contractually waives it, or voluntarily discloses information

Hearsay

Is it a statement?

Oral, written, or assertive nonverbal conduct

Was the statement made out of court?

  • Does the testimony quote a person or a document?
  • Is the testimony based on direct knowledge?

Is the statement offered for its truth?

Not hearsay if offered for another purpose (e.g., impeachment)

Is there an applicable exception?

Unavailable declarant: dying declaration, statement against interest, personal/family history

Availability immaterial: present sense impression, excited utterance, medical diagnosis, mental/physical condition, recorded recollection, business records, public records, learned treatises

ca-fro-evidence:what-is-hearsay.html:
Hearsay is an out-of-court statement made by a person that is offered to prove the truth of the matter asserted therein. Therefore, a statement is not hearsay if it is offered for a purpose other than its truth (e.g., proving legally operative facts, the effect on the recipient of the statement, the declarant’s state of mind, identity, impeachment and rehabilitation evidence).

Exam tip

Consider the purpose for which the proponent of the out-of-court statement seeks to use it. If it is being offered for something other than to prove the truth of the statement, then it is not hearsay.

A hearsay statement may be made orally, in writing, or by assertive nonverbal conduct. Hearsay evidence is inadmissible unless it falls within an exclusion or exception. And when a statement contains hearsay within hearsay (i.e., multiple hearsay), each part of the combined statement must conform to a hearsay exclusion or exception to be admissible.

crumb: CHAPTER V ▪ PRIVILEGES AND OTHER POLICY EXCLUSIONS
ca-fro-evidence:work-product-doctrine.html:
Documents prepared or compiled in anticipation of litigation (i.e., ordinary work product) are protected from disclosure unless the party seeking disclosure:

  • Demonstrates substantial need and
  • Cannot obtain the information by any other means without undue hardship.

However, work product may be excepted from this privilege under the crime-fraud exception.

In contrast, materials created in anticipation of litigation that contain the attorney’s mental impressions, trial tactics, conclusions, opinions, legal theories, etc. (i.e., core or opinion work product) is almost never discoverable.

ca-fro-profresp:acceptance-generally.html: There is generally no duty to accept representation of a client. However, court appointments must be accepted, absent good cause. Good cause exists if the lawyer could not handle the matter competently, the representation would result in an improper conflict of interest, or acceptance would be unreasonably burdensome.

ca-fro-profresp:advertising.html:
The general rule is that a lawyer must not make false or materially misleading statements about the lawyer or her services.

Advertising Requirements

Requirements

Model Rules

California

Advertiser’s Identity

Must include the name and contact information of at least one lawyer or firm responsible for its content

Must include the name and address of at least one lawyer or firm responsible for its content

Client’s Name

Names of regularly represented clients may be used in an advertisement with the client’s’ consent

Certified Specialist

Lawyers may state that they are certified as specialists in a particular field if they have been certified by an appropriate organization and that organization is clearly stated on the communication

Statements in Advertisements

Lawyers cannot make false or materially misleading statements

Advertisement cannot

  • Guarantee the outcome of a legal matter
  • Promise immediate cash or quick settlements or
  • Offer representation on a contingent basis unless it indicates whether the client is responsible for costs if there is no recovery

ca-fro-profresp:chapter-vii-advertising-.html:
Advertising refers to widely distributed, public statements (e.g., phone book listings, newspaper or television ads) about the services available from a lawyer or law firm.

Solicitation refers to narrower, targeted communications directed at a small group identified as requiring a specific service (e.g., letters to accident victims, in-person solicitation of employment, real-time electronic contact).

ca-fro-profresp:client-s-funds-accounts.html:
In California, all funds received or held for the benefit of a client by a lawyer or law firm must be deposited in an identifiable bank account labeled “Trust Account,” “Client’s Funds Account,” or something similar.

No commingling is allowed. Lawyers must keep client property and funds separate from their own and are subject to discipline for commingling them.

The lawyer must maintain account records for at least five years after the final distribution of the funds. And if there is a dispute over funds, then the disputed portion of the funds cannot be withdrawn until the dispute is resolved.

Notice and Delivery of Client Funds

Requirement

Model Rules

California

Notice

The lawyer must promptly notify the client of the receipt of funds

The lawyer must notify the client no later than 14 days after the receipt of funds

Delivery

The lawyer must promptly deliver undisputed funds to the client

The lawyer must deliver undisputed funds to the client within 45 days. Failure to do so creates a rebuttable presumption that the lawyer violated the CA RPC

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ LITIGATION AND OTHER FORMS OF ADVOCACY
COMMUNICATIONS IN THE COURSE OF REPRESENTATION
ca-fro-profresp:communicating-with-a-cli.html:
Lawyers have a duty to keep clients reasonably informed of the status of the matter and to respond to their reasonable requests for information so that clients can make informed decisions.

A lawyer who receives a settlement offer in a civil controversy or a proffered plea bargain in a criminal case must promptly inform the client of its substance unless a previous arrangement has been made for dealing with such offers.

ca-fro-profresp:communication-with-perso.t: Communication With Persons Represented by Counsel
crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ LITIGATION AND OTHER FORMS OF ADVOCACY
COMMUNICATIONS IN THE COURSE OF REPRESENTATION
ca-fro-profresp:communication-with-perso.html:
A lawyer is not permitted to communicate about the subject of the representation with any person the lawyer knows is represented by counsel unless the lawyer has the consent of the other lawyer or is authorized by law to do so.

And under the CA RPC, a lawyer is prohibited from communicating with a constituent of a represented organization regarding any act or omission that may be binding on or imputed to the organization for the purpose of liability. The Model Rules have no such prohibition.

ca-fro-profresp:conduct-in-the-course-of.t: CONDUCT IN THE COURSE OF LITIGATION
crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ LITIGATION AND OTHER FORMS OF ADVOCACY
ca-fro-profresp:conduct-in-the-course-of.html:
Conduct in the Course of Litigation

Duty

Model Rules

California

Avoid Frivolous Claims

A lawyer cannot bring a claim unless there is a basis in law and fact for doing so that is not frivolous

A lawyer cannot present a claim that is not warranted under existing law unless it can be supported by a good-faith argument for a change in existing law

Expedite Litigation

A lawyer must make reasonable efforts to expedite litigation consistent with the interests of the client

Test: The lawyer’s course of action must be for a substantial purpose other than delay

There is no specific duty to expedite litigation, but a lawyer must not use means that have no substantial purpose other than to delay or prolong the proceeding or cause needless expense

Candor to Tribunal

A lawyer must not knowingly make (and must correct) false statements of law or fact

Fairness to Opposing Party or Counsel

A lawyer must not unlawfully obstruct another party’s access to evidence or unlawfully alter, destroy, or conceal a document or other material having potential evidentiary value

Additionally, the CA RPC prohibit a lawyer from suppressing any evidence that the lawyer or her client has a legal obligation to reveal

A lawyer generally must not offer any inducement to a witness but may:

  • Pay a nonexpert witness the statutory fee and reasonable expenses or
  • Pay an expert witness a reasonable rate and reasonable expenses

A lawyer must not offer any inducement to a witness (including payment contingent upon the outcome the case or testimony) but may pay a witness’s:

  • Reasonable expenses
  • Reasonable compensation for loss of time attending/testifying or
  • Reasonable fee for professional services as an expert

Inadvertently Sent Documents

If the receiving lawyer knows or reasonably should know that the writing is protected by privilege or the work product doctrine, then the lawyer must limit examination of the writing to no more than is necessary to determine if it is protected

A lawyer who inadvertently receives a writing relating to representation of a client, and knows or should reasonably know of the mistake, must promptly notify the sender

ca-fro-profresp:conflicts-between-curren.t: Conflicts Between Current and Former Clients
crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ INDEPENDENT PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT— CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
ca-fro-profresp:conflicts-between-curren.html:
A lawyer who formerly represented a client in a matter cannot represent a new client in the same or a substantially related matter if the new client’s interests are materially adverse to the former client’s interests. Substantially related matters involve (1) the same transaction or legal dispute or (2) a substantial risk that confidential factual information normally obtained in the prior representation would materially advance the new client’s position in the subsequent matter. A former client can waive this conflict by giving informed consent, confirmed in writing (Model Rules) or informed written consent (CA RPC).

When a Lawyer Switches Firms

Party/Entity

Model Rules

California

Lawyer

The lawyer cannot accept employment materially adverse to a client from a former law firm when the lawyer has obtained confidential information material to the new matter without the former client’s informed consent, confirmed in writing (Model Rules) or informed written consent (CA RPC).

New Firm

The conflict will not be imputed to the disqualified lawyer’s new firm if:

  • The lawyer is screened and apportioned no part of the fee and
  • Written notice and certification of compliance is given to the former client

The conflict will not be imputed to the disqualified lawyer’s new firm if:

  • The lawyer did not substantially participate in the same or a substantially related matter
  • The lawyer is timely screened and
  • Written notice is given to the former client

Old Firm

The old firm may represent a person with interests materially adverse to those of a former client now represented by the formerly associated lawyer unless:

  • The matter is the same or substantially related to the matter in which the former lawyer represented the client and
  • Any lawyer remaining in the firm has confidential information that is material to the matter

Former Government Lawyer

The lawyer is prohibited from representing a client in a matter in which the lawyer participated personally and substantially as a government lawyer, unless the appropriate government agency gives its informed consent, confirmed in writing (Model Rules) or informed written consent (CA RPC)

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ INDEPENDENT PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT— CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
ca-fro-profresp:conflicts-involving-pros.html:
In general, a lawyer may not represent a person with interests materially adverse to those of a prospective client in the same or a substantially related matter if the lawyer received information from the prospective client that:

  • Could be significantly harmful to that person in the matter (Model Rule) or
  • Is material to the matter (CA RPC).

However, representation is permissible if the affected current and prospective clients give informed consent, confirmed in writing (Model Rule) or informed written consent (CA RPC) to the representation. Representation is also permissible if:

  • The lawyer took reasonable measures to avoid exposure to more disqualifying information than was reasonably necessary to determine whether to represent the prospective client
  • The disqualified lawyer is timely screened from the matter and gets no part of the fee and
  • Written notice is promptly given to the prospective client.

ca-fro-profresp:contingent-fees.html:
Attorneys may charge a fee contingent on the outcome of the case except in (1) criminal cases and (2) domestic relations cases when the fee is contingent on obtaining a divorce or on the amount of support recovered.

Contingent Fees

Model Rules

Contingent fee agreements must be in writing and include the client’s signature. The agreement must also:

  • Include the calculation methodology of the fee and
  • Explain the details of the calculation for expense deductions

California

Contingent fee agreements must be in a writing and be signed by the lawyer and the client. The agreement must also:

  • Include the contingency fee rate
  • Explain how disbursements and costs affect the fee and the client’s recovery
  • Identify the extent to which the client must compensate the lawyer for matters not covered in the agreement and
  • State that the fee is negotiable

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ LITIGATION AND OTHER FORMS OF ADVOCACY
CLIENT FRAUD AND PERJURY BY A CLIENT OR WITNESS
ca-fro-profresp:criminal-defendant.html:
A lawyer generally cannot refuse to offer a criminal defendant’s testimony when the lawyer reasonably believes, but does not know, that the testimony is false.

But if the lawyer knows the client’s testimony will be false, then the lawyer must take remedial measures and confidentially attempt to dissuade the client from testifying falsely. Under the Model Rules, if the client refuses, then the lawyer must take further remedial measures (e.g., seek permission to withdraw).

In contrast, California applies the narrative-statement approach to perjury by a criminal defendant. So if a criminal defendant insists on testifying and the lawyer knows that the testimony will be false, then the lawyer may offer the testimony in a narrative form if the lawyer:

  • Made reasonable efforts to dissuade the client and
  • Sought permission from the court to withdraw.

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ INDEPENDENT PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT— CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
ca-fro-profresp:current-clients.html:
A lawyer cannot represent a client if doing so would be directly adverse to the interests of a current client or there is a significant risk that the representation of the client will be materially limited by the lawyer’s responsibilities to the current client, unless:

  • The lawyer reasonably believes that he can provide competent and diligent representation to each affected client
  • The representation is not prohibited by law
  • The representation does not involve the assertion of a claim by one client against another client represented by the lawyer in the same litigation or other proceeding before a tribunal and
  • The affected client gives informed consent, confirmed in writing (Model Rules) or informed written consent (CA RPC).

Under no circumstances may a lawyer represent opposing parties in the same lawsuit or transaction. However, a lawyer representing multiple clients may make an aggregate settlement if all clients consent in writing after full disclosure.

ca-fro-profresp:dealing-with-persons-unr.t: Dealing With Persons Unrepresented by Counsel
crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ LITIGATION AND OTHER FORMS OF ADVOCACY
COMMUNICATIONS IN THE COURSE OF REPRESENTATION
ca-fro-profresp:dealing-with-persons-unr.html: In dealing with a person who is not represented by counsel on behalf of a client, a lawyer is not permitted to state or imply that he is disinterested. And when a lawyer knows or reasonably should know that an unrepresented person misunderstands the lawyer’s role, the lawyer must make reasonable efforts to correct the misunderstanding.

ca-fro-profresp:death-or-substantial-bod.html:
Under the Model Rules, disclosure of confidential information is permitted to prevent “reasonably certain” death or substantial bodily harm.

The CA RPC allow disclosure reasonably believed to be necessary to prevent a criminal act that the lawyer reasonably believes will result in death or substantial bodily harm. Before disclosure, the lawyer must make a good-faith effort (if reasonable under the circumstances) to persuade the client not to commit or continue the criminal act or to prevent the threatened death or substantial bodily harm.

crumb: SCOPE, OBJECTIVE, AND MEANS OF THE REPRESENTATION
ca-fro-profresp:decisions-made-by-the-at.html:
In contrast, the lawyer is responsible for deciding the strategy and methods to employ to achieve the client’s goals. However, the lawyer must reasonably consult with the client about the means to accomplish the client’s objectives and keep the client reasonably informed about the status of the matter.

A lawyer can limit the scope of representation if the limitation is reasonable under the circumstances, it is not otherwise prohibited by law, and the client gives informed consent. Note that a lawyer is prohibited from counseling or assisting the client in conduct that the lawyer knows to be criminal or fraudulent. If the client’s conduct has already begun, then the lawyer must avoid assisting the client and withdraw from the representation. In California, a lawyer is also prohibited from counseling or assisting the client in conduct that the lawyer knows to be a violation of any law, rule, or ruling of a tribunal.

crumb: SCOPE, OBJECTIVE, AND MEANS OF THE REPRESENTATION
ca-fro-profresp:decisions-made-by-the-cl.html:
The client is responsible for the objectives and goals of the representation. This includes the authority to decide whether to:

  • Testify in a criminal case
  • Waive the right to a trial by jury or other fundamental rights
  • Enter a plea or
  • Accept a settlement offer.

Note that there are special rules for acceptance of a settlement.

Settlement Offers

Model Rules

A lawyer must communicate all settlement offers to the client, and the client must make the final decision to accept

California

A lawyer must communicate written settlement offers to the client or an oral offer in a civil matter if it is a significant development

crumb: CHAPTER V ▪ COMPETENCE, LEGAL MALPRACTICE, AND CIVIL LIABILITY
ca-fro-profresp:diligence.html:
The Model Rules require that a lawyer act with reasonable diligence and promptness in representing a client. This means that a lawyer must be dedicated and committed to the client’s interests despite inconvenience or obstruction, control his workload, act with reasonable promptness, and pursue all matters to completion.

Under the CA RPC, a lawyer also must act with reasonable diligence in representing a client. “Reasonable diligence” means that a lawyer acts with commitment and dedication to the client’s interests and does not neglect, disregard, or unduly delay a legal matter entrusted to the lawyer.

MALPRACTICE

In addition to professional discipline, a lawyer may be subject to civil liability for malpractice based upon various theories such as breach of contract or fiduciary relationship, intentional tort, or negligence.

ca-fro-profresp:elements.html:
A communication is protected by the lawyer-client privilege if it (1) is intended to be confidential and (2) is for the purpose of seeking legal advice or representation. The client holds the privilege and is the only one who can waive it. However, the lawyer must assert the privilege on the client’s behalf.

Duration of Lawyer-Client Privilege

Model Rules

Lasts indefinitely, even after the client’s death

California

Terminates when the client’s estate is settled and the personal representative is discharged

ca-fro-profresp:entering-into-business-t.t: ENTERING INTO BUSINESS TRANSACTIONS WITH A CLIENT
crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ INDEPENDENT PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT— CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
ca-fro-profresp:entering-into-business-t.html:
A lawyer must not enter into a business transaction with a client or knowingly acquire any interest adverse to a client unless:

  • The terms are fully disclosed in writing, fair and reasonable to the client, and understood by the client
  • The client is advised in writing of the desirability of seeking independent counsel and been given an opportunity to do so and
  • The client consents in writing to the transaction and its terms after full written disclosure of the terms and the lawyer’s role in the transaction (i.e., informed written consent).
Other Client Transactions

Transaction

Model Rules

California

Literary or media rights

A lawyer is prohibited from negotiating for literary or media rights relating to representation of a client prior to the conclusion of the representation

No specific ethics rule, but the rules regarding business transactions with a client (see above) may apply

Soliciting gifts

A lawyer cannot solicit a gift or prepare an instrument giving a substantial gift to the lawyer unless the lawyer is related to the client or, under the CA RPC, the client has been advised by independent counsel

Unsolicited gifts are generally acceptable if they meet general standards of fairness

Promise to limit malpractice recovery

An agreement limiting malpractice liability to a client is permitted if the client is represented by another lawyer in making the agreement

A lawyer is prohibited from entering into an agreement prospectively limiting malpractice liability to a client

Settlement of legal malpractice claim

Settlement with an unrepresented client or former client is permitted if the client is given a reasonable opportunity to seek independent counsel regarding the agreement

ca-fro-profresp:exceptions.html:
Confidential communications are not protected by the lawyer-client privilege if they:

  • Are made to enable the client’s crime or fraud (in California, the crime or fraud can be committed by anyone, not just the client)
  • Are relevant to a dispute between the lawyer and a client or between co-clients or
  • Include information likely to result in substantial bodily injury or death if not disclosed.

ca-fro-profresp:fee-splitting.html:
Fee Splitting

Scenario

Model Rules

California

Lawyers in the Same Firm

A fee may be shared with lawyers in the same law firm (even with a former or retired attorney) by agreement

Lawyers From Different Firms

Fee splitting among attorneys at different firms is permitted only if:

  • The fee is in proportion to the services rendered
  • The client gives written consent and
  • The total fee charged is reasonablei

A fee may not be shared with a different firm unless:

  • The lawyers enter into a written agreement to divide the fee
  • The client gives written consent after full disclosure and
  • The total fee is not higher solely because of fee splitting

Referral Fees

Unless paying the fees of a legal services plan or approved lawyer-referral service, a lawyer cannot pay anyone for recommending the lawyer’s services

Exception (CA RPC only): A lawyer may give a gift to a person whose recommendation resulted in employment so long as the gift is not given based on an understanding that:

  • The gift would be forthcoming or
  • Additional referrals would be made or encouraged in the future

Nonlawyers

Fee sharing with nonlawyers is prohibited unless the fee is paid:

  • To a lawyer’s estate or a specific person as a death benefit
  • To law firm staff through compensation or retirement agreements
  • As part of a court-ordered fee and shared with a nonprofit organization that associated with the lawyer on the matter
  • To a lawyer’s estate or representative by a lawyer who purchases the practice or
  • (CA RPC only) To a lawyer referral service

Reciprocal Referral Agreements

A reciprocal referral agreement is allowed if:

  • It is not exclusive
  • The client is informed of the existence and nature of the agreement and
  • (Model Rules only) The agreement is not of indefinite duration

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ INDEPENDENT PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT— CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
ca-fro-profresp:financial-assistance.html:
Under the Model Rules, a lawyer is prohibited from providing financial assistance to a client with respect to pending litigation, but the lawyer is permitted to advance litigation costs to the client.

Under the CA RPC, a lawyer is prohibited from directly or indirectly paying or agreeing to pay the personal or business expenses of a prospective or existing client. However, a lawyer is permitted, with the client’s consent, to pay such expenses to third persons from funds collected or to be collected for the client as a result of the representation.

Therefore, the Model Rules limit financial assistance only in the context of litigation, while the CA RPC limit financial assistance generally.

ca-fro-profresp:general-rule.html:
Under the Model Rules, a lawyer is prohibited from disclosing information relating to the representation of a client (regardless of the source), unless:

  • The client gives informed consent
  • The disclosure is impliedly authorized to carry out the representation or
  • Other specific exceptions apply.

This includes information that could reasonably lead to the discovery of protected information by a third party.

Under the CA RPC, a lawyer must not reveal any confidential information, unless:

  • The client gives informed consent after adequate communication of the material risks and reasonably available alternatives or
  • The lawyer reasonably believes that disclosure is necessary to prevent a criminal act likely to result in death or substantial bodily harm.

The duty of confidentiality also requires that a lawyer take reasonable precautions to safeguard confidential information from inadvertent or unauthorized disclosure.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ LITIGATION AND OTHER FORMS OF ADVOCACY
CLIENT FRAUD AND PERJURY BY A CLIENT OR WITNESS
ca-fro-profresp:general-rule-2.html:
A lawyer is prohibited from knowingly offering false evidence and may refuse to offer evidence that the lawyer reasonably believes is false.

Exam tip

Remember that the prohibition against offering false evidence applies only if the lawyer knows that the evidence is false. A mere reasonable belief that evidence is false does not preclude a lawyer from presenting that evidence to the trier of fact.

When a lawyer learns of false material evidence after it is offered, the lawyer must take reasonable remedial measures. Unlike the Model Rules, reasonable remedial measures under the CA RPC do not include disclosure of confidential client information that the lawyer is required to protect.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ LITIGATION AND OTHER FORMS OF ADVOCACY
COMMUNICATIONS IN THE COURSE OF REPRESENTATION
ca-fro-profresp:improper-ex-parte-commun.html:
A lawyer is not permitted to communicate ex parte (i.e., without the opposing party or his lawyer present) with persons serving in an official capacity in the proceeding (e.g., judges, jurors, other officials) unless the communication is for “housekeeping” or emergency matters.

Exam tip

Look for questions placing a lawyer with good intentions in an ex parte situation with a judge or juror. If the lawyer communicates with that person, he is subject to discipline even though no harm was intended or results.

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ INDEPENDENT PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT— CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
AS AFFECTED BY LAWYER’S PERSONAL INTEREST
ca-fro-profresp:in-general.html:
A lawyer must not represent a client if there is a significant risk that representation may be materially limited by the lawyer’s own interests unless:

  • The lawyer reasonably believes that he can provide competent/diligent representation to the affected client
  • The representation is not prohibited by law
  • The representation does not involve the assertion of a claim by one client against another client represented by the lawyer in the same litigation or other proceeding before a tribunal and
  • The affected client gives informed consent confirmed in writing (Model Rules) or informed written consent (CA RPC).
Exam tip

Instead of “informed consent confirmed in writing” as under the Model Rules, the CA RPC have a requirement of “informed written consent,” which requires written disclosure of the potential adverse consequences of the client consenting to a conflicted representation.

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ INDEPENDENT PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT— CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
ca-fro-profresp:in-general-2.html:
If one lawyer in a firm is unable to represent a client, then the prohibition applies to all other lawyers in the firm unless the prohibition is based on a personal interest of one lawyer and there is no significant risk of materially limiting representation of the client. However, a client can waive this disqualification if:

  • The lawyer reasonably believes that he can provide competent and diligent representation
  • The representation is not prohibited by law
  • The lawyer is not representing two clients with claims against each other in the same litigation and
  • The client gives informed consent, confirmed in writing (Model Rules) or informed written consent (CA RPC).

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ INDEPENDENT PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT— CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
ca-fro-profresp:lawyer-as-a-witness.html:
A lawyer may be disqualified from representing a client if the lawyer is likely to be a necessary witness in the action in which she is representing the client.

Limitations on Lawyers as Witnesses

Model Rules

The lawyer may not represent the client unless:

  • The lawyer’s testimony relates to an uncontested issue
  • The testimony relates to the value of the legal services or
  • Disqualification would be a substantial hardship to the client

California

The lawyer may not represent the client unless:

  • The lawyer’s testimony relates to an uncontested matter
  • The testimony relates to the value of the legal services or
  • The lawyer obtains the client’s informed written consent

ca-fro-profresp:lawyer-s-duties-upon-ter.html:
To mitigate harm to the client, the lawyer must (1) give reasonable notice to the client to allow the client time to find other counsel and (2) return the client’s papers and property.

Duty to Return Fees and Papers

Item

Model Rules

California

Fees

A lawyer must promptly refund any unearned advance fees.

A lawyer need not return a true retainer fee.

Papers

A lawyer can retain papers as security for a fee.

A lawyer must return all papers regardless of fee payment. Note that a lawyer in a criminal matter may be required to retain a copy of a former client’s file.

ca-fro-profresp:mandatory-and-permissive.html:
Mandatory Withdrawal

Reason

Model Rules

California

Resulting Violation

A lawyer must withdraw if failure to do so would result in violating the ethics rules or other laws

A lawyer must withdraw if he knows, or reasonably should know, that the representation will violate the CA RPC or the SBA (but not other law)

Lawyer’s Health

A lawyer must withdraw when the lawyer’s physical or mental condition materially impairs his ability to represent the client

A lawyer must withdraw when the lawyer’s physical or mental condition renders it unreasonably difficult to carry out the representation effectively

Client’s Bad Faith

N/A

A lawyer must withdraw when the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that the client asserted a claim without probable cause or to harass or injure another person

Discharge

A client has an absolute right to discharge a lawyer. The lawyer is required to withdraw from the representation upon being discharged, unless he is ordered by the court to continue the representation

Under the Model Rules, permissive withdrawal is allowed if doing so would not materially harm the client. In contrast, under the CA RPC, a lawyer cannot withdraw merely because it would not materially harm the client. However, there are instances in which a lawyer may withdraw even when doing so would materially harm the client.

Permissive Withdrawal (even with material harm to client)

Reason

Model Rules

California

Breach of Obligation

The client fails substantially to fulfill an obligation to the lawyer regarding the lawyer’s services

The client breaches a material term of an agreement with or obligation to the lawyer regarding the representation

Rule Violation

N/A

Continued representation is likely to violate the CA RPC or the SBA

Course of Action

The client insists on a course of action that the lawyer finds repugnant or fundamentally disagrees with

N/A

Client Assent

N/A

The client knowingly and freely assents to termination of representation

Co-Counsel Issues

N/A

The lawyer’s inability to work with co-counsel indicates that the client’s best interests likely will be served by withdrawal

Crime or Fraud

The lawyer reasonably believes that the client’s actions are criminal or fraudulent

The client seeks to (or insists the lawyer) pursue a criminal/fraudulent course of conduct or used the lawyer’s services to advance conduct the lawyer reasonably believes was a crime/fraud

The client used the lawyer’s services to perpetrate a crime/fraud

Difficult Client

The client makes representation unreasonably difficult

Financial Burden

Representation will cause an unreasonable financial burden to the lawyer

N/A

Lawyer’s Health

N/A

The lawyer’s mental or physical condition renders it difficult for her to effectively represent the client

Unsupported Claim

N/A

The client’s claim is not supported under existing law or a good-faith argument for modification or reversal

Catchall

Other good cause exists

crumb: CHAPTER VIII ▪ MAINTAINING THE INTEGRITY OF THE PROFESSION
ca-fro-profresp:misconduct-committed-by-.html:
A lawyer is generally subject to discipline for misconduct by others if the lawyer:

  • Ordered the misconduct or ratified it with knowledge of the specific conduct or
  • Had direct supervisory authority over another lawyer or law-related employee and knew of the conduct at a time when its consequences could have been avoided or mitigated but failed to take reasonable remedial action.

Partners in a law firm must make reasonable efforts to ensure that the firm has measures in place that give reasonable assurance that all lawyers and nonlawyers in the firm conform to the ethics rules.

Exam tip

Partners’ obligations are often tested in the context of paralegals or nonlawyers making mistakes without the lawyer’s knowledge. The outcome depends on whether the firm’s procedures were established and followed.

A lawyer with direct supervisory authority over another lawyer or nonlawyer must make reasonable efforts to ensure that the supervised person’s conduct conforms to the ethics rules.

Exam tip

Supervisory obligations are frequently tested in situations where nonlawyers are not properly instructed on the need to maintain confidential communication.

A subordinate lawyer must conform to the ethics rules even if acting under the direction of a supervising lawyer. However, a subordinate lawyer does not violate the ethics rules if the subordinate lawyer is acting in accordance with the supervising lawyer’s reasonable resolution of an arguable question of professional duty.

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ INDEPENDENT PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT— CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
AS AFFECTED BY LAWYER’S PERSONAL INTEREST
ca-fro-profresp:opposing-lawyers-related.html:
Under the Model Rules, a lawyer related to another lawyer (e.g., parent, spouse, child, sibling) may not represent a client in a matter in which that lawyer is representing another party, unless each client gives informed consent.
