In California, unless the lawyer provides written disclosure of the relationship to the client, a lawyer cannot represent a client in a matter in which the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that another party’s lawyer:

  • Is a spouse, parent, child, or sibling of the lawyer
  • Lives with the lawyer
  • Is a client of the lawyer or
  • Has an intimate personal relationship with the lawyer.

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ INDEPENDENT PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT— CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
INFLUENCE BY PERSONS OTHER THAN THE CLIENT
ca-fro-profresp:organization-as-a-client.html:
A lawyer who represents an organization (e.g., corporation) generally owes the duties of loyalty and confidentiality to the organization—not its individual constituents (e.g., officers, directors, employees).

Hire lawyer to

However, a lawyer may represent the organization and its constituents as long as no other conflict exists. The lawyer must explain the client’s identity to constituents when the organization’s interests are or may become adverse to those of the constituents.

Upon learning of an action by a constituent that the lawyer knows or reasonably should know is a violation of a legal obligation or a violation of the law that will likely cause substantial injury to the organization, the lawyer must:

  • Proceed as is reasonably necessary in the best interest of the organization and
  • Report misconduct to a higher authority in the organization if the lawyer reasonably believes it is in the best interest of the organization.

But in California, in taking any such action, a lawyer must not reveal any information protected by the duty of confidentiality.

If the highest authority within the organization fails to act in a timely and appropriate manner, then the lawyer:

  • May report the action to an outside authority to the extent that the lawyer reasonably believes necessary to prevent substantial injury (Model Rules) or
  • Must proceed as reasonably necessary in the best interest of the organization, which may include the lawyer’s right/duty to resign or withdraw (CA RPC).

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ INDEPENDENT PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT— CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
INFLUENCE BY PERSONS OTHER THAN THE CLIENT
ca-fro-profresp:payment-from-a-third-par.html:
A lawyer may not accept payment for representation from someone other than the client unless:

  • The client gives informed consent
  • There is no interference with the lawyer’s professional judgment and
  • Lawyer-client confidentiality is preserved.

California also requires that the lawyer obtain the client’s informed written consent.

Exam tip

A common exam question involves a parent paying for representation of a son or daughter. Remember that a competent child, not the parent, is the client whose expressed interests govern.

crumb: CHAPTER V ▪ COMPETENCE, LEGAL MALPRACTICE, AND CIVIL LIABILITY
ca-fro-profresp:professional-competence.html:
The Model Rules require that a lawyer possess the legal knowledge, skill, thoroughness, and preparation reasonably necessary for the representation. In contrast, the CA RPC require that a lawyer not intentionally, recklessly, with gross negligence, or repeatedly fail to perform legal services with competence. Competence, as defined in the CA RPC, includes learning and skill as well as the mental, emotional, and physical ability reasonably necessary to perform such services.

When a lawyer lacks competence, the Model Rules require that the lawyer decline or withdraw, become competent without unreasonable delay, associate with competent counsel, or refer the client to a competent lawyer. Under the CA RPC, a lawyer must possess sufficient learning and skill before performance is required.

ca-fro-profresp:prohibited-objectives-of.html:
In California, a lawyer must not:

  • Bring an action for the purpose of harassing or maliciously injuring a person or
  • Present a claim or defense not warranted under existing law, unless it can be supported by a good-faith argument for modification or reversal.

ca-fro-profresp:proprietary-interest-in-.t: Proprietary Interest in Cause of Action
crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ INDEPENDENT PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT— CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
ca-fro-profresp:proprietary-interest-in-.html:
Under the Model Rules, a lawyer must not obtain a proprietary interest in the cause of action or subject matter of litigation in which the lawyer is representing a client except when the lawyer:

  • Acquires a lien to secure fee payment or
  • Contracts for a contingent fee (not in domestic relations or criminal matters).

ca-fro-profresp:prospective-clients.html:
A lawyer is generally not permitted to use or reveal information learned in consultation with a prospective client. However, under the CA RPC, communications are not protected if they are made:

  • By a person who consults a lawyer without a good-faith intention to retain the lawyer
  • After the lawyer states that she is unwilling or unable to represent the person or
  • Unilaterally without a reasonable expectation that the lawyer will be retained.

ca-fro-profresp:reasonable-fee.html:
A lawyer must charge fees that are reasonable under the circumstances. The following factors may be considered to determine the reasonableness of the lawyer’s fees:

  • Difficulty of the case (e.g., time, labor, novelty, necessary skill)
  • Preclusion of other employment (e.g., time requirements, potential conflicts)
  • Development of the attorney-client relationship and fee arrangement (e.g., fixed, contingent, prepaid)
  • Lawyer’s expertise
  • Customary fee for similar services in the locality
Exam tip

Whenever a lawyer’s fee is mentioned in a fact pattern, consider the reasonableness of that fee.

California prohibits illegal or unconscionable fees. Factors used to determine if a fee is unconscionable include:

  • Whether the lawyer engaged in fraud or overreaching in negotiating or setting the fee
  • Whether the lawyer has failed to disclose material facts
  • The fee amount in proportion to the value of the services performed
  • The relative sophistication of the lawyer and the client
  • The novelty and difficulty of the questions involved, and the skill needed to perform
  • The likelihood, if apparent to the client, that the matter will preclude other employment
  • The amount involved and the results obtained
  • Time limitations imposed by the client or by the circumstances
  • The nature and length of the professional relationship with the client
  • The lawyer’s experience, reputation, and ability
  • Whether the fee is fixed or contingent
  • The time and labor required and
  • Whether the client gave informed consent to the fee.

California also requires that an attorney-client contract be in writing when it is reasonably foreseeable that the total expenses, including attorney’s fees, will exceed $1,000. A copy of the written contract, signed by both parties, must be given to the client. However, a writing is not required when:

  • The client is a corporation
  • The client waives a written contract after full disclosure of the right to a written agreement
  • The fee is implied by previous similar services rendered by the lawyer to the client
  • Services were rendered in an emergency situation to avoid prejudice to the client or
  • A writing is impracticable.

Note that these exceptions do not apply to contingent fee arrangements, which must be in writing.

The written agreement must include (1) the basis of the compensation, (2) the nature of the legal services, and (3) the contractual responsibilities of the lawyer and the client. Failure to comply with the writing requirement will render the agreement voidable at the option of the client. But if the client voids the agreement, then the lawyer is entitled to a reasonable fee.

ca-fro-profresp:relationship-or-responsi.t: Relationship or Responsibility to Party or Witness
crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ INDEPENDENT PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT— CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
AS AFFECTED BY LAWYER’S PERSONAL INTEREST
ca-fro-profresp:relationship-or-responsi.html: In California, a lawyer may not represent a client in a matter in which the lawyer has (or knows that another lawyer in the lawyer’s firm has) a legal, business, financial, professional, or personal relationship with or responsibility to a party or witness in the same matter, unless the lawyer first informs the client in writing of the relationship. This is true even when there is no significant risk that the lawyer’s representation of the client will be materially limited.

crumb: CHAPTER VIII ▪ MAINTAINING THE INTEGRITY OF THE PROFESSION
ca-fro-profresp:reporting-misconduct.html:
Under the Model Rules, a lawyer must report another lawyer’s misconduct if:

  • The lawyer has actual knowledge of the misconduct and
  • The misconduct involves a material matter of clear and weighty importance that raises a serious question as to the other lawyer’s honesty or professional fitness.

In California, a lawyer must, without undue delay, inform the State Bar or an appropriate tribunal when the lawyer knows of credible evidence that another lawyer has engaged in misconduct. California also requires a lawyer to report herself to the State Bar within 30 days of having knowledge of:

  • The filing of three or more lawsuits in a 12-month period against the lawyer alleging malpractice or professional misconduct
  • The entry of a civil judgment against the lawyer for fraud, misrepresentation, breach of fiduciary duty, or gross negligence
  • The imposition of judicial sanctions against the lawyer (except discovery sanctions or monetary sanctions of less than $1,000)
  • A felony charge against the lawyer
  • A conviction of the lawyer on a misdemeanor charge for misconduct committed while practicing law that involves dishonesty or moral turpitude
  • The imposition of discipline against the lawyer by any professional or occupational agency or licensing board or
  • The reversal of a judgment in a proceeding due to willful or grossly incompetent representation or misconduct by the lawyer.

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ INDEPENDENT PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT— CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
AS AFFECTED BY LAWYER’S PERSONAL INTEREST
ca-fro-profresp:sexual-relations-with-cl.html: Sexual relations with a client are not allowed unless a consensual sexual relationship predated the lawyer-client relationship. This cannot be waived through informed consent. The CA RPC include an express exception for sexual relations with a client who is the lawyer’s spouse or registered domestic partner.

ca-fro-profresp:solicitation.html:
In-Person or Real-Time Electronic Contact

Model Rules

Real-time electronic contacts (e.g., text messages, chat rooms, or other written communications that recipients may easily disregard) are permitted

Solicitation of professional employment by live person-to-person contact (e.g., in-person, face-to-face, live telephone) is prohibited when a significant motive is the lawyer’s pecuniary gain, unless the person contacted is:

  • A lawyer
  • A person who has a family, close personal, or prior business or professional relationship with the soliciting lawyer or
  • A person who routinely uses for business purposes the type of legal services offered by the lawyer

California

Solicitation of prospective clients by real-time electronic contact or live person-to-person contact is prohibited when a significant motive for the lawyer’s action is the lawyer’s pecuniary gain, unless the person contacted:

  • Is a lawyer or
  • Has a family, close personal, or prior professional relationship with the soliciting lawyer

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ LITIGATION AND OTHER FORMS OF ADVOCACY
COMMUNICATIONS IN THE COURSE OF REPRESENTATION
ca-fro-profresp:special-responsibilities.html:
Prosecutors, as advocates and ministers of justice, must:

  • Avoid any conflict that might result from their obligation to seek justice on behalf of the public against any private client’s interest
  • Bring charges only when supported by probable cause
  • Make reasonable efforts to ensure that defendants are advised of their right to counsel and have been given reasonable opportunity to obtain counsel
  • Not attempt to obtain a waiver of important pretrial rights from an unrepresented defendant
  • Timely disclose all known exculpatory evidence or information that tends to disprove the guilt of the accused or mitigates the offense, including new evidence after conviction and
  • Take special precautions to prevent and reverse convictions of innocent defendants.

ca-fro-profresp:substantial-financial-ha.html: Under the Model Rules (but not the CA RPC), a lawyer may reveal confidential information to the extent that the lawyer reasonably believes it is necessary to prevent the client from using the lawyer’s services to commit a crime or fraud that is reasonably certain to cause substantial financial harm to another.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ LITIGATION AND OTHER FORMS OF ADVOCACY
COMMUNICATIONS IN THE COURSE OF REPRESENTATION
ca-fro-profresp:trial-publicity.html:
A lawyer is not permitted to make an extrajudicial statement that the lawyer knows or reasonably should know will be disseminated publicly and has a substantial likelihood of materially prejudicing an adjudicative proceeding. This includes prosecutors, who must refrain from making comments that have a substantial likelihood of heightening public condemnation of the accused.

However, a lawyer may make a statement that a reasonable lawyer would believe is required to protect a client from the substantial undue prejudicial effect of recent publicity not initiated by the client or the lawyer. Additionally, a lawyer may state basic information, such as:

  • The claim/offense and identity of persons involved
  • Information in the public record
  • The status of the investigation or litigation
  • The identity of a criminal defendant, including information necessary to aid in apprehension
  • The identity of investigating and arresting officers or agencies and the length of the investigation
  • Assistance in obtaining evidence and information necessary to the case and
  • A warning of danger if there is a likelihood of substantial harm to an individual or the public, but only to the extent reasonably necessary to protect them.

crumb: CHAPTER VIII ▪ MAINTAINING THE INTEGRITY OF THE PROFESSION
ca-fro-profresp:unauthorized-practice-of.html: A lawyer is obligated to ensure that only authorized persons engage in the practice of law and is subject to discipline for assisting nonlawyers in the unlicensed practice of law. Additionally, the nonlawyer may be subject to civil and criminal penalties.

ca-fro-profresp:undertaking-representati.html:
The lawyer-client relationship begins when the client reasonably believes that the relationship exists. No formal writing is required.

Exam tip

Look for casual conversations between a lawyer and a potential client. If the facts indicate that the client reasonably believes that the relationship exists, it does.

A lawyer has a duty to reject representation if it would violate an ethical rule or if the lawyer’s physical or mental condition materially impairs her ability to represent the client.

ca-fro-profresp:work-product.html: In California, writings that reflect the lawyer’s impressions, conclusions, opinions, or legal research or theories are not discoverable under any circumstances. Other work product is discoverable if denial would unfairly prejudice the requesting party.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ REMEDIES AND SPECIFIC CONTRACTS
ca-fro-remedies:building-contracts.html:
An owner’s damages are based on:

  • The reduction in the building’s market value due to the builder’s breach (value damages) or
  • The cost to complete the building or remedy any defect (cost damages) plus any special damages.

A builder’s damages are:

  • The amount owed by the owner under the contract (complete building) or
  • Lost profits (incomplete building).

Rescission and restitution are also potential remedies for breach of building contracts.

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ DECLARATORY JUDGMENT, RESTITUTION, REPLEVIN, AND EJECTMENT
ca-fro-remedies:claim-in-restitution.html:
A claim in restitution may establish liability for a benefit:

  • Received as the result of a transfer that is subject to avoidance
  • Conferred as the result of voluntary but unrequested intervention by the plaintiff or
  • Wrongfully obtained.

ca-fro-remedies:compensatory-damages.html: Compensatory damages compensate the plaintiff for a legally recognized harm or injury. The award seeks to place the plaintiff in the same position he would have been in absent the defendant’s tortious behavior

ca-fro-remedies:contract-actions.html:
For a plaintiff in a contract action, the focus is prospective. The goal is to put the plaintiff in as good a position as performance of the contract would have done. The general formula to calculate these compensatory damages is as follows:

Expectation damages

[flowchart/figure omitted — see printed outline]

Consequential damages

When the defendant is a seller who has breached a contract for the sale of goods, the formula to calculate the buyer’s damages is as follows:

If the buyer “covers” (i.e., buys substitute goods), then the buyer is entitled to the difference between the replacement cost and the contract price. In contrast, a buyer who accepts nonconforming goods may sue for breach of warranty to recover the value of the goods as warranted minus the value of the goods accepted plus any incidental and consequential damages.

A plaintiff in a contract action may alternatively recover:

  • Liquidated damages – damages agreed upon by the contracting parties during contract formation that are enforceable if the amount is reasonable based on anticipated loss or actual loss
  • Reliance damages – expenses incurred in reasonable reliance on the contract, not to exceed the contract price or
  • Restitutionary damages – when there is a total breach of the contract by the defendant, damages are measured by the benefit that the plaintiff conferred on the defendant (i.e., unjust enrichment).
Exam tip

Contracts and remedies are often tested together on the California bar exam.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ REMEDIES AND SPECIFIC CONTRACTS
ca-fro-remedies:contractual-misconduct.html: If a contract has been voided due to duress or undue influence, then the victim who has conferred a benefit on the other party is entitled to the same restitutionary remedies as those available for fraud.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ REMEDIES AND TORTIOUS HARMS
ca-fro-remedies:damages.html:
• Pain and suffering – the plaintiff is entitled to damages for loss of physical abilities, loss of enjoyment of life, illness, and/or emotional distress. The plaintiff must be aware of the pain to recover.
  • Medical expenses – the plaintiff may recover reasonable expenses necessarily incurred in treating an injury/illness resulting from the defendant’s tortious conduct. However, future expenses must be reasonably certain to be recoverable.
  • Lost earnings capacity – damages are based on the loss/diminution of the plaintiff’s earning capacity, whether the plaintiff was employed (actual earnings) or unemployed (loss of opportunity) at the time of the tort. Future earnings capacity is determined based on several factors, including the plaintiff’s age, education, employment history, and physical condition.

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ DECLARATORY JUDGMENT, RESTITUTION, REPLEVIN, AND EJECTMENT
ca-fro-remedies:defenses-and-limitations.html:
• Unclean hands – the plaintiff’s conduct was unethical or immoral and closely related to his claim (but need not have caused injury to the defendant).
  • Laches – the plaintiff unreasonably delayed pursuing a remedy that he was aware of, and that delay prejudiced the defendant.
  • Defendant’s changed position – it may be inequitable to award restitution to the plaintiff when an innocent defendant changed her position in reliance on the benefit.
  • Bona fide purchaser – equitable restitution is not available against a bona fide purchaser who took property without notice of the prior equities.
  • Discharge for value – if the defendant used a plaintiff’s mistaken payment to discharge a debt that the defendant owed to a third party, then restitution is unavailable unless the defendant had notice of the mistake or made a misrepresentation that caused the payment.
  • Unsolicited benefit – a plaintiff who unconditionally confers a benefit on a defendant is not generally entitled to restitution, unless the benefit was conferred to protect another’s life or property or to perform another’s duties.

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ DECLARATORY JUDGMENT, RESTITUTION, REPLEVIN, AND EJECTMENT
ca-fro-remedies:ejectment.html: A plaintiff who establishes title to land and the right to immediate possession is entitled to a judgment ordering the sheriff to take possession of the property and return it to the plaintiff.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ REMEDIES AND SPECIFIC CONTRACTS
ca-fro-remedies:employment-contracts.html:
Other remedies for breach of employment contracts include rescission/restitution and injunctions to enforce a covenant not to compete.

Exam tip

While California prohibits (with very narrow exceptions) contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind, remember that knowledge and application of California law are not required when answering a remedies question.

crumb: CHAPTER III ▪ SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE, RESCISSION, REFORMATION, AND EQUITABLE REMEDIES
ca-fro-remedies:equitable-defenses.html:
Equitable defenses are defenses to equitable actions (discussed above). As a result, courts have discretion to refuse to apply these defenses if there are countervailing equitable considerations. Additionally, these defenses do not prevent a plaintiff from bringing an action at law for damages. Equitable defenses include:

  • Unclean hands – the plaintiff’s conduct was unethical or immoral and closely related to the plaintiff’s claim (but need not have caused injury to the defendant).
  • Laches – the plaintiff unreasonably delayed pursuing a remedy that she was aware of, and that delay caused prejudice to the defendant.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ REMEDIES AND TORTIOUS HARMS
ca-fro-remedies:fraud-and-misrepresentat.html:
For intentional misrepresentation (fraud), the plaintiff’s damages are measured by either the loss of bargain expectancy interest or out-of-pocket loss, plus consequential and incidental damages (and punitive damages, if appropriate).

For negligent misrepresentation, the plaintiff’s damages are measured by the out-of-pocket loss plus any consequential and incidental damages.

Loss of bargain expectancy interest*

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ REMEDIES AND TORTIOUS HARMS
ca-fro-remedies:harms-to-property-intere.html:
Remedies for Harms to Land

Type of Claim

Remedies

Trespass

Damages

Nominal, consequential, or punitive

Injunction

To prevent future trespass

Restitution

  • Conscious wrongdoer – disgorgement of gains derived from the trespass
  • Other defendants – reasonable rental value or reasonable cost of a license

Encroachment

Damages

  • Permanent – measured by the reduction in value of the plaintiff’s land
  • Temporary – measured by the cost to remove the encroachment and the value of the loss of use

Injunction

  • Intentional – mandatory injunction ordering removal of the structure
  • Innocent – court applies disproportionate burden test

Injury to Real Property

Damages

Measured by:

  • Cost to repair injury or cost to replace destroyed property plus the value of the loss of use or
  • Diminution in the property value

Injunction

To prevent the defendant from injuring or destroying the plaintiff’s real property

Remedies for Harms to Land (continued)

Types of Claim

Remedies

Severance

Damages

  • Measured by the reduction in the value of the land due to the severance (or based on the value of the severed items as goods if treated like a conversion)
  • Some states – statutory damages against a willful trespasser

Injunction

To prevent a defendant’s future trespass to engage in severance

Restitution

  • Replevin the goods or
  • Restitution to the same extent as allowed for a mere trespass

Trespass With Addition to Land

Damages

Measured by:

  • The value of the loss of use or
  • The cost of restoring the land to its pre-trespass condition

Injunction

Mandatory injunction ordering removal of items on the plaintiff’s land

Restitution

To the same extent as allowed for a mere trespass

Interference With Easement

Damages

Measured by:

  • Reduction in value of easement (easement destroyed/injured) or
  • The value of the loss of use during the period of interference

Injunction

  • Mandatory or prohibitory
  • Unavailable if the defendant satisfies disproportionate burden test

Waste

Damages

Measured by:

  • Reduction in value of property due to waste or
  • Repair cost

Injunction

To prevent affirmative or ameliorative waste, but generally not with respect to permissive waste

Nuisance

Damages

  • Private – measured by diminution in value of property due to nuisance (includes past and future interference)
  • Public – measured by the plaintiff’s harm or loss that is different in kind from the general public’s

Injunction

  • Private – only available if consistent with balancing the equities
  • Public – generally available when punishment is an inadequate remedy

A plaintiff’s recovery for harm to personal property is dependent on the type of harm.

Remedies for Harm to Personal Property

Type of Harm

Remedies

Harm

Damages

  • Diminution in value or
  • Reasonable cost to repair or restore the personal property plus the value of the loss of use (capped at fair market value)

Destruction

Damages

Market value of property immediately before destruction plus interest or replacement cost

Loss of Possession

Damages

  • Trespass to chattel – same as harm to personal property
  • Conversion – same as destruction of personal property

Injunction

Only available if property is unique

Restitution

Market value of the property at:

  • The time of loss of possession or
  • Its highest value within a reasonable time after the tortious conduct

Replevin

Regain possession and recover damages for loss of use

Other

  • Constructive trust
  • Equitable lien

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ REMEDIES AND SPECIFIC CONTRACTS
ca-fro-remedies:land-contracts.html:
to a title defect the seller was unaware of and unable to correct.

Other remedies in land contract cases include specific performance (because land is unique), rescission/ restitution (if sought by a nonbreaching party), and equitable conversion.

ca-fro-remedies:limitations-on-damages.html:
• Causation – the plaintiff must show that the defendant’s conduct was the “but for” cause (i.e., actual cause) of the plaintiff’s injuries.
  • Foreseeability (negligence) – for negligence actions, the plaintiff must also prove proximate causation (i.e., legal causation), so the defendant is only liable for harms that were reasonably foreseeable at the time the defendant breached a duty.
  • Certainty – the plaintiff must establish damages with reasonable certainty. Lost profits or earnings (especially for a new business) and prospective harm are often too uncertain for recovery. As a result, emotional distress damages are not generally awarded in breach-of-contract actions.
  • Consequential damages (contracts) – these damages are recoverable if they were a natural and probable result of the breach, within the parties’ contemplation at the time the contract was entered, or otherwise foreseeable. Unlike general damages, special (consequential) damages must be specifically pleaded.
  • Avoidable consequences – the plaintiff may not recover for a harm/loss that she could have reasonably avoided. The burden is on the defendant to prove that the plaintiff could have avoided the harm.
  • Reduction for benefits – the plaintiff’s recovery will be offset by any benefits received as a direct consequence of the defendant’s tortious conduct or by any savings received as a result of the defendant’s breach of contract.
  • Collateral source – the plaintiff’s recovery is not reduced by benefits the plaintiff received from third parties (e.g., insurers) for harm suffered due to the defendant’s conduct (not applicable in contracts).

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ REMEDIES AND TORTIOUS HARMS
ca-fro-remedies:misappropriation-of-mone.html:
A plaintiff whose money has been misappropriated can seek the following remedies:

  • Damages via a conversion action and punitive damages if the defendant acted willfully or with malice
  • Restitution for defendant’s gains (conscious wrongdoer) or defendant’s benefits (innocent/negligent)
  • Replevin (i.e., return of specific property that the defendant seized)
  • Constructive trust (court-ordered trust) requiring the defendant (“trustee”) to turn over the funds
  • Equitable lien on the plaintiff’s funds that were appropriated by the defendant
  • Subrogation to the rights of the defendant’s creditor if the funds were used to pay the creditor

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ REMEDIES AND SPECIFIC CONTRACTS
ca-fro-remedies:mistake.html:
Contract reformation is available to correct a mistake in integration with regard to a valid contract. A plaintiff who conferred a nonmonetary benefit by mistake on the defendant may seek restitution of any unjust enrichment of the defendant to the extent that:

  • Specific restitution is feasible
  • The benefit was realized in money or its equivalent
  • The recipient has shown a willingness to pay for the benefit or
  • The recipient has been spared an otherwise necessary expense.

ca-fro-remedies:other-damages.html:
• Nominal damages may be awarded when a plaintiff establishes a cause of action but has suffered little to no harm or loss. However, nominal damages are not available for causes of action that require that the plaintiff establish damages (e.g., negligence, fraud).
  • Punitive damages (i.e., exemplary damages) are awarded to punish a defendant who engaged in serious misconduct with an improper state of mind (e.g., malice). Punitive damages typically do not exceed compensatory damages, and they are generally unavailable in contract actions unless the defendant’s behavior was also tortious.
  • Incidental damages are “reliance-type” reasonable expenses incurred in a failed transaction that are recoverable without special proof.

ca-fro-remedies:permanent-injunction.html:
To obtain a permanent injunction, the plaintiff must usually establish that:

  • The plaintiff will suffer irreparable harm if the injunction is not issued (inadequate remedy at law) and
  • The plaintiff’s hardships if the injunction is not issued will be significantly greater than the hardships on the defendant and third parties if the injunction is issued (balancing of equities).

Likelihood of success on the merits is not a factor in issuing a permanent injunction because the plaintiff has already succeeded on the merits.

As an equitable remedy, an injunction is subject to equitable defenses (see below).

ca-fro-remedies:prejudgment-injunctions.html:
A prejudgment injunction is often sought to prevent irreparable harm to the plaintiff until a court action is resolved. There are two main types of prejudgment injunctions: a temporary restraining order (TRO) and a preliminary injunction.

Prejudgment Injunctions

Type

Issuance

Duration

Notice

TRO

Issued before trial to preserve status quo until a preliminary injunction hearing can be held

≤ 14 days (federal)

≤ 15 days (California)

Can be issued ex parte but is not effective until the defendant is notified

Preliminary Injunction

Issued before or during triali

Effective until a final judgment

Cannot be issued without notifying the defendant of the hearing

An injunction is an equitable remedy, so courts have discretion as to the issuance of an injunction and its form. Factors a court will consider include:

  • Whether the plaintiff will suffer irreparable harm without the injunction (inadequate remedy at law)
  • Whether the plaintiff’s hardships if the injunction is not issued would be significantly greater than the hardships to the defendant and third parties if the injunction is issued (balancing of equities)
  • The likelihood that the plaintiff will succeed on the merits and
  • The feasibility of enforcement.
Exam tip

On a Remedies essay, thoroughly analyze any arguments that the plaintiff and the defendant would make regarding hardship. Keep in mind that a defendant can reference hardship to third parties.

crumb: CHAPTER III ▪ SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE, RESCISSION, REFORMATION, AND EQUITABLE REMEDIES
ca-fro-remedies:reformation.html:
Reformation is the judicial rewriting of an agreement or other document to reflect the parties’ true intent. Therefore, if an agreement does not exist (e.g., no offer or acceptance, voidable contract), then reformation is not an available remedy.

If a writing purports to reflect the parties’ prior agreement, then that writing may be reformed to conform to the prior agreement if both parties were unaware that it did not do so (i.e., there is a mutual mistake).

If only one party is mistaken as to the content or effect of a writing (i.e., there is a unilateral mistake), then reformation is not allowed unless the other party was aware of the mistake and fraudulently induced (or failed to correct) the mistaken party’s erroneous belief.

Rescission vs. Reformation

Type of Remedy

Meeting of the Minds

Result

Rescission

No

Contract will be undone to reflect that fact

Reformation

Yes

Contract will be corrected to reflect that fact

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ DECLARATORY JUDGMENT, RESTITUTION, REPLEVIN, AND EJECTMENT
ca-fro-remedies:replevin.html: A replevin action allows a plaintiff to regain possession of property from the defendant and recover damages for loss of use of that property.

crumb: CHAPTER III ▪ SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE, RESCISSION, REFORMATION, AND EQUITABLE REMEDIES
ca-fro-remedies:rescission.html:
Recission is the avoidance or unmaking of a contract. To obtain a rescission at law, the plaintiff must, prior to filing suit and promptly upon discovering grounds for rescission (e.g., mistake, fraud):

  • Give notice of the rescission to the defendant and
  • Return or tender any benefits (e.g., property, money) of the contract.

To obtain an equitable rescission, the plaintiff must return any benefits received from the defendant after the suit concludes.

Exam tip

Most states (including California) have eliminated the distinction between recission at law and equitable recission.

Once the court grants rescission, the plaintiff can bring an action against the defendant for replevin (recovery of specific property) or restitution (monetary compensation).

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ DECLARATORY JUDGMENT, RESTITUTION, REPLEVIN, AND EJECTMENT
ca-fro-remedies:restitution.html: Restitution refers both to a cause of action and the remedy available for that action. A plaintiff can recover from a defendant who has been unjustly enriched at the plaintiff’s expense if the plaintiff conferred a benefit on the defendant that measurably increased the defendant’s wealth.

crumb: CHAPTER III ▪ SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE, RESCISSION, REFORMATION, AND EQUITABLE REMEDIES
ca-fro-remedies:specific-performance.html:
Specific performance is a contractual remedy by which a party to a contract is compelled by court order to render the promised performance or a substitute. It is generally not available unless the defendant has breached a contract by nonperformance or repudiation. A plaintiff’s request for specific performance can be granted if the following requirements are met:

  • A contract exists
  • The contract terms are sufficiently certain to provide the basis for a court order
  • The conditions required for the defendant’s performance have been satisfied
  • Damages are inadequate (e.g., property is unique, shortage of goods)
  • Relief is equitable and
  • Enforcement is feasible.

ca-fro-remedies:summary-reminders-for-re.html:
Knowledge and application of California law to a party’s substantive claims is not required when answering a remedies question. Instead, you must identify a successful plaintiff’s potential remedies based on the plaintiff’s goals.

Identifying the Applicable Remedy

Plaintiff’s Goal

Remedy

Compensate the plaintiff

Restore the plaintiff’s position

Punish the defendant

Damages

Force or prevent conduct

Equitable relief (injunction or specific performance)

(Note: the legal remedy must be inadequate)

Prevent unjust enrichment

Restitution (constructive trust, equitable lien)

ca-fro-remedies:tort-actions.html: For a plaintiff in a tort action, the focus is retrospective. The goal is to put the plaintiff in the position he was in prior to the tort. Damages include compensation for past and present harm (including pain and suffering) as well as future harm (e.g., lost earnings capacity).

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ DECLARATORY JUDGMENT, RESTITUTION, REPLEVIN, AND EJECTMENT
ca-fro-remedies:types-of-remedies.html:
In a restitutionary action for a monetary judgment, recovery is based on the extent to which the defendant has been unjustly enriched at the plaintiff’s expense. Calculating a defendant’s enrichment depends on the type of defendant:

  • Conscious wrongdoer or defaulting fiduciary = the greater of the net profit attributable to the wrongdoing or the market value of the benefits wrongfully obtained
  • Wrongdoer without fault = merely the market value of the benefits wrongfully obtained

Constructive trusts and equitable liens are similar, judicially created remedies that give a plaintiff legal rights in property held by the defendant to prevent the defendant’s unjust enrichment at the plaintiff’s expense. A constructive trust imposes a duty to convey property to the plaintiff, while an equitable lien imposes a lien on the defendant’s property as security for the plaintiff’s claim against the defendant. Both require the plaintiff to prove that:

  • The defendant holds title to (not just possession of) property
  • Retaining the property would unjustly enrich the defendant and
  • A legal remedy is inadequate.

However, a plaintiff who provides services that improve the defendant’s property may be entitled to an equitable lien (but not a constructive trust) on such property.

A plaintiff may have a choice between a constructive trust and an equitable lien. Because an equitable lien does not exchange the plaintiff’s claim for the defendant’s property, an equitable lien is usually more advantageous if the value of the property has declined. In contrast, a constructive trust is often more advantageous when the value of the property has risen. That is because the plaintiff is not required to account to the defendant for the amount by which the value of the property exceeds the amount of the claim.

Exam tip

A plaintiff should pursue a constructive trust if the value of the property held by the defendant has increased but pursue an equitable lien if the property value has decreased.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ REMEDIES AND SPECIFIC CONTRACTS
ca-fro-remedies:unenforceable-contracts.html:
• Statute of Frauds (oral contracts) – damages are generally not recoverable. If estoppel applies, the plaintiff can recover reliance damages unless restitution provides an adequate remedy.
  • Impracticability/frustration of purpose – a plaintiff may recover through restitution for any benefit (e.g., the market value of the plaintiff’s performance) conferred on the defendant in reliance on their agreement.
  • Lack of capacity – a minor or person lacking mental capacity is entitled to receive full restitution with respect to whatever benefit was bestowed on the other party to the contract.
  • Illegality – restitution is permitted only in very limited circumstances (disproportionate forfeiture).

ca-fro-wills:abatement.html:
Gifts by will are reduced or abated when the assets of the estate are insufficient to pay all debts and legacies. The testator may indicate his intended order of abatement, but if he fails to do so, then the law prescribes the following order.

Abatement within a category is pro-rata, and bequests to persons other than relatives will be abated before gifts to relatives in the same category. Nonprobate property does not abate.

ca-fro-wills:ademption-by-extinction.html:
If the subject matter of a specific bequest is missing, destroyed, or experiences a substantial change (extinct), then the beneficiary generally takes nothing. However, the beneficiary may be entitled to whatever is left of the specifically devised property or the balance of the purchase price owing from the purchaser of the property.

Note that the intent of the testator is not relevant in most states if the bequest is extinct. However, California does consider the testator’s intent at the time she disposed of the subject matter of the bequest. Because an incompetent testator lacks the intent to adeem and the opportunity to avoid effect of ademption by making a new will, ademption does not apply when the testator was incompetent (unless the will was executed before the incompetency).

Exam tip

Courts will try to avoid ademption, if possible, by identifying replacement property.

ca-fro-wills:calculating-intestate-share.html:
Methods for Calculating Intestate Shares

Method

Description

Applicability

Per Capita

The property passes per capita, which divides the property equally among the issue.

Surviving issue are of all equal degree of kinship.

Per Capita with Representation

The property is divided equally among the first generation in which at least one member survives the decedent.

Shares of each member of that generation who does not survive the decedent will pass to the then-living issue of the nonliving member.

If the nonliving member has no then-living issue, then the nonliving member does not receive a share.

The issue are of unequal degree of kinship and the decedent either died intestate or did not specify the method of calculating shares.

Per Stirpes

The issue take in equal portions the share that their deceased ancestor would have taken, if living. The estate is first divided into the total number of children of the ancestor who survive or leave issue who survive.

The will calls for it.

Per Capita at Each Generation

Divides the property into as many equal shares as there are members of the nearest generation of issue who survive the decedent and deceased members of that generation with issue who survive the decedent.

The will calls for it.

ca-fro-wills:chapter-i-intestacy.html:
Key Terminology

Decedent

Someone who has died

Will

A legal document used to dispose of the decedent’s property

Testate

The decedent dies with a will

Intestate

The decedent dies without a will

Codicil

A supplement that amends or revokes a decedent’s will in whole or part

Probate

The judicial process for administering and settling the decedent’s estate

Intestate succession

The default estate plan developed by the legislature when the decedent dies

Heirs

Individuals entitled to receive property by intestate succession

Spouse

The decedent’s marital partner

Issue/decedents

The decedent’s lineal line (children, grandchildren, etc.)

Ancestor

The decedent’s parental line (parents, grandparents, etc.)

Collaterals

Relatives through an ancestor (siblings, cousins, aunts, uncles, etc.)

ca-fro-wills:classification.html:
Classification establishes the order of distribution and abatement when the estate’s assets are insufficient to satisfy all the gifts contained in the will. The classes of gifts made under the will are distinguished by the type of item given.

Gift Classification

Class

Description

Example

Specific

A transfer of specifically identifiable property

“My car to John”

General

A transfer to be satisfied from the general assets of the estate

“$1,000 to John”

Demonstrative

A general gift that specifies the fund or property from which the transfer is primarily to be made

“$100,000 to John from my X account, but if funds are not sufficient, then the rest paid out of general funds”

Residuary

A transfer of property that remains when specific, general, and demonstrative gifts have been satisfied

“I give all the rest and residue of my property to John”

ca-fro-wills:codicils.html:
A codicil is a supplement to a will that alters, amends, or modifies (but does not replace) the will. Codicils must be executed with the same formalities as a will. A validly executed codicil republishes the will as of the date of the codicil. In addition, a codicil may even validate an invalid will if the codicil refers to it with sufficient certainty to identify and incorporate it.

Holographic codicils to an attested will and attested codicils to a holographic will are both valid.

ca-fro-wills:community-and-quasi-communi.html: The surviving spouse or registered domestic partner of an intestate decedent is entitled to the decedent’s one-half of community property and one-half of quasi-community property. As a result, a surviving spouse will receive all of the community and quasi-community property.

ca-fro-wills:community-property-and-quas.html: At the death of one spouse or domestic partner, one-half of the community and quasi-community property is already owned by the other spouse or domestic partner. As a result, only the decedent’s half is subject to disposition by will.

ca-fro-wills:compliance.html:
In California, there must be “substantial compliance” with will formalities. A will is treated as though it complied with the law if the proponent of the will establishes by clear and convincing evidence that at the time the testator signed the will, the testator intended the will to constitute his will. This rule also applies to holographic wills and codicils (discussed below).

Exam tip

Begin your essay by fully analyzing the required will formalities before discussing the doctrine of substantial compliance. Remember that a testator’s intent at the time of execution must be established by clear and convincing evidence, so absence of a signature cannot be cured by relying on the substantial compliance doctrine.

ca-fro-wills:disclaimer.html: Because acceptance of a testamentary gift is presumed, a party must actively disclaim if she wishes not to accept it. A disclaimer must (1) identify the decedent, (2) describe the interest being disclaimed, and (3) state the extent of the disclaimer. This means partial disclaimers are valid. Additionally, California requires that the disclaimer be in writing, signed, and filed within nine months of the decedent’s death.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ INCAPACITY, FRAUD, AND UNDUE INFLUENCE
ca-fro-wills:elements.html:
Under the traditional doctrine (majority view), undue influence requires proof of the following elements:

  • Susceptibility (the testator was susceptible to being influenced)
  • Motive or predisposition (the influencer had reason to benefit)
  • Opportunity (the influencer had the opportunity to influence) and
  • Causation (the influencer caused an unnatural result).

The burden is on the contestant to show the existence and exertion of influence, and that the influence overpowered the mind and will of the testator.

ca-fro-wills:formalities.html:
A will requires a writing that the testator signs with present testamentary intent in the joint presence of two witnesses, and that both witnesses understand the significance of the testator’s act.

The will consists of all papers present at the time of execution that are intended to form part of the will. This is established by physical connection of the pages or the ongoing consistent nature of the will’s language.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ INCAPACITY, FRAUD, AND UNDUE INFLUENCE
ca-fro-wills:fraud.html:
Fraud, unlike undue influence, must have been present when the will was executed. The misrepresentation must be made by the beneficiary with (1) the intent to deceive the testator and (2) the purpose of influencing the testamentary disposition.

  • Fraud in the inducement is a knowingly false representation that causes the testator to make a different will than he would have otherwise made. A fraudulently procured inheritance or bequest is invalid only if the testator would have acted differently had he known the truth.
  • Fraud in the execution is fraud as to the very nature of the instrument or its contents.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ INCAPACITY, FRAUD, AND UNDUE INFLUENCE
ca-fro-wills:general-testamentary-capaci.html:
The testator lacks the requisite mental capacity if he, at the time of execution:

  • Did not have sufficient mental capacity to (1) understand the nature of the act, (2) understand and recollect the nature and character of his property, or (3) remember and understand his relationship to living descendants, spouse, parents, and those affected by his will or
  • Suffered from a mental disorder involving delusions or hallucinations, which resulted in his devising property in a way that he would not have done but for the delusions or hallucinations.

ca-fro-wills:holographic-wills.html: Holographic wills are recognized in California. To constitute a valid holographic will, the material provisions must be handwritten by the testator, and the instrument must be signed by the testator. The instrument need not be witnessed or dated, but it must be clear that the document was intended to be a will.

ca-fro-wills:homicide.html: Under the Slayer Rule, an intentional and felonious killer of the decedent is treated as predeceased and severs any joint tenancies. Additionally, California does not apply anti-lapse to protect the killer’s issue. Note that courts have not applied the Slayer Rule in involuntary manslaughter or self-defense cases.

ca-fro-wills:in-general.html:
A will can provide for the designation of a beneficiary or the amount of a disposition by reference to some unattested act or event occurring before or after the execution of the will or testator’s death if the act or event has some significance that is unrelated to the will.

Exam tip

Because this doctrine applies to acts that occur in the future (i.e., after the will is executed), acts of independent significance differ from republication by codicil and incorporation by reference.

ca-fro-wills:incorporation-by-reference.html:
Under the common law, a writing that is not executed with testamentary formalities may be incorporated by reference if it:

  • Existed at the time the will was executed
  • Is intended to be incorporated and
  • Is described in the will with sufficient certainty as to permit its identification.

California waives the requirement that the document have been in existence at the time the will was executed if the document referenced in an unrevoked will disposes only of the testator’s personal property and describes the items and recipients with reasonable certainty.

ca-fro-wills:independent-legal-significa.html: If the testator, the beneficiary, or a third person has some control over the act or event, then the will may still have independent legal significance if it is unlikely that the testator or other person would perform the act solely for testamentary reasons.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ INCAPACITY, FRAUD, AND UNDUE INFLUENCE
ca-fro-wills:insane-delusion.html:
An insane delusion is a belief for which there is no factual or reasonable basis, but to which the testator adheres to despite all reason and evidence to the contrary. Once it is determined that the testator suffered from an insane delusion, it must be shown that this was the sole cause of the testamentary disposition. In California, this requires but-for causation (i.e., that the testator would not have disposed of the property in the same manner but for the insane delusion).

Exam tip

Remember to discuss causation when analyzing whether an insane delusion exists. Unless the insane delusion was the cause of the strange disposition, there is no defect in capacity.

ca-fro-wills:lapse.html:
If a beneficiary dies before the testator, then the gift will fail (lapse) and go to the residue unless the will provides for an alternative disposition. Absent a residuary clause, the gift will pass through intestacy.

However, California and most other states have adopted an anti-lapse statute. If a gift was made to a blood relation of the testator (or the testator’s spouse) and the relation (or spouse) predeceased the testator but left issue, then the issue will succeed to the gift.

When a gift is to an entire class and one member of the class dies, the gift will not lapse. It will go to the surviving class members. However, if the anti-lapse statute applies because the deceased class member is related to the testator, then the issue of the predeceased member will also take. In California, the anti- lapse statute will not apply to save a gift to a class member who died before the will was executed if the testator knew of this fact.

ca-fro-wills:marriage-requirement.html: The surviving spouse must be legally married to the decedent at the time of death. In California, spouses who are separated or in the process of obtaining a divorce remain legally married until the issuance of a final decree of dissolution of the marriage. Similar rules apply to registered domestic partners, and putative spouses qualify as spouses for inheritance purposes. However, couples who merely live together generally do not qualify as spouses.

ca-fro-wills:methods.html:
A will may be revoked wholly or partially in three ways: (1) by subsequent instrument, (2) by physical act, or (3) by operation of law.

If a testator revokes a provision of a will, the new provision will not be given effect until the will is re-executed or republished. Holographic wills can be altered or revoked in whole or in part by holographic changes and without a new signature. Holographic wills can also be revoked by formal wills and vice versa.

ca-fro-wills:omitted-children.html:
An omitted (i.e., pretermitted) child is one who is born or adopted after the execution of a will. An omitted child receives a share equal to that which the child would have received if the decedent had died intestate. However, this rule does not apply if:

  • The omission was intentional
  • The testator had other children at the time the will was executed and left substantially all of his estate to the other parent of the omitted child or
  • The testator provided for the child outside of the will and intended this to be in lieu of a provision in the will.

ca-fro-wills:omitted-spouse-or-domestic-.html:
A spouse or domestic partner who is not mentioned in a will is entitled to an intestate share of the testator’s estate, unless:

  • The omission was intentional (and intent was apparent in the will)
  • The spouse or domestic partner was given property outside of the will in lieu of any disposition in the will
  • The spouse or domestic partner is a party to a valid contract waiving the right to share in the estate or
  • The spouse was a care custodian who married a dependent adult testator while providing services to that dependent adult, or if within 90 days after those services were last provided, and the decedent died less than six months after the marriage commenced (unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the marriage was not the product of fraud or undue influence).

If none of the above exceptions apply, then the omitted spouse or domestic partner takes an intestate share from the decedent’s estate but cannot receive more than one-half of the decedent’s separate property.

ca-fro-wills:operation-of-law.html: Divorce or the dissolution of a domestic partnership revokes all will provisions in favor of the former spouse or domestic partner, unless it can be shown that the testator intended for the will to survive. Mere separation does not affect the rights of the spouse or domestic partner.

ca-fro-wills:physical-act-with-intent-to.t: Physical Act With Intent to Revoke
ca-fro-wills:physical-act-with-intent-to.html:
A will may be revoked by burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying a material portion of the will with the intent to revoke it. Some defacement of language is required (e.g., crossing out or writing “null and void” over material text will suffice). A third party can revoke on behalf of the testator as long as the revocation is at the testator’s direction and in the testator’s presence.

If a will once known to exist cannot be found at the testator’s death, then there is a rebuttable presumption of revocation by physical act. The burden is on the proponent of the existence of a will to prove the will’s existence by clear and convincing evidence.

Duplicate originals of a will can be admitted to probate, but copies of a will may only be used as proof of testamentary intent for a lost or missing will.

ca-fro-wills:plain-meaning-rule.html:
A court will interpret a will using only the four corners of the document. The use of extrinsic evidence is typically prohibited. However, extrinsic evidence is admissible to resolve any ambiguities, equivocations, and personal usage.

An unambiguous will may be reformed if clear and convincing evidence (1) establishes that the will contains a mistake in the expression of the testator’s intent at the time the will was drafted and (2) establishes the testator’s specific intent at that time. Extrinsic evidence is admissible to establish the mistake and the testator’s actual intent despite the fact that the will itself is unambiguous under the “four corners” rule.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ INCAPACITY, FRAUD, AND UNDUE INFLUENCE
ca-fro-wills:presumptions.html:
A presumption of undue influence arises when the beneficiary:

  • Stands in a confidential relationship (e.g., attorney, physician, clergy member) to the testator
  • Actively participated in executing the will and
  • The gift to the beneficiary is unnatural.

When a presumption of undue influence arises, the burden shifts to the beneficiary to show by clear and convincing evidence that influence was not exercised.

California law holds invalid any will provision making a gift to any of the following:

  • The drafter of the will
  • A person who transcribed the will or caused it to be transcribed and who was in a fiduciary relationship with the testator at the time it was transcribed
  • Any care custodian of a dependent adult
  • Anyone related to, living with, or employed by the drafter, transcriber, or care custodian or
  • A partner or employee of the law firm of the drafter or transcriber.

This California statutory presumption is not applicable to the testator’s relatives or those living with the testator or to a will reviewed by an independent attorney.

ca-fro-wills:qualifications.html:
only one common parent (i.e., half-bloods). A child of a marriage or domestic partnership is presumed to be the natural child of the parties to the marriage or domestic partnership.

Adoption severs the child’s relationship with the child’s natural parents. But for stepparent adoption, the child’s relationship with the natural parent married to the stepparent is not severed.

A posthumously born child is a child conceived before, but born after, death of the mother’s husband.

  • If the child is born within 300 days of the husband’s death, then there is a rebuttable presumption that the child is the husband’s and will inherit as if she was born before the husband died.
  • If the child is born more than 300 days after the husband’s death, then the child will have to prove parentage to inherit from the husband.

A similar rule applies in California when a child is born out of wedlock. A child born less than 300 days after the termination of a marriage by death or divorce is presumed to be the child of both parties to the marriage. A presumption of paternity also arises if:

  • The father and mother attempted to marry before the child’s birth and the child was born within 300 days of the failed marriage
  • The father and mother attempted to marry or married after the child’s birth and (1) the father’s name is on the birth certificate or (2) the father promises or is ordered to pay child support or
  • The father holds the child out as his own.

ca-fro-wills:revival.html:
Revocation of a will or codicil that revokes another will revives the original will if there is proof that the testator intended to revive the original will. Extrinsic evidence is more likely to be admissible if the revocation was done by physical act.

A testator who revokes a will or a portion of a will under a mistaken belief of fact—and who would not have revoked but for such a belief—has not effectively revoked his will. This doctrine of dependent relative revocation (DRR) allows the original will or portion of the will to be revived.

Exam tip

If you see an otherwise valid revocation based upon a mistake (whether of fact or law), begin your analysis by stating the DRR rule. DRR should only be applied if doing so honors the testator’s true intent. If a gift is increased in a new will, apply DRR to avoid a forfeiture. If recognizing the revocation and not reviving the will reflects the testator’s true intent (e.g., the gift is decreased in the new will), do not apply DRR.

ca-fro-wills:revocation-of-codicils.html: Revocation of a will revokes all codicils, but revocation of a codicil does not revoke a will. Instead, revocation of a codicil revives a will.

ca-fro-wills:separate-property.html:
The distribution of separate property is more complicated. The surviving spouse’s share of the intestate decedent’s separate property depends on who else survives the decedent.

Exam tip

Community property = property acquired during marriage and jointly owned by the parties.

Quasi-community property = property acquired by a married couple while living outside California that would have been characterized as community property had they been living in California when it was acquired.

Separate property = property that a spouse owns or claims before marriage or acquires by gift, devise, or descent during marriage.

ca-fro-wills:signed-writing.html:
The entire will must be in writing and signed by the testator (or some other person in the testator’s presence and at this discretion). The testator must be at least 18 years of age and of sound mind, and the signature must indicate the testator’s desire to sign (e.g., even “X” is permissible). In California, the signature may appear on any part of the will.

Exam tip

California does NOT recognize oral (nuncupative) wills.

ca-fro-wills:subsequent-instrument.html:
A testator can revoke a will (formal or holographic) by executing a later will or codicil (formal or holographic) that partly or completely revokes the prior will. The revocation can be express or implied from inconsistent terms in the subsequent instrument, but oral revocations are invalid. If there are any inconsistencies between the former and later instruments, then the later instrument controls.

Exam tip

Know how to distinguish a codicil to an existing will from a new will. If the subsequent document has a residuary clause, then it is likely a new will. If the subsequent document does not have a residuary clause but the first document does, then the subsequent document is likely a codicil.

ca-fro-wills:survival-requirements.html:
An heir must survive the decedent to take under the law of intestacy.

  • Common law – an heir’s survival must be proven by a preponderance of the evidence.
  • Uniform Simultaneous Death Act (USDA) – requires proof by clear and convincing evidence that an heir (or will beneficiary) survived the decedent by 120 hours to take by intestacy or under the will (unless the will provides otherwise). If there is insufficient evidence of the order of death of two individuals, then the property of each individual passes as though he had survived the other individual.
  • California – the 120-hour rule applies only to intestate succession. If spouses or domestic partners die simultaneously, then one-half of the community property is included in the estate of one and the other half is included in the estate of the other.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ INCAPACITY, FRAUD, AND UNDUE INFLUENCE
ca-fro-wills:testamentary-capacity.html: To execute or revoke a will in California, the testator must be at least 18 years old and possess a sound mind at the time of execution or revocation.

ca-fro-wills:testamentary-intent.html: The testator must understand that he is executing a will and must intend for it to have testamentary effect. The testator must also generally know and approve of the will’s contents.

crumb: CHAPTER VI ▪ INCAPACITY, FRAUD, AND UNDUE INFLUENCE
ca-fro-wills:undue-influence.html:
Undue influence is the excessive persuasion that causes another to act or refrain from acting by overcoming that person’s free will, resulting in inequity. The court considers the following factors when determining whether a person has exerted undue influence:

  • The victim’s vulnerability
  • The influencer’s apparent authority
  • The Influencer’s actions or tactics and
  • The equity of the result

ca-fro-wills:will-substitutes.html:
A will substitute is a method of transferring a decedent’s property outside of probate. Will substitutes include the following:

  • Revocable trusts: When a trustee holds legal title and a beneficiary holds equitable title to inter vivos trust property, there is no need for title to be transferred upon the death of the creator.
  • Pour-over wills: This refers to a devise of the testator’s property to a trust created during the testator’s life
  • Bank accounts and securities registered: Amounts on deposit in a bank account may be transferred at death by means of a joint account designation. And the owner of a security may register it in “beneficiary form” so that the security is transferred to the designated beneficiary upon the owner’s death.
  • Payable-on-death clauses: This refers to an agreement with a bank, employer, or other entity to distribute property held under a contract at the decedent’s death to a named beneficiary
  • Life insurance: Policy proceeds are not part of the decedent’s estate, unless they are payable to the estate as a beneficiary.
  • Deeds: A nonprobate transfer of property upon the grantor’s death is valid if the deed is delivered to a grantee or escrow agent during the grantor’s lifetime with the present intent to transfer a property interest to the grantee (e.g., a remainder interest).
  • Joint tenancies: Jointly held property with survivorship rights (e.g., joint tenancy with the right of survivorship, tenancy by the entirety) can automatically pass, upon the death of a joint tenant, to the surviving joint tenants.

ca-fro-wills:witnesses.html:
A will must also be signed in the joint presence of and attested to by two witnesses during the testator’s lifetime. If the testator does not sign in the presence of witnesses, then the testator must expressly or impliedly acknowledge his signature to the witnesses before they sign.

Under the conscious-presence test (adopted by California and a majority of jurisdictions), the testator and the witnesses must be aware of each other’s presence and of what each is doing. However, they are not required to watch each other sign.

Witnesses must be of sufficient mental capacity and maturity to comprehend the value of their act of witnessing a will, but there is no minimum age requirement. In California and most states, an interested witness is competent. But unless there were two other disinterested witnesses, a rebuttable presumption arises that the devise to the interested witness is a result of duress, menace, fraud, or undue influence. If the presumption is not rebutted, then the interested witness may not take more than he would have received had the decedent died intestate.

crumb: CHAPTER XIV ▪ PRIVILEGES AND IMMUNITIES CLAUSES
mbe-fro-conlaw:article-iv.html:
The Article IV Privileges and Immunities Clause (i.e., Comity Clause) prohibits a state from discriminating against citizens of another state with respect to fundamental rights or essential activities (e.g., pursuit of employment, transfer of property, access to state courts).

However, discrimination against out-of-state citizens may be valid if the state can show:

  • A substantial reason for the difference in treatment and
  • A substantial relationship between the discrimination and the state’s objective.

The market-participant exception that applies to the Dormant Commerce Clause does not apply to the Comity Clause.

Exam tip

The Privileges and Immunities Clause of Article IV and the Commerce Clause tend to mutually support each other, so consider both when analyzing a bar exam question.

mbe-fro-conlaw:chapter-x-state-action.html:
The Constitution generally protects against wrongful conduct by the government—not private parties. State action, which encompasses action by federal and local governments, is therefore a prerequisite to triggering constitutional protections.

State action occurs when a private person carries on a traditional government function, which is an activity that is traditionally performed exclusively by the state (e.g., running primary elections, running a company town).

State action may exist if there are sufficient mutual contacts between the conduct of a private entity and the government such that the government is pervasively entwined with the entity and:

  • Constitutional standards should apply to the private entity
  • A mutual benefit results (e.g., joint venture) or
  • The state creates a private entity by special law to further governmental objectives and retains permanent control of the entity.

Mere licensing or regulation of a private party is not state action. The state must affirmatively facilitate, encourage, or authorize the activity.

mbe-fro-conlaw:chapter-xi-procedural-due.html:
The Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment applies to the federal government, while the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment applies to states and local governments. Procedural due process ensures that the necessary procedures are followed before persons (including corporations) can be deprived of life, liberty, or property. Remember that procedural due process is only required when the government acts intentionally—not merely negligently.

Procedural Due Process Rights

Right

Applicability

Examples

Life

The government deprives a person of life

  • Death penalty

Liberty

The government significantly restrains a person’s physical freedom, fundamental rights, or freedom of choice

  • Incarceration
  • Commitment to a mental institution
  • Loss of parental rights

Property

The government deprives a benefit from a person with a legitimate claim of entitlement by virtue of a statute, an employment contract, or custom

  • Loss of government-issued licenses
  • Loss of welfare or disability benefits
  • Loss of public (not at-will) employment

The court will determine the amount of process due by weighing:

  • The individual interest affected
  • The risk of erroneous deprivation and value of additional safeguards and
  • The government’s interest, including the burden/cost of additional process.

The individual whose interest is being deprived is generally entitled to (1) notice of the government’s action by an unbiased decision-maker and (2) an opportunity to be heard.

Exam tip

If you see a question in which a person’s liberty or property interest is adversely affected by governmental action, ask whether the threatened interest is a protected one and, if so, what process is due.

mbe-fro-conlaw:chapter-xii-substantive-d.html:
Substantive due process is the principle that laws should be reasonable (not arbitrary) and protect individual freedom. It guarantees fundamental rights to all persons and acts as a “catchall” for rights not explicitly set forth in the Constitution.

Exam tip

Substantive due process issues arise when a fundamental right is being infringed for all persons. Equal protection issues arise when a right is being denied to a particular class of persons.

mbe-fro-conlaw:chapter-xiv-privileges-an.t: CHAPTER XIV ▪ PRIVILEGES AND IMMUNITIES CLAUSES
mbe-fro-conlaw:chapter-xiv-privileges-an.html: “Privileges and immunities” is addressed in Article IV as well as the Fourteenth Amendment. Under both constitutional provisions, the term “citizen” does not include corporations or noncitizens.

mbe-fro-conlaw:chapter-xv-takings-clause.html: The Takings Clause of the Fifth Amendment provides that private property cannot be taken for public use without just compensation. It serves as a check on the government’s eminent domain power.

crumb: CHAPTER III ▪ POWERS OF THE PRESIDENT
mbe-fro-conlaw:commander-in-chief.html: Only Congress can declare war. The president can take military action without a declaration of war in the case of actual hostilities against the United States, but Congress may limit these activities through its military appropriation (i.e., funding) power.

mbe-fro-conlaw:commerce.html:
Congress has the power to regulate the channels and instrumentalities of interstate commerce, as well as intrastate activities that substantially affect interstate commerce.

This includes intrastate activities that have no direct economic impact on interstate commerce, as long as there is a rational basis for concluding that the “total incidence” of the activity—in the aggregate— substantially affects interstate commerce. The practical effect of this aggregation principle is that economic activities are presumed to have a substantial economic effect.

However, Congress has limited power to regulate noneconomic intrastate activity. And though Congress’s commerce power is construed broadly, Congress cannot mandate that individuals not engaged in commercial activities engage in such activities.

crumb: CHAPTER XVIII ▪ FREEDOM OF EXPRESSION AND ASSOCIATION
mbe-fro-conlaw:commercial-speech.html:
Restrictions on commercial speech are subject to the following four-part test:

  • The commercial speech must concern lawful activity and be neither false nor misleading.
  • The asserted governmental interest must be substantial.
  • The asserted regulation must directly advance the asserted interest.
  • The regulation must be narrowly tailored to serve that interest (meaning a “reasonable fit” between the government’s ends and the means chosen to accomplish those ends).

crumb: CHAPTER XVIII ▪ FREEDOM OF EXPRESSION AND ASSOCIATION
mbe-fro-conlaw:criminal-penalty.html:
A statute that criminally punishes membership in an association violates the First and Fourteenth Amendments unless:

  • The group is actively engaged in unlawful activity or is engaging in advocacy that passes the “clear and present danger” test and
  • The defendant knows of and specifically intends to further the group’s illegal activity.

mbe-fro-conlaw:delegation-of-legislative.html: Congress may delegate some of its authority to the executive branch if Congress specifies an “intelligible principle” to guide the delegate. However, certain powers are nondelegable (e.g., impeachment power, power to declare war).

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ STATE REGULATION AND TAXATION OF COMMERCE
mbe-fro-conlaw:discrimination-against-ou.html:
A state or local government cannot enact a discriminatory regulation that protects local economic interests at the expense of out-of-state competitors except in the rare instance when:

  • The regulation is necessary to an important state interest and
  • No nondiscriminatory means are available to achieve that purpose.

crumb: CHAPTER III ▪ POWERS OF THE PRESIDENT
mbe-fro-conlaw:domestic-power.html:
The president has the power to enforce—but not make—the law. Accordingly, the president has significant power over administrative agencies that enforce the law. The president can issue executive orders telling these agencies what to do, and the president can set enforcement and rulemaking priorities.

However, this power is subject to control by congressional statute, which means that the president cannot tell agencies to enforce a law in a way that exceeds the statutory mandate.

mbe-fro-conlaw:establishment.html:
When a governmental program shows preference for one religion over another—or for religion over nonreligion—strict scrutiny applies.

To determine if a particular program violates the Establishment Clause, a court will consider the country’s “historical practices and understandings” about the role of religion in everyday life. For example, some long-standing practices (e.g., “In God We Trust” on money) are not establishments of religion due to the history and tradition of their use.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ STATE REGULATION AND TAXATION OF COMMERCE
mbe-fro-conlaw:exceptions.html:
• Market-participant exception – A state can favor local commerce or discriminate against nonresident commerce if the state is acting as a buyer or seller and not as a market regulator.
  • Traditional government function exception – State and local regulations can favor state and local government (not private) entities if the entities are performing a traditional government function (e.g., waste disposal).
  • Congressionally permitted discrimination – An otherwise impermissible state regulation is valid if it is unmistakably clear that Congress intended to permit it.

crumb: CHAPTER III ▪ POWERS OF THE PRESIDENT
mbe-fro-conlaw:executive-agreements.html: The president has the power to enter into executive agreements with foreign nations. No Senate approval is required. Note that conflicting federal statutes and treaties take precedence over executive agreements, but executive agreements take precedence over conflicting state laws.

crumb: CHAPTER VIII ▪ FEDERAL PREEMPTION OF STATE LAW
mbe-fro-conlaw:express-and-implied-preem.html:
When Federal Law Supersedes State and Municipal Laws

Express preemption

  • Constitution makes federal power exclusive or
  • Congress has enacted legislation explicitly prohibiting state regulation in the same area

Implied preemption

  • Field preemption – when Congress’s pervasive regulation shows an intent to occupy the entire field or
  • Conflict preemption – when it is nearly impossible to comply with both laws or when state/municipal law frustrates the federal law’s purpose

If federal law does not preempt state law, then a state is free to enact legislation regarding the same issue.

Exam tip

Federal law sets a floor below which a state law cannot go, but federal law does not set a ceiling above which state law cannot go.

crumb: CHAPTER XVIII ▪ FREEDOM OF EXPRESSION AND ASSOCIATION
mbe-fro-conlaw:expressive-conduct.html:
Expressive conduct (i.e., symbolic speech) is subject to a lesser degree of protection than traditional speech. A regulation of expressive conduct will be upheld if:

  • The regulation is within the government’s power to enact
  • It furthers an important governmental interest
  • The interest is unrelated to the suppression of ideas and
  • The burden on speech is no greater than necessary.

mbe-fro-conlaw:federal-regulation.html:
The federal government has virtually unlimited power to regulate the states so long as Congress exercises one of its enumerated powers.

Congress cannot “commandeer” state legislatures by commanding them to enact (or not enact) specific legislation. However, Congress can regulate states through conditional funding if the condition:

  • Is for the general welfare
  • Is unambiguous
  • Relates to a federal interest in particular national projects or programs
  • Does not induce states to act unconstitutionally and
  • Does not exceed the point at which “pressure turns into compulsion.”
