The defendant must intend to confine the plaintiff within a limited area, the defendant must cause the plaintiff’s confinement (or fail to release the plaintiff despite a duty to do so), and the plaintiff must be conscious of the confinement.

Confinement usually occurs within a limited area, but it also occurs when the plaintiff is forced to move in a highly restricted way. Methods of confinement include physical barriers, force, threats, legal authority, duress, and failure to provide a means of escape. The length of the confinement is unimportant, except that it can affect the amount of damages.

Proof of actual damages is typically not required, but a minority of jurisdictions require such proof when the plaintiff is unaware of the confinement. That is because a plaintiff can generally recover nominal damages and, in appropriate cases, punitive damages without showing actual damages.

mbe-fro-torts:foreseeability-of-the-plai.html:
• Cardozo (majority) view – The defendant is liable only to plaintiffs who are within the zone of foreseeable harm.
  • Andrews (minority) view – If the defendant can foresee harm to anyone due to his negligence, then a duty is owed to everyone harmed (foreseeable or not).

The defendant may be liable for negligently putting the rescued party or the rescuer in danger. If the rescuer’s efforts are unreasonable, comparative responsibility applies. However, under the firefighter’s rule, emergency professionals cannot recover for injuries resulting from the risks of the job.

mbe-fro-torts:generally.html:
Under the traditional (majority) approach for determining negligent conduct (i.e., breach of duty), courts compare the defendant’s conduct with what a reasonably prudent person would have done under the circumstances (objective standard).

The modern trend and the Third Restatement approach is to do a cost-benefit analysis. Courts consider the foreseeable likelihood that the defendant’s conduct would cause harm, the foreseeable severity of any resulting harm, and the defendant’s burden in avoiding the harm.

Exam tip

The traditional approach and the cost-benefit analysis are two ways of reaching the same result, so do not get hung up on their differences.

mbe-fro-torts:implied-warranties.html:
The implied warranty of merchantability warrants that the product is generally acceptable and reasonably fit for the ordinary purposes for which it is being sold.

The implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose warrants that a product is fit for a particular purpose, but only if the seller knows that purpose and the buyer relies on the seller’s skill or judgment in supplying the product.

If either warranty is breached, the buyer may recover damages for personal injury and property damage as well as purely economic loss. In addition, the Uniform Commercial Code includes three alternative privity provisions that allow a plaintiff who is not the buyer to recover:

  • Alternative A (majority rule) allows a member of the buyer’s family or household to recover for personal injury, but not for property damage or pure economic loss.
  • Alternative B allows anyone reasonably expected to use, consume, or be affected by the product to recover for personal injury.
  • Alternative C is similar to Alternative B but also allows recovery for property damage and economic loss.

crumb: CHAPTER I ▪ INTENTIONAL TORTS INVOLVING PERSONAL INJURY
mbe-fro-torts:intentional-infliction-of-.html:
Exam tip

IIED is often tested in comparison to negligent infliction of emotional distress (NIED).

The defendant, by extreme and outrageous conduct, must intentionally or recklessly cause the plaintiff severe emotional distress. Conduct is extreme and outrageous if it exceeds the possible limits of human decency, making it unacceptable in civilized society.

Transferred intent may apply if, instead of harming the intended person, the defendant’s extreme and outrageous conduct harms another. But transferred intent does not apply in other IIED contexts.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ DEFAMATION, INVASION OF PRIVACY, AND BUSINESS TORTS
mbe-fro-torts:intentional-interference-w.html:
This tort requires proof that:

  • A valid contract existed between the plaintiff and a third party (not the plaintiff and the defendant)
  • The defendant knew about the contract
  • The defendant intentionally interfered in a way that substantially exceeded fair competition and free expression, resulting in a breach and
  • The breach caused the plaintiff damages. Defendant liable Defendant not liable

However, the interference may be justified if:

  • The interference was motivated by health, safety, or morals
  • The contract was terminable at will or
  • The defendant is a business competitor.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ DEFAMATION, INVASION OF PRIVACY, AND BUSINESS TORTS
mbe-fro-torts:intentional-misrepresentat.html:
Intentional misrepresentation requires proof that:

  • The defendant knowingly or recklessly misrepresented a material fact, opinion, intention, or law with the intent to induce the plaintiff’s reliance and
  • The plaintiff justifiably relied on the defendant’s misrepresentation and suffered damages as a result.

The plaintiff must prove actual damages because nominal damages are not available. Consequential and punitive damages may be awarded. Damages are often limited to the economic loss suffered by the plaintiff. Damages for emotional distress are generally not awarded.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ DEFAMATION, INVASION OF PRIVACY, AND BUSINESS TORTS
mbe-fro-torts:interference-with-prospect.html: A defendant may be liable for intentionally interfering with a prospective business relationship between the plaintiff and a third party, even in the absence of an existing contract. But when there is no contract, more egregious conduct is required. The other elements remain the same, except that the defendant’s conduct must be independently tortious, in violation of federal or state law, or improper as decided by a balancing process. Without wrongful conduct, a business competitor will not be held liable for encouraging a third party to switch business.

mbe-fro-torts:intra-family-immunity.html: Interspousal immunity has been abolished in most states. Parent-child immunity is generally limited to core parenting activities.

mbe-fro-torts:liability-for-independent-.html:
A person who engages an independent contractor is generally not vicariously liable for the contractor’s torts, subject to the following exceptions:

  • The person retains control over the item that is the source of the tortious conduct.
  • The independent contractor is performing abnormally or inherently dangerous activities or nondelegable duties.
  • The independent contractor is acting with apparent agency.

A person may also be liable for his own negligence in selecting or supervising an independent contractor.

mbe-fro-torts:liability-of-the-governmen.t: Liability of the Government and Its Officers
mbe-fro-torts:liability-of-the-governmen.html:
• Federal government – Under the Federal Tort Claims Act, the U.S. government waives immunity—and therefore can be liable—in tort actions, with these exceptions: (1) enumerated torts, (2) discretionary functions, (3) government contractor in a products-liability case (unless the contractor failed to warn or conform to government specifications), and (4) traditional government activities. Note that immunity is waived for intentional torts by law enforcement officers.
  • State government – Most states have at least partially waived immunity, but states’ tort claims acts vary.
  • Municipalities – The liability of municipalities, other local governments, and their agencies is usually governed by state tort claims acts. Traditionally, immunity attached only to government functions.
  • Government officials – Immunity applies to the performance of discretionary functions entrusted to government officials by law, unless the official acts with malice or an improper purpose. There is no tort immunity for carrying out ministerial acts. Legislators performing their legislative functions, judges performing their judicial functions, and prosecutors are absolutely immune from personal liability.

Under the public-duty rule, there is no liability to any one citizen for the failure to fulfill a duty to the public at large unless that citizen has a special relationship with the municipality that creates a special duty.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ DEFAMATION, INVASION OF PRIVACY, AND BUSINESS TORTS
mbe-fro-torts:libel.html: Libel is defamation by words written, printed, or otherwise recorded in permanent form. This generally includes radio and television. A libel plaintiff may recover general damages for reputational harm. But in some jurisdictions, under the doctrine of libel per quod, the plaintiff must prove special damages or that the statement fits into a category of slander per se when extrinsic facts are needed to prove that the statement is defamatory.

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ DEFENSES TO INTENTIONAL TORTS INVOLVING PERSONAL INJURY
mbe-fro-torts:merchant-s-privilege.html: A seller of goods or services (i.e., a merchant) may use force against another to investigate a theft, recapture personal property, or facilitate arrest. The merchant must reasonably believe that the other has wrongfully taken merchandise or failed to pay. The force must be used on or immediately surrounding the merchant’s premises, in a reasonable manner, and for a reasonable time. Deadly force is not allowed.

mbe-fro-torts:negligence.html:
As in any other negligence action, a plaintiff filing a products-liability suit based on negligence must prove:

  • Duty – The duty of reasonable care that a commercial manufacturer, distributor, retailer, or seller owes to any foreseeable plaintiff
  • Breach – Failure to exercise reasonable care in the inspection or sale of a product
  • Causation – Requires actual and proximate causation
  • Damages – Personal injury or property damage (not pure economic loss)

The standard negligence defenses—contributory/comparative negligence and assumption of the risk—apply.

mbe-fro-torts:negligence-per-se.html:
Negligence per se has four elements:

  • A criminal or regulatory statute imposes a specific duty for the protection of others.
  • The defendant violates the statute by failing to perform that duty.
  • The plaintiff is in the class of people intended to be protected by the statute.
  • The harm is of the type that the statute was intended to protect against.

Once these elements are established, the defendant is liable for any injuries that were proximately caused by the violation of the statute. However, liability may be avoided if any of these defenses apply:

  • Compliance was impossible or more dangerous than violating the statute.
  • The violation was reasonable under the circumstances, or the defendant exercised reasonable care in attempting to comply with the statute.
  • The statutory requirements were confusing (e.g., vague or ambiguous).
  • Compliance with federal regulations preempts a common-law tort action.

mbe-fro-torts:negligent-infliction-of-em.html:
To recover under the zone-of-danger theory, the plaintiff must prove that she was in the zone of danger (i.e., feared for her safety) and that the threat of physical impact caused emotional distress. Under the majority rule, this emotional distress must be manifested by physical symptoms (e.g., nightmares, shock). But under the minority rule and the Third Restatement, physical symptoms are not required.

A plaintiff outside the zone of danger can still recover under a bystander theory if the plaintiff is closely related to the person injured by the defendant, was present at the scene, and personally observed or perceived the injury. Under the majority rule, physical symptoms of emotional distress are required.

No threat of physical impact or physical symptoms are required in special-relationship cases (e.g., a mortician mishandling a corpse, a common carrier mistakenly reporting the death of a relative).

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ DEFENSES TO INTENTIONAL TORTS INVOLVING PERSONAL INJURY
mbe-fro-torts:nondeadly-force.html:
The defendant must reasonably believe that:

  • The plaintiff is intentionally using, or about to use, unprivileged force on the defendant
  • The force that the defendant is using is proportionate and
  • The immediate use of force is the only way to prevent the plaintiff’s force or threat of force.

mbe-fro-torts:possessors-of-land.html:
Standards of Care for Possessors of Land

Type of Land Entrant

Traditional Approach (majority rule)

Third Restatement (minority rule)

Invitees

Inspect of unknown dangers, make safe or warn, and prevent harm from active operations

Exercise reasonable care under all circumstances

Licensees

Warn of concealed dangers that are known or should be obvious to the land possessor

Known or anticipated trespassers

Warn of concealed artificial dangers

Other trespassers

Refrain from willful, wanton, reckless, or intentional misconduct (e.g., traps)

  • Trespassing children – Under the attractive nuisance doctrine, land possessors are liable for injuries to trespassing children if:
    • An artificial condition poses an unreasonable risk of serious bodily injury to children
    • Children cannot discover or appreciate the danger
    • The utility of the condition is slight compared with the risk of injury and
    • The land possessor fails to exercise reasonable care.
  • Landlords and tenants – Landlords are liable for injuries occurring (1) in common areas, (2) as a result of hidden dangers about which they fail to warn the tenant, (3) on premises leased for public use, (4) as a result of a hazard caused by negligent repair, or (5) as a result of a hazard that the landlord had agreed to repair. Conversely, tenants are liable for injuries arising from dangerous conditions within their control.
  • Off-premises victims – Landowners generally owe no duty to someone off the premises who is harmed by a natural condition (except for trees in urban areas). But for artificial conditions, landowners owe a duty to prevent an unreasonable risk of harm.
  • Sellers of real property – Sellers of real property have a duty to disclose to buyers any concealed and unreasonably dangerous conditions known to the seller. The seller’s liability continues until the buyer has a reasonable opportunity to discover and remedy the defect.

crumb: CHAPTER III ▪ HARMS TO PERSONAL PROPERTY AND LAND
mbe-fro-torts:private-nuisance.html:
Private nuisance is a substantial and unreasonable interference with another’s use or enjoyment of land. The interference must be intentional, reckless, negligent, or the result of abnormally dangerous conduct.

  • Substantial interference – One that would be offensive, inconvenient, or annoying to an average reasonable person in the community.
  • Unreasonable interference – One that makes the land unavailable for ordinary use or enjoyment and (1) is a per se nuisance, (2) does not comport with customs/expectations, (3) results from a failure to use reasonable care, (4) causes physical damage to land/fixtures, or (5) is motivated by malice.

Anyone with possessory rights in the real property (or any member of that person’s household) is an appropriate plaintiff. Defenses include:

  • Regulatory compliance (incomplete defense) – Evidence of the defendant’s compliance is admissible but not determinative.
  • Coming to the nuisance – Evidence that the plaintiff came to the nuisance does not entitle the defendant to judgment as a matter of law, but the jury may consider such evidence.

mbe-fro-torts:proximate-cause-legal-caus.html:
Proximate cause is a legal limitation on actual cause, focusing on foreseeability. Superseding cause

  • Majority rule – Defendants are liable for reasonably foreseeable consequences, so plaintiffs can recover if they were foreseeable victims of the defendant’s conduct.
  • Minority rule/Andrews test – Defendants are liable Defendant's Plaintiff's for all direct consequences of their conduct, and plaintiffs can recover if their harm was within the scope of liability of the defendant’s conduct breach harm

Events occurring after the defendant’s breach may impact the chain of causation between the defendant’s breach and the plaintiff’s harm.

  • An intervening cause is any cause that occurs after the defendant’s tortious act.
  • An unforeseeable intervening cause is a superseding cause that breaks the chain of proximate causation.
Exam tip

Intervening negligence is always foreseeable and, therefore, is never a superseding cause that breaks the chain of causation. This is often tested in the context of medical malpractice.

The type of damages must be foreseeable, but under the eggshell-skull rule, the extent of the damages need not be. This means that a defendant may be liable for the full extent of a plaintiff’s injuries even if those injuries were worsened by the plaintiff’s preexisting condition.

crumb: CHAPTER III ▪ HARMS TO PERSONAL PROPERTY AND LAND
mbe-fro-torts:public-nuisance.html: Public nuisance is an unreasonable interference with a right common to the general public. Private citizens are appropriate plaintiffs if they have suffered harm that is different in kind from the general public. And the same defenses that can be asserted for private nuisance apply to public nuisance.

mbe-fro-torts:pure-economic-loss.html: Economic damages are only recoverable alongside related personal injury or property damage (i.e., no recovery for pure economic loss).

mbe-fro-torts:reasonably-prudent-person.html:
This objective standard takes physical characteristics (e.g., blindness) into account but not mental ones. A defendant with special skills or knowledge must apply them with reasonable attention and care.

A voluntarily intoxicated person is held to the same standard as a sober person.

A child is held to the standard of a reasonable child of similar age, intelligence, and experience. But a child engaged in a high-risk, adult activity is held to an adult standard. A child under the age of five generally cannot be negligent.

crumb: CHAPTER III ▪ HARMS TO PERSONAL PROPERTY AND LAND
mbe-fro-torts:remedies-for-nuisance.html: The usual remedy is damages, but injunctive relief may be available if the balance of equities supports it.

mbe-fro-torts:res-ipsa-loquitur.html:
Res ipsa loquitur is Latin for “the thing speaks for itself.” Under this doctrine, the trier of fact may infer the existence of a defendant’s negligent conduct in the absence of direct evidence of such negligence. This requires proof of each of the following:

  • The accident was of a kind that ordinarily does not occur in the absence of negligence.
  • The accident was caused by an agent/instrumentality within the exclusive control of the defendant.
    • Courts generously interpret this requirement.
    • Many courts ignore this requirement for products-liability cases if the manufacturer wrapped the package or it is clear that negligence occurred during production.
    • If several medical personnel had access to the plaintiff during surgery, some jurisdictions will presume that each defendant breached a duty of care unless each defendant rebuts.
  • The accident was not due to any action on the part of the plaintiff.
Exam tip

Res ipsa loquitur merely reminds the trier of fact that circumstantial evidence can be sufficient to infer negligence. Accordingly, this doctrine does not apply if there is direct evidence of the cause of the injury.

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ DEFENSES TO INTENTIONAL TORTS INVOLVING PERSONAL INJURY
mbe-fro-torts:self-defense.html:
A defendant claiming self-defense must reasonably believe that the force is necessary and proportionate to the unprivileged force that the plaintiff is intentionally inflicting or about to inflict. Although the defendant’s force must be for a defensive purpose, the defendant may have additional motives. Deadly

Harmful

Offensive A defendant who uses excessive force in self-defense remains liable for (1) any harm attributable to the excessive force or (2) all indivisible harm caused by the privileged force and the excessive force. Force used

And if the plaintiff withdraws, the defendant loses the privilege to use force in self-defense.

mbe-fro-torts:sharing-liability-among-mu.html:
Under the doctrine of joint and several liability (default rule on the MBE), each of two or more defendants who are found liable for a single and indivisible harm to the plaintiff is subject to liability to the plaintiff for the entire harm.

However, most jurisdictions instead recognized pure and several liability, under which each defendant is liable only for his proportionate share of the plaintiff’s damages.

Δ’s proportion of ’s recovery under joint and several liability (default rule) ’s recovery under pure fault several liability

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ DEFAMATION, INVASION OF PRIVACY, AND BUSINESS TORTS
mbe-fro-torts:slander.html:
Slander is defamation by spoken word, gesture, or any form other than libel. A slander plaintiff must prove:

  • Special damages – Someone heard the defamatory comments and acted adversely to the plaintiff (an economic loss is usually involved)
  • Slander per se – The defamatory comments accused the plaintiff of (1) committing a crime, (2) conduct reflecting poorly on the plaintiff’s fitness to conduct her business, trade, or profession, (3) having a loathsome disease. or (4) sexual misconduct.
Exam tip

If a statement is true but seems like defamation, consider whether it constitutes IIED or invasion of privacy.

Defamation Defenses

Truth

Truthful statements are not defamatory

Consent

  • Actual – the plaintiff agreed to the publication
  • Apparent – the defendant reasonable believed that the plaintiff agreed to the publication
  • Note that the defendant cannot exceed the scope of consent

Absolute privileges

Statements made:

  • By a participant in a judicial or legislative proceeding
  • During the performance of official duties by a legislator or executive governmental official
  • Between spouses concerning a third person or
  • In required publications by radio, television, or newspaper

Qualified (conditional) privileges

Statements made:

  • In the interest of the publisher (defendant), such as defending the publisher’s reputation
  • In the interest of the recipient of the statement or a third party or
  • Affecting an important public interest.

The plaintiff must prove that the privilege has been abused and therefore lost

mbe-fro-torts:strict-products-liability.html:
Strict products liability requires proof of three elements:

  • The product was defective (in manufacture, design, or failure to warn).
  • The defect existed when the product left the defendant’s control.
  • The defect caused the plaintiff’s injury during intended or reasonably foreseeable use.

The plaintiff need not be in privity of contract with the defendant. Anyone foreseeably injured or whose property is harmed may bring a strict products-liability action.

The defendant must be in the business of selling (or otherwise distributing) the kind of product that harmed the plaintiff. A “seller” includes the manufacturer of the product, its distributor, and its retail seller. Casual sellers, auctioneers, the plaintiff’s employer, and service providers are generally not strictly liable.

Exam tip

Strict products liability is one way that a manufacturer or supplier of a product can be held liable for a plaintiff’s injuries. Remember to also consider breach of warranty and negligence.

crumb: CHAPTER III ▪ HARMS TO PERSONAL PROPERTY AND LAND
mbe-fro-torts:trespass-to-chattels.html:
Trespass to chattels (i.e., tangible personal property) is an intentional interference with the plaintiff’s right of possession by dispossession or by use or intermeddling. Only the intent to do the interfering act is needed, and transferred intent applies. A defendant’s mistake of law or fact is not a defense.

The type of interference impacts the recoverable damages:

  • Dispossession cases – The plaintiff may recover for actual damages, loss of use, and nominal damages or
  • Use or intermeddling cases – The plaintiff may recover only when there are actual damages

Actual damages may include the chattel’s decrease in value or cost of repair.

crumb: CHAPTER III ▪ HARMS TO PERSONAL PROPERTY AND LAND
mbe-fro-torts:trespass-to-land.html:
Trespass to land requires the intent to enter the land or cause a physical invasion (e.g., throwing an object onto the land)—not the intent to commit a wrongful trespass. Transferred intent applies. And anyone in actual or constructive possession of the land is an appropriate plaintiff. No proof of actual damages is required because nominal damages may be awarded.

However, necessity is a defense to trespass to land (as well as trespass to chattels and conversion).

  • Private necessity (qualified privilege) – Allows a limited number of people to enter or remain on land to protect themselves or their property from serious harm. However, the defendant is still responsible for actual damages.
  • Public necessity (absolute privilege) –

Trespass

Private Necessity

Public Necessity

Nominal Damages

Actual Damages

Punitive Damages

Allows a trespass to protect a large number of people from public disaster (e.g., spreading of a fire). The defendant is not liable for damage if she acts reasonably. So even if there was no actual necessity, the defendant is not liable if she reasonably believed that there was.

mbe-fro-torts:vicarious-liability.html: Vicarious liability is a form of strict liability in which one person is liable for the tortious actions of another. It arises when a person has the right, ability, or duty to control the activities of another.

mbe-fro-torts:wild-animals.html: The owner or possessor of a wild animal is strictly liable for harm that arises from a dangerous propensity that (1) is characteristic of such an animal or (2) the owner or possessor has reason to know about. This strict liability extends to injuries caused by the plaintiff’s fearful reaction to seeing an unrestrained wild animal.

mbe-fro-torts:wrongful-death-actions-and.html:
In wrongful death actions, a decedent’s spouse, next of kin, or personal representative may bring suit to recover losses suffered as a result of a decedent’s death. This includes damages for loss of support, companionship, society, and affection (but not pain and suffering). Recovery is limited to whatever the deceased would have recovered had the deceased lived.

In survival actions, the representative of the decedent’s estate may pursue any claims that the decedent would have had at the time of death (including claims for pain and suffering). However, double recovery is not allowed if the jurisdiction recognizes both wrongful-death and survival actions.

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ LIABILITY OF THE PRINCIPAL AND THE AGENT TO THIRD PARTIES
mee-fro-agency:actual-authority.html:
Express actual authority can be created by (1) oral or written words, (2) clear, direct, and definite language or (3) specific detailed terms and instructions. The principal must give clear notice if the principal disagrees with the agent’s actions.

Common sources of implied actual authority include:

  • Custom: Absent contrary instructions, an agent has implied authority to act within accepted business customs or general trade usage within an industry.
  • Position: Placing an agent in a position of authority (e.g., vice president, treasurer) may manifest assent to the agent’s actions.
  • Acquiescence: Implied authority results from: 1. The principal’s acceptance of the agent’s acts or 2. The principal’s failure to object to the unauthorized actions of the agent that: a) Affirm the agent’s belief about the principal’s objectives and b) Support the agent’s perceived authority to act in the future.

Delegation: Generally, an agent is prohibited from delegating either express or implied authority to a third party without the principal’s express authorization.

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ LIABILITY OF THE PRINCIPAL AND THE AGENT TO THIRD PARTIES
mee-fro-agency:apparent-authority.html:
Reasonableness: Words or conduct that can cause a third party to reasonably believe that the agent has authority to act on the principal’s behalf include:

  • Past dealings between the principal and agent
  • Trade customs for similar transactions
  • Relevant industry standards
  • The principal’s written statements of authority

By appointing an agent to a specific position (e.g., vice president, general manager), the principal manifests to the public that the agent has the customary level of authority of that position.

mee-fro-agency:chapter-iii-rights-and-du.t: CHAPTER III ▪ RIGHTS AND DUTIES OF PARTIES TO AN AGENCY RELATIONSHIP
mee-fro-agency:chapter-iii-rights-and-du.html:
Rights and Duties of Principals

Principal’s Rights

Principal’s Duties

Principal’s Remedies for Breach

  • Control the agent
  • Notice and accounting
  • Duty to deal fairly and in good faith
  • Duty to compensate
  • Duty not to interfere
  • Duty to indemnify
  • Injunction
  • Contract damages
  • Tort damages
  • Rescission
  • Restitution
  • Accounting
  • Termination of agency relationship
  • Forfeiture of compensation
  • Disgorgement of profits

Rights and Duties of Agents

Agent’s Rights

Agent’s Duties

Agent’s Remedies for Breach

  • Compensation
  • Work without interference by principal
  • Indemnification
  • Reimbursement
  • Safe work environment
  • Duty of care
  • Duty of obedience
  • Duty to provide information
  • Duty to keep and render accounts
  • Duty of loyalty
  • Contract damages
  • Tort damages
Exam tip

When answering questions about an agent’s duties to the principal, include the key duties: Duty of care, duty of obedience, duty to provide information, and duty of loyalty.

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ LIABILITY OF THE PRINCIPAL AND THE AGENT TO THIRD PARTIES
mee-fro-agency:contract-liability.html:
An agent’s liability as a party to a contract depends on the terms and whether the principal was disclosed, partially disclosed, or undisclosed.

Principal

Third party’s notice

Is the agent a party to the contract?

Disclosed

Notice of the principal’s existence and identity

No*

Partially disclosed

Notice of the principal’s existence, but not the principal’s identity,

Yes*

Undisclosed

No notice of the principal’s existence

Yes

*unless the agent and the third party agree otherwise.

Exam tip

Disclosed and partially disclosed principals become parties to a contract only if the agent had authority to bind the principal or the principal ratified the contract.

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ LIABILITY OF THE PRINCIPAL AND THE AGENT TO THIRD PARTIES
mee-fro-agency:contractual-liability-of-.html:
A principal is bound to a contract entered into by an agent when the agent had actual authority, the agent had apparent authority, or the principal is estopped from denying the agent’s authority.

Type of Authority

Created by:

Actual

Express

Express manifestation of a principal’s intent that causes the agent to reasonably believe (objective standard) that the agent is doing what the principal wants (subjective standard)

Implied

What an agent reasonably believes to be necessary, usual, and proper to achieve the principal’s objectives

Apparent

Words or conduct that cause a third party to reasonably believe that the agent has authority to act on the principal’s behalf

Exam tip

When discerning between actual and apparent authority, look to the communication that caused someone to reasonably believe that the agent had authority.

  • When the principal communicates with the agent, the agent has actual authority.
  • When the principal communicates with a third party, the agent has apparent authority.

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ LIABILITY OF THE PRINCIPAL AND THE AGENT TO THIRD PARTIES
THE PRINCIPAL’S LIABILITY TO THIRD PARTIES IN TORT FOR THE AGENT’S CONDUCT
mee-fro-agency:direct-liability-to-third.html:
A principal is directly liable to a third party harmed by an agent’s conduct if:

  • The principal authorized the agent’s conduct (or intended its consequences) or ratified prior acts done on the principal’s behalf
  • The principal was negligent in selecting, training, supervising, or controlling the agent or
  • The principal delegated to the agent the performance of a nondelegable duty (e.g., inherently dangerous activities like using explosives).

mee-fro-agency:introduction.html:
Agency addresses the legal consequences of one person (the agent) acting on behalf of, and subject to

Exam tip

Agency principles often apply in essays testing partnerships and corporations.

Assent, Benefit, and Control (ABC): ABCs of agency relationships

  • Both parties manifest Assent to the agency relationship
  • The agent acts for the principal’s Benefit and
  • The agent’s actions are subject to the principal’s Control.
Exam tip

The parties do not always say, or even know, that they are in an agency relationship. Always list and analyze the elements to determine whether an agency relationship exists.

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ LIABILITY OF THE PRINCIPAL AND THE AGENT TO THIRD PARTIES
mee-fro-agency:ratification.html:
A principal can ratify an act performed by another person—regardless of whether that person was an actual agent—by affirming that the act was done on the principal’s behalf. Ratification has four requirements:

1. The principal must ratify the entire act or transaction.

2. The principal must have the legal capacity to ratify the transaction and the third party must have the capacity to engage in the transaction.

3. The principal’s ratification must be timely.

4. The principal must have knowledge of the material facts involved in the original act.

Exam tip

Remember that regardless of whether the principal is liable, the agent may also be liable.

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ LIABILITY OF THE PRINCIPAL AND THE AGENT TO THIRD PARTIES
THE PRINCIPAL’S LIABILITY TO THIRD PARTIES IN TORT FOR THE AGENT’S CONDUCT
mee-fro-agency:scope-of-employment.html:
Frolic and detour: An employee’s personal errand involving a significant deviation from performing work (frolic) is outside the scope of employment. But travel for a personal errand may be within the scope of employment if it is merely a de minimis deviation from an assigned path (detour).

Work-related travel: Commuting is not within the scope of employment, but travel to complete work at a job site or completing an errand for the employer may be within the scope.

Intentional torts: Intentional torts are generally outside the scope of employment. However, there are exceptions when:

  • The conduct is within the space and time limits of the employment
  • The employee was motivated to act for the employer’s benefit or
  • The act was the kind that the employee was hired to perform.

mee-fro-agency:types-of-agents.html:
Employee: An employee’s tasks are completed under the employer’s direction.

Independent contractor: Independent contractors are agents but are not subject to the principal’s control regarding the manner in which the agent performs.

Employee vs. Independent Contractor Factors

Employee (Agent)

Independent Contractor (Nonemployee Agent)

High level of employer control and direction

High level of independence

Paid on a structured pay period

Paid a fixed fee based on results

Works primarily for employer

Free to work for others

Employer provides tools

Provides own tools

mee-fro-agency:types-of-principals.html:
Almost any person or entity that has legal capacity can be a principal.

Individual: Principal status is established by a person’s intent to delegate an act and control the way in which another performs that act.

Employer: An employer is a principal who hires an employee to perform services and controls the manner in which the employee performs. The more control a principal exerts, the more likely an employer-employee relationship exists. Factors supporting an employer-employee relationship include:

  • The principal exercises significant control over the worker’s day-to-day activities
  • The principal supplies the tools of the trade
  • The principal pays the worker on a structured pay period
  • The worker’s skill is specialized
  • The principal directs the work to completion

Business: An entrepreneur, corporation, or partnership can be a principal.

Exam tip

Look for sole proprietors of a business as principals in agency and partnership questions.

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ LIABILITY OF THE PRINCIPAL AND THE AGENT TO THIRD PARTIES
THE PRINCIPAL’S LIABILITY TO THIRD PARTIES IN TORT FOR THE AGENT’S CONDUCT
mee-fro-agency:vicarious-liability-for-a.html:
Right to control Tort committed within

Under the doctrine of respondeat superior, a principal is vicariously liable to a third party harmed by an agent who is an employee and was acting within the scope of employment (i.e., while performing assigned work or engaging in a course of conduct subject to the employer’s control).

Exam tip

To hold an employer vicariously liable, you must establish that: 1. The principal had sufficient control over the agent’s conduct such that they had an employer-employee relationship and 2. The employee’s harmful conduct was committed while working within the scope of employment.

Independent contractors are not employees. Therefore, a principal is not vicariously liable for an independent contractor’s conduct unless one of the following exceptions applies:

  • The principal retained control over the source of the tortious conduct
  • The principal hired the independent contractor to perform nondelegable duties
  • The independent contractor had apparent authority
  • The principal was negligent in selecting, training, or supervising the independent contractor

mee-fro-corporations:articles-of-incorpo.html:
The articles of incorporation must be filed with the state and must include the corporation’s name, the number of shares it is authorized to issue, the name and address of its registered agent, and the name and address of each incorporator (i.e., a person who signs and files the articles with the state).

The articles may include a statement of the corporation’s legal purpose and may enumerate powers that the corporation possesses or limit its duration.

Corporate existence begins once the articles are filed, unless the articles establish a later date. Once all statutory requirements for incorporation have been satisfied, a “de jure” corporation is formed. This means that the corporation—rather than shareholders, directors, officers, and other employees—is liable for the corporation’s activities.

mee-fro-corporations:articles-of-organiz.html:
An LLC is created by filing articles of organization with the state. The articles must include the LLC’s name, mailing address, and, if the LLC has no members upon filing, a statement to that effect. The LLC does not come into existence until it has at least one member.

Some states apply doctrines of de facto corporation and corporation by estoppel if there was a good-faith effort to create an LLC.

mee-fro-corporations:chapter-v-board-of-.html:
The board of directors manages the corporation’s business and affairs (e.g., appointing and overseeing officers, making high-level corporate decisions). The board of directors can have as few as one director, but a director must be a natural person—not a corporation.

Directors are selected at the annual shareholders’ meeting and may receive compensation for their service. The term is typically one year, but a director may serve longer if the directors’ terms are staggered. Shareholders may remove a director with or without cause unless the articles provide otherwise, and a director can resign at any time with written notice to the board, its chair, or the corporation.

mee-fro-corporations:chapter-vi-officers.t: CHAPTER VI ▪ OFFICERS AND OTHER EMPLOYEES
mee-fro-corporations:chapter-vi-officers.html:
A corporation’s officers are typically composed of a president, secretary, and treasurer. These officers are elected by the board of directors but may be removed at any time, with or without cause.

An officer’s duties of care and loyalty—and the right to indemnification—are the same as a director’s (see above). And, as an agent of the corporation, an officer is not liable to third parties for the performance of her duties to the corporation. However, an officer may be liable if she acted in her personal capacity or engaged in purposeful tortious behavior.

Officer’s Authority

Express

An officer’s express authority is defined by the corporate bylaws or set by the board of directors

Implied

An officer has implied authority to perform tasks that are necessary to carry out the officer’s duties by virtue of her status or position, provided the matter is within the scope of ordinary business (i.e., not an “extraordinary” transaction)

Apparent

An officer has apparent authority if the corporation holds her out as having authority to bind the corporation to third parties

An employee can similarly act on behalf of the corporation to the extent of the employee’s authority and is usually protected as an agent from liability for actions undertaken in accordance with that authority.

mee-fro-corporations:chapter-vii-mergers.html:
• Merger: A merger is the combination of two or more corporations such that only one corporation survives. A merger requires board and shareholder approval by a majority vote at a meeting with a quorum (i.e., a majority of the shares entitled to vote) present for each corporation.
  • Asset acquisition: The approval procedure is the same as for mergers, except that only the transferor corporation’s board and shareholders can vote on the transaction. The transferor corporation remains liable for its debts.
  • Stock acquisition: A corporation can acquire stock in another corporation to secure control of that corporation without a merger by exchanging its own stock for that stock or paying cash or other property for the stock.

A shareholder who is entitled to vote on and objects to a merger/acquisition—or whose rights are materially and adversely affected by an amendment of the corporation’s articles—may force the corporation to buy his stock at a fair value as determined by an appraisal. To invoke dissenter’s rights, the shareholder must:

  • Send written notice to the corporation prior to the vote
  • Abstain or vote “no” at the meeting and
  • Make prompt written demand for fair market value after the action is approved.

mee-fro-corporations:chapter-x-limited-l.html:
A limited liability company (LLC) enjoys the pass-through tax advantage of a partnership and the limited liability of a corporation.

Exam tip

While LLCs have some unique terms and features, most issues can be analyzed under general corporate-law principles.

mee-fro-corporations:controlling-shareho.html:
A controlling shareholder is a shareholder (or a group of shareholders acting in concert) who holds a high enough percentage of ownership (> 50%) to enact changes at the highest level. The controlling shareholder has a fiduciary duty to minority shareholders if the controlling shareholder is:

  • Selling an interest to an outsider
  • Eliminating other shareholders from the corporation or
  • Receiving a distribution denied to the other shareholders.

This fiduciary duty includes:

  • The duty to disclose information that a reasonable person would consider important in deciding how to vote on a transaction and
  • The duty of fair dealing when purchasing a minority shareholder’s interest.

mee-fro-corporations:corporation-s-liabi.html: A corporation generally is not liable for pre-incorporation transactions entered into by a promoter, even if those transactions were entered into for the corporation’s benefit. However, a corporation is liable when it expressly or impliedly adopts a pre-incorporation contract by (1) accepting the benefits of the transaction or (2) expressly accepting liability for the debt.

mee-fro-corporations:defective-incorpora.html:
Lack of good faith – A person who conducted business as a corporation without attempting to comply with incorporation requirements is personally liable for the nonexistent corporation’s obligations.

Good-faith effort – A person who unsuccessfully attempted to comply with incorporation requirements may escape personal liability in two ways:

  • De facto corporation – The owner must have (1) made a good-faith effort to comply with the incorporation requirements and (2) operated the corporation without actual knowledge of the defect.
  • Corporation by estoppel (limited to contractual agreements) – A person who dealt with an entity as though it was a corporation will be estopped from denying its existence and seeking personal liability.
Exam tip

When a question involves a third party who is suing a shareholder for breach of contract, consider the following issues:

  • Promoter liability for pre-incorporation conduct: The promoter is liable unless there is a novation
  • Personal liability of the shareholder:
    • De jure formation – The shareholder is not liable for debts of a corporation if it was validly formed unless the corporate veil is pierced (look for fraud, undercapitalization, or alter ego)
    • Defective corporation – Analyze de facto formation and/or corporation by estoppel

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ STOCK AND OTHER CORPORATE SECURITIES
mee-fro-corporations:distributions.html:
The board of directors is authorized to make distributions, usually in the form of cash dividend payments. However, a corporation cannot make a distribution if the corporation is insolvent or the distribution would make the corporation insolvent. A director who votes for or agrees to an unlawful distribution—in violation of the duties of care and loyalty—is personally liable to the corporation for any distribution amount over the lawful amount.

A shareholder has a primary and personal right to compel a dividend that can be enforced by a direct action against the corporation. To prevail, the shareholder must prove that:

  • Funds to pay a dividend were legally available and
  • The directors acted in bad faith in refusing to pay.

mee-fro-corporations:duties.html: While carrying out duties owed to the corporation, a director is required to act in good faith and in a manner the director reasonably believes to be in the best interests of the corporation. These duties include the duty of care and the duty of loyalty.

mee-fro-corporations:duties-2.html:
Members of a member-managed LLC and managers of a manager-managed LLC owe duties of loyalty and care to the LLC and its members.

  • Duty of loyalty: This includes the duty to account to the LLC for any benefit derived by the member related to the LLC’s business, avoid dealing with the LLC on behalf of one having an adverse interest, and avoid competing with the LLC.
  • Duty of care: This duty is subject to the business judgment rule; there is no liability for simple negligence.
  • Fiduciary waiver: These waivers are recognized in LLCs. Members can agree to specific types of activities that do not violate the duty of loyalty, if the agreement is not manifestly unreasonable.

mee-fro-corporations:duty-of-care.html:
A director’s duty of care is the obligation to act with the care that a person in a like position would reasonably believe appropriate under similar circumstances (i.e., a prudent-person standard).

Directors are required to use any additional knowledge and special skills they have when deciding how to act. They can also rely on information and opinions of officers, employees, committees, and outside experts (e.g., attorneys, accountants) if the director reasonably believes them to be reliable and competent.

A director’s exercise of managerial powers is subject to the business judgment rule, which provides a rebuttable presumption that a director reasonably believed that his actions were in the best interests of the corporation. To overcome this rule, it must be established that the director:

  • Did not act in good faith
  • Was not informed to the extent that he reasonably believed was necessary
  • Had a material interest in the challenged conduct and was not objective
  • Failed to devote attention to the corporation’s affairs
  • Failed to timely investigate matters of material concern or
  • Received a financial benefit to which he was not entitled.

The presumption of good faith can be overcome in cases involving fraud, dereliction of duty, condoning illegal conduct, or a conflict of interest.

Exam tip

In the absence of fraud, illegality, or self-dealing, a court will not disturb an honest business judgment.

mee-fro-corporations:duty-of-loyalty.html:
The duty of loyalty is the obligation to act in a manner that the director reasonably believes is in the best interests of the corporation. This duty is typically breached when a director (1) engages in a conflict-of-interest transaction with the corporation, (2) usurps a corporate opportunity, or (3) competes with the corporation.

A director violates the duty of loyalty by engaging in a conflict-of-interest transaction with the corporation (i.e., self-dealing) unless that transaction is protected under a safe-harbor rule—the business judgment rule does not apply. A conflict-of-interest transaction is one that:

  • Would normally require approval of the board of directors and
  • Is of such financial significance to the director that it would reasonably be expected to influence his vote on the transaction (i.e., interest involved must be financial and material).

However, there are three safe harbors that can protect a conflict-of-interest transaction:

offering the opportunity to the corporation. Courts have applied the following tests to determine whether the opportunity is one that must first be offered to the corporation:

  • Interest-or-expectancy test – Whether the corporation has an existing interest, or an expectancy arising from an existing right, in the opportunity
  • Line-of-business test – Whether the opportunity is within the corporation’s current or prospective line of business

Courts will also look at (1) the relationships between the third party offering the opportunity, the director, and the corporation and (2) how and when the director learned about the opportunity.

Lastly, a director violates the duty of loyalty by engaging in a business venture that competes with the corporation.

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ STOCK AND OTHER CORPORATE SECURITIES
mee-fro-corporations:federal-causes-of-a.html:
The fraudulent purchase or sale of any stock or other security (e.g., bonds, stock options, warrants) can give rise to a Rule 10b-5 action. For a private person to prevail in a Rule 10b-5 action, the following requirements must be met:

  • The plaintiff purchased or sold a security
  • The transaction involved the use of interstate commerce
  • The defendant engaged in fraudulent or deceptive conduct
  • The conduct related to material information
  • The defendant acted with scienter (i.e., intent or recklessness)
  • The plaintiff justifiably relied on the defendant’s fraudulent conduct and
  • The plaintiff suffered harm because of that conduct.

Section 16(b) actions can be used to force a corporate insider to return short-swing profits to applicable corporations.

  • Corporate insiders: Directors, officers, and shareholders with > 10% of the corporation’s stock.
  • Short-swing profits: Profits made when stock is bought and sold during a six-month period.
  • Applicable corporations: Corporations that have (1) securities traded on a national securities exchange or (2) > $10 million in assets and > 500 shareholders.

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ STOCK AND OTHER CORPORATE SECURITIES
mee-fro-corporations:issuance-of-stock.html:
Types of Stock

Common stock

Stock that grants voting rights on matters of corporate governance and represents an ownership interest in the corporation

Preferred stock

Stock that grants preference over other stock with respect to distributions

Exam tip

If the fact pattern includes preferred stock and the amount of the dividend being distributed, keep close track of where that money is going.

The issuance of stock must be authorized by the corporation’s board of directors (or the shareholders, if the articles of incorporation so provide). And rights, options, and warrants to buy the corporation’s stock may be issued by the board of directors.

Consideration that corporations may accept as payment for stock include money, tangible or intangible property, and services rendered to the corporation. So long as the board of directors determines that the consideration is adequate, the stock is deemed fully paid and is not subject to challenge.

Prior to incorporation, persons may subscribe to purchase stock when the corporation comes into existence. These pre-incorporation subscriptions are irrevocable for six months from the date of the subscription (unless all subscribers agree to a revocation).

Shareholders may have the preemptive right to purchase newly issued shares of stock, which allows shareholders to maintain their proportional ownership interests in the corporation. However, preemptive rights exist only if the articles so provide, and a written waiver of these rights is irrevocable.

mee-fro-corporations:liability.html: Shareholders are generally subject to limited liability for corporate conduct—they are only at risk to the extent of their investment. However, the shield that protects a shareholder from full personal liability for corporate conduct can be removed in limited circumstances. This is known as piercing the corporate veil.

mee-fro-corporations:management.html: The management of an LLC can either be direct (by members) or centralized (by one or more managers who need not be members). In a member-managed LLC, members have broad authority to bind the LLC. In contrast, in a manager-managed LLC, the managers have authority to bind the LLC.

mee-fro-corporations:meeting-requirement.html:
A corporation must hold a shareholders’ meeting each year. The primary purpose of this annual meeting is to elect directors, but any business may be addressed.

A corporation may also hold special meetings, the purpose of which must be specified in the notice of the meeting. A special meeting may be called by the board of directors or by shareholders who own at least 10% of the voting shares.

Voting shareholders must be notified of the time, date, and place of a meeting (annual or special) in a timely manner (i.e., 10 to 60 days before the meeting). Shareholders may waive such notice in writing or by attending the meeting. Shareholders can also take any action that could have been taken at a meeting by unanimous written consent.

Note that failure to hold shareholder meetings does not affect the corporation’s existence or invalidate its business.

Exam tip

The board of directors may also hold regular or special meetings, but the meeting requirements for shareholder meetings do not apply to board meetings.

mee-fro-corporations:piercing-the-corpor.html:
Piercing the corporate veil is an equitable response to a particular situation. Therefore, courts consider the “totality of circumstances” when determining whether the corporation is being used as the shareholder’s alter ego or a façade for a dominant shareholder’s personal dealings. Factors cutting in favor of piercing the corporate veil include:

  • Undercapitalization of the corporation at the time of formation
  • Disregard of corporate formalities
  • Intermingling of corporate and personal assets
  • Use of corporate assets for personal purposes
  • Self-dealing with the corporation
  • Siphoning of corporate funds or stripping of corporate assets
  • Use of the corporate form to avoid existing statutory requirements or other legal obligations
  • Misleading, fraudulent, or otherwise wrongful dealings with a corporate creditor

mee-fro-corporations:promoters.html:
incorporation activities undertaken on the corporation’s behalf and reimbursement for related expenses. However, a promoter cannot compel the corporation to pay because the acts were not undertaken at the corporation’s direction.

Additionally, promoters stand in a fiduciary relationship with the pre-incorporated corporation, so promoters can be liable to the corporation for violating fiduciary duties.

Promoters are also personally liable to third parties for knowingly acting on a corporation’s behalf before incorporation and remain jointly and severally liable for those actions after incorporation—unless a subsequent novation releases the promoter from liability.

mee-fro-corporations:suits-by-shareholde.html:
There are two types of actions that a shareholder may bring against the corporation in which the shareholder owns stock:

  • Direct action – (1) an action to enforce shareholder rights or (2) a non-shareholder action, the recovery from which benefits the indirect shareholder
  • Derivative action – an action in which the shareholder sues on behalf of the corporation for harm suffered by the corporation

To have standing to bring a derivative action, the shareholder must:

  • Have been a shareholder at the time of the wrong, be a shareholder when the action is filed, and remain a shareholder during the litigation (i.e., the “contemporaneous ownership” rule)
  • Fairly and adequately represent the interests of the corporation and
  • File a written demand on the board of directors at least 90 days before the action commences.

The plaintiff-shareholder can seek reimbursement for reasonable litigation expenses, including attorney’s fees, if the derivative action produces a substantial benefit to the corporation. However, the board can seek dismissal of the derivative action if a majority of the board’s qualified directors decide in good faith, after reasonable inquiry, that the action is not in the corporation’s best interests.

Exam tip

When a question involves a shareholder suing the board of directors for wrongful conduct, consider the following issues:

  • Shareholder direct suit (to vindicate a shareholder right)
  • Shareholder derivative suit (on behalf of the corporation based on breach of fiduciary duties)
    • Duty of care and the “business judgment rule” defense
    • Duty of loyalty and the defense of ratification or fairness
    • Remedies for breach

Remember that a creditor of the corporation cannot bring a derivative action.

mee-fro-corporations:ultra-vires-actions.html:
Exam tip

An ultra vires act occurs when a corporation that has stated a narrow business purpose in its articles of incorporation subsequently engages in activities outside that stated purpose.

A corporation’s ultra vires action can be challenged in the following ways:

  • A shareholder can file suit to enjoin the action
  • The corporation can act against a director, officer, or employee who engaged in the action or
  • The state can initiate a proceeding against the corporation.

crumb: CHAPTER VIII ▪ TERMINATION OF CORPORATE STATUS
mee-fro-corporations:voluntary-dissoluti.html:
Prior to the issuance of stock, a corporation may voluntarily dissolve by a majority vote of the incorporators or initial directors. After the issuance of stock, the board of directors must adopt a proposal for the dissolution of the corporation and a majority of the shareholders must approve.

A dissolved corporation can continue to exist as a corporation to (1) collect assets, (2) dispose of property that will not be distributed to shareholders, (3) discharge liabilities, and (4) distribute property among the shareholders according to their interests. Directors must distribute corporate assets in the following order: to creditors of the corporation, then to shareholders of stock with preferences in liquidation, then to shareholders of other stock.

mee-fro-corporations:voting-eligibility.html:
Typically, each share of stock is entitled to one vote. Ownership of stock at the close of business on the record date (typically set by the board of directors) entitles a shareholder to vote the stock—even if that shareholder subsequently transfers the stock to another.

A shareholder may vote in person or by proxy. However, a proxy vote must be executed in a signed writing and delivered to the corporation or its agent.

Note that a corporation generally cannot vote its own stock.

mee-fro-corporations:voting-requirements.html:
For the board of directors’ acts at a meeting to be valid, a quorum of directors must be present at the meeting. A quorum requires that a majority of directors be present (unless the bylaws provide otherwise).

The assent of a majority of the directors present when the vote takes place is typically necessary for board approval. Unlike shareholders, directors cannot vote by proxy. And agreements between directors as to how to vote (i.e., pooling agreements) are generally unenforceable.

crumb: CHAPTER I ▪ PARTNERSHIP FORMATION AND RELATIONSHIPS
mee-fro-partnerships:fiduciary-duties.html:
A partner is an agent of the partnership and can commit it to binding contracts with third parties. But in so doing, a partner must comply with its duties to the partnership and the other partners. A partner owes the partnership and its partners the duties of loyalty and care and has an obligation of good faith and fair dealing in observing those duties.

Overview of a Partner’s Duties

Duty

Requirements

Modification

Duty of loyalty

A partner must not:

  • Compete with partnership business
  • Advance an interest adverse to the partnership or
  • Usurp a partnership opportunity
  • Cannot eliminate this duty
  • Can identify activities as not violating this duty
  • Can provide a safe-harbor defense (i.e., approval by other partners after full disclosure of material facts)

Duty of care

A partner must refrain from:

  • Grossly negligent or reckless conduct
  • Intentional misconduct or
  • Knowingly violating the law
  • Cannot unreasonably reduce this duty

Formation Dissociation Winding Up Dissolution

Nondissociating partner

Duties apply

Duties apply

Duties apply

Duty not to compete ends

Dissociating partner

Partner winding up

crumb: CHAPTER I ▪ PARTNERSHIP FORMATION AND RELATIONSHIPS
mee-fro-partnerships:formation-of-a-part.html: A partnership is an association of two or more persons (or entities) to carry on a for-profit business as co-owners.

mee-fro-partnerships:limited-liability-p.html: A partner in a limited liability partnership (LLP) is not personally liable for LLP obligations but is personally liable for her own misconduct. To enjoy LLP status, the partnership must file a statement with the state, and the name must end with “RLLP” (Registered Limited Liability Partnership), “LLP,” or words describing such status. Revocation of the state’s qualification of LLP status transforms the LLP into a simple partnership but does not trigger dissolution.

crumb: CHAPTER I ▪ PARTNERSHIP FORMATION AND RELATIONSHIPS
mee-fro-partnerships:management-rights.html:
Each partner has equal rights in the management and conduct of the partnership. However, different types of decisions have different approval requirements.

Partner-Approval Requirements

Type of Decision

Minimum Approval Required

Usual and customary matters

Partner acting individually*

Ordinary partnership decisions

Majority of partners

Matters outside the ordinary course of the partnership’s business

All partners

Amendments to the partnership agreement

All partners

Admission of a new partner

All partners

*Unless the partner has reason to know that other partners might disagree or that consultation with fellow partners is appropriate.

crumb: CHAPTER I ▪ PARTNERSHIP FORMATION AND RELATIONSHIPS
mee-fro-partnerships:nature-of-partnersh.html: A partnership is a separate legal entity from each of the partners. It may hold property, and it can sue and be sued. Its partners are personally liable for the partnership’s obligations.

crumb: CHAPTER I ▪ PARTNERSHIP FORMATION AND RELATIONSHIPS
mee-fro-partnerships:partnership-profits.html:
If there is no partnership agreement or the agreement is silent regarding partnership profits and losses, each partner is entitled to an equal share of the profits and losses. If the agreement specifies only the division of profits, then losses are shared in the same manner as profits.

Each partner has a partnership account, which consists of contributions to the partnership and the partner’s share of the profits (reduced by any liabilities, distributions, or losses). A partner cannot demand a profit distribution but is entitled to have her account credited with a share of the profits.

crumb: CHAPTER I ▪ PARTNERSHIP FORMATION AND RELATIONSHIPS
mee-fro-partnerships:partnership-require.html:
Intent – At least two people or entities must intend to carry on a for-profit business as co-owners (i.e., to share profits and control). The specific intent to form a partnership is not required.

Statute of Frauds – A written agreement is not required to form a partnership, but a contract that cannot be performed in one year must be in writing per the Statute of Frauds.

Profit sharing – If there is a sharing of profits from a business, then the arrangement is presumed to be a partnership except in six enumerated circumstances (mnemonic: DRAWING):

  • Debt payments Shared profits = Partnership presumed
  • Rent
  • Annuities or other retirement or health benefits
  • Wages or other compensation
  • INterest or loan charges and
  • Goodwill payments from the sale of a business.
Exam tip

A written partnership agreement is not required—but if there is a written partnership agreement, the agreement will govern partnership business.

crumb: CHAPTER I ▪ PARTNERSHIP FORMATION AND RELATIONSHIPS
mee-fro-partnerships:relationships-with-.html:
A partner is an agent of the partnership, so a partner can contractually bind the partnership when the partner acts with actual or apparent authority. And the partnership is liable for a partner’s tortious acts committed in the ordinary course of the partnership business or with actual or apparent partnership authority.

Actual vs. Apparent Authority

Actual authority

  • Express – Arises from the partnership agreement, the authorization of the partners, or a statement of authority filed with the state or
  • Implied – Arises from the partner’s reasonable belief that an action is necessary to carry out his express authority

Apparent authority

  • Arises when the partner’s unauthorized act occurred in the ordinary course of partnership business
  • Exception: Third parties with knowledge or notice of the partner’s lack of actual authority cannot hold the partnership liable
Exam tip

The rules for actual and apparent authority are similar to those in agency law.

However, a partner is jointly and severally liable for all partnership obligations that arise after the partner joins the partnership. This means that an incoming partner is generally not personally liable for prior partnership obligations.

A judgment against a partnership is not a judgment against its partners, so the judgment can only be satisfied from the partnership’s assets. But even when the judgment is also against a partner, the judgment must first be satisfied from the partnership’s assets and then the partner’s personal assets.

crumb: CHAPTER II ▪ PARTNERSHIP CHANGES AND TERMINATION
mee-fro-partnerships:termination.html:
Terminating a partnership is a two-step process: (1) dissolution and (2) winding up.

Events Causing Dissolution

Type of Partnership

Triggering Events

Partnership at will

  • A partner’s notice of withdrawal (dissociation)
  • A dissolving event set forth in the partnership agreement
  • An event making it unlawful for the partnership business to continue
  • A judicial determination

Partnership for a term or undertaking

  • The term expires or the undertaking is completed
  • All partners agree to dissolve the partnership
  • A partner is dissociated due to death, bankruptcy, or other circumstance and, within 90 days of the occurrence, at least half of the remaining partners agree to dissolve

A partnership that has dissolved continues only to wind up its business unless, before winding up is complete, all partners agree to waive the right to terminate the partnership and resume partnership business as if dissolution had never occurred.

Any partner who has not wrongfully dissociated or the legal representative of the last surviving partner may wind up the partnership’s business. That person may dispose of and transfer partnership property to discharge the partnership’s liabilities and settle the partners’ accounts. However, creditors have priority over partners to the partnership’s assets. The partnership is bound by a partner’s acts that are appropriate for winding up the partnership. And each partner is liable to the other partners for his share of partnership liability incurred by those post-dissolution acts.

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ RIGHT OF BENEFICIARIES AND CREDITORS TO DISTRIBUTION
mee-fro-trusts:alienation.html:
A beneficiary’s equitable interest in trust property is freely transferable unless a statute or trust instrument limits this right. However, once trust property is distributed to the beneficiary, any attempt to restrain the transferability is generally invalid.

A beneficiary’s creditors can reach trust principal or income only when such amounts become payable to the beneficiary or are subject to her demand.

mee-fro-trusts:ascertainable-beneficiari.html:
Beneficiaries must be identifiable by name (i.e., ascertainable) so that the equitable interest can be transferred automatically by operation of law and directly benefit the beneficiaries. The settlor may refer to acts of independent significance to identify the beneficiaries.

However, ascertainable beneficiaries are not required for trusts for the benefit of unborn children, trusts for the benefit of a reasonably definite class, or charitable trusts.

crumb: CHAPTER V ▪ TRUST MODIFICATION AND TERMINATION
mee-fro-trusts:automatic-termination.html:
A trust terminates to the extent that:

  • The trust is revoked or expires pursuant to its terms
  • No purpose of the trust remains to be achieved or
  • The purposes of the trust have become unlawful, contrary to public policy, or impossible to achieve.

A trustee may block premature termination if the trust is still serving some material purpose (e.g., discretionary trusts, support trusts, age-dependent trusts).

mee-fro-trusts:beneficiaries-right-of-en.html: The trustee is responsible for lost profits, lost interests, and other losses resulting from a breach of trust. Beneficiaries may sue the trustee and seek damages or removal of the trustee for such a breach.

mee-fro-trusts:chapter-i-introduction-to.html:
Beneficiary (holds equitable title)

Manages property for

There are three parties to a trust, but one person may serve in more than one capacity (e.g., the settlor may also be a trustee and a beneficiary).

  • Settlor: The creator of the trust (sometimes referred to as the grantor).
  • Trustee: The holder of the legal interest in or title to the trust property. The trustee manages, protects, and invests certain property (and any income generated therefrom) for the benefit of one or more named beneficiaries. A court will appoint a trustee if the settlor fails to designate one or the trustee dies.
  • Beneficiary: The holder of the equitable interest, and the person for whose benefit the property is held in trust. The beneficiary must expressly or impliedly accept this interest.

To create a trust, the settlor must have intended to do so. A trust is valid as long as it has a trustee, an ascertainable beneficiary, and assets (e.g., cash, securities, real estate, life insurance policies).

Trust property is divided between principal (i.e., original trust property and any increase in value) and income (i.e., money generated by trust property).

mee-fro-trusts:chapter-iii-remedial-trus.html:
A remedial trust is an equitable remedy created by operation of law; therefore, it is not subject to the trust-creation requirements, the Statute of Wills, or the Statute of Frauds. The sole duty of the trustee is to convey the trust property to the beneficiary, so a remedial trust terminates once this duty has been fulfilled.

  • Resulting trust: When a trust fails or there is an incomplete disposition of trust property, a court may create a resulting trust requiring the holder of the property to return it to the settlor or the settlor’s estate to prevent unjust enrichment.
  • Constructive trust: This type of trust is imposed when the court concludes that the person holding title to the property, if permitted to keep it, would profit by a wrong or be unjustly enriched. Note that wrongful conduct (e.g., fraud, undue influence) is required.
  • Gift-over clause: A clause included in some trusts that provides for the disposition of trust property if the trust purpose fails. Many jurisdictions will honor such a clause before imposing an equitable remedy.

mee-fro-trusts:chapter-ix-resignation-an.t: CHAPTER IX ▪ RESIGNATION AND REMOVAL OF TRUSTEE
mee-fro-trusts:chapter-ix-resignation-an.html:
A trustee may resign from the position by (1) providing at least 30 days’ notice to the qualified beneficiaries, the settlor (if living), and any co-trustees or (2) obtaining court approval.

A trustee may be removed by the court if:

  • The trustee becomes incapable of performing his duties
  • The trustee materially breaches one or more of his duties
  • A conflict of interest arises
  • A serious conflict develops between the trustee and any beneficiary (or between co-trustees) or
  • The trust is persistently performing poorly because of the trustee’s actions or inaction.

mee-fro-trusts:chapter-vi-principal-and-.t: CHAPTER VI ▪ PRINCIPAL AND INCOME ALLOCATIONS
mee-fro-trusts:chapter-vi-principal-and-.html:
Life beneficiaries are generally entitled to trust income, while remainder beneficiaries are generally entitled to trust principal.

  • Stock distribution, whether classified as a dividend or a split, is typically treated as a distribution of principal.
  • An amount received in exchange for trust property is generally allocated to principal, while an amount received for use of trust property is income.
  • Half of the trustee’s compensation, accounting, and other costs is charged to income, while the other half is charged to principal.

The trustee must allocate all assets to income or principal and, unless otherwise authorized by the trust instrument, the allocation must be balanced so that the life and remainder beneficiaries are treated fairly.

Allocation of Trust Property

Traditional approach (minority rule)

  • Any money generated by trust property = Income
  • Any money generated in connection with a conveyance of trust property = Principal

Modern/UPAIA approach (majority rule)

  • Trustee can recharacterize items and reallocate as reasonably necessary to fulfill the trust purposes

mee-fro-trusts:chapter-x-future-interest.html:
Identifying Future Interests in a Trust

Retained by a grantor

  • Reversion (most common): The grantor has the right to possess the property at the end of a life estate, fee tail, or term of years
  • Possibility of reverter: The grantor conveys a fee simple determinable, which automatically ends upon the happening of a specified condition or event, and the right of possession reverts to the grantor
  • Right of entry: The grantor conveys a fee simple subject to a condition subsequent, which gives the grantor the ability to exercise the right of reentry upon the happening of the condition subsequent

Given to a beneficiary

  • Remainder: Someone other than the grantor holds an interest that becomes possessory upon the expiration of a life estate, fee tail, or term of years
  • Executory interest: Someone other than the grantor holds an interest that is preceded by a vested remainder subject to divestment or a fee simple subject to an executory limitation

When a future interest in a trust is in the form of a class gift (i.e., a gift to a group of individuals), the class remains open and may admit new members until:

  • At least one class member is entitled to obtain possession of the gift or
  • The preceding interest terminates (e.g., the holder of the present life interest dies).

Unless the governing instrument provides otherwise, the general rule is that the gift is expressly limited to the transferor’s surviving children. This means that the surviving issue of a deceased child do not take.

Exam tip

When analyzing future-interest questions, ask yourself the following questions: 1. Who is entitled to possession now? 2. Who has a future interest? 3. When, if ever, will the future interest vest?

mee-fro-trusts:charitable-trusts.html:
To be a charitable trust, the trust must (1) have a stated charitable purpose and (2) exist for the benefit of the community at large or for a class of persons in which membership varies. Charitable purposes include relief of poverty, advancement of education or religion, promotion of good health, governmental purposes, and other purposes benefiting the community at large.

Charitable trusts are not subject to the Rule Against Perpetuities and may continue indefinitely. And under the cy pres doctrine, a court may modify a charitable trust to further an alternative charitable purpose if the original one becomes illegal, impracticable, or impossible to perform.

Exam tip

If it is difficult to achieve the charitable trust purpose, apply the cy pres doctrine before applying a resulting trust.

An honorary trust is a legally enforceable trust that is not created for charitable purposes and has no definite human beneficiaries. It Is usually created for pets.

mee-fro-trusts:duty-of-loyalty-and-good-.t: DUTY OF LOYALTY AND GOOD FAITH
crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ TRUST ADMINISTRATION AND TRUSTEE’S DUTIES
mee-fro-trusts:duty-of-loyalty-and-good-.html:
A trustee has the duty to (1) administer the trust in good faith and (2) act reasonably when investing property and otherwise managing the trust in the best interests of the beneficiaries.

Exam tip

On the bar exam, determine whether the trustee acted in good faith (subjective standard) and in a reasonable manner (objective standard). Good faith alone is not enough.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ TRUST ADMINISTRATION AND TRUSTEE’S DUTIES
mee-fro-trusts:duty-of-prudent-administr.html:
A trustee also has a duty to administer the trust as a prudent person and must exercise reasonable care, skill, and caution with regard to trust property. This includes the duty to:

  • Secure possession of property within a reasonable time
  • Maintain real property and
  • Segregate personal property from trust assets (the trustee is only liable for a breach of this duty if it causes damage to trust property).

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ TRUST ADMINISTRATION AND TRUSTEE’S DUTIES
mee-fro-trusts:duty-to-inform-and-accoun.html:
A trustee has a duty to disclose to the beneficiaries complete and accurate information about the nature and extent of the trust property—including allowing access to trust records and accounts. The trustee must also provide notice to the beneficiaries if she intends to sell a significant portion of the trust assets.

A trustee also has a duty to periodically account for actions taken on behalf of the trust so that the trustee’s performance can be assessed against the terms of the trust.

mee-fro-trusts:intent.html:
