The settlor’s manifestation of intent must occur prior to or simultaneously with the transfer of property. It can be done orally, in writing, or by conduct—but a writing is required when the Statute of Frauds applies (e.g., conveyance of real property) or the Statute of Wills applies (e.g., trust created in a will). Use of common trust terms (e.g., “in trust,” “trustee”) will create a presumption of intent to create a trust.

When a donor transfers property to a donee using language that expresses a hope or a wish (rather than creating a legal obligation) that such property be used for another’s benefit, then it may be considered a precatory trust (as opposed to an outright gift). But to be a precatory trust:

  • The transfer must contain specific instructions to a fiduciary and
  • It must be shown that absent imposition of a trust, there would be an unnatural disposition of the donor’s property because of familial relations or a history of support between the donor and the intended beneficiary.
Exam tip

It is important to distinguish between a gift and a trust. A gift is a revocable, outright transfer of property without compensation. A trust involves a bifurcated transfer—the settlor gives the property to one person for the benefit of another.

crumb: CHAPTER V ▪ TRUST MODIFICATION AND TERMINATION
OTHER METHODS OF MODIFICATION OR TERMINATION
mee-fro-trusts:judicial-modification-ref.html:
A court may modify or terminate a trust:

  • Due to unanticipated circumstances or the inability to manage the trust effectively or
  • If the trust is uneconomic (i.e., the value of the trust property is insufficient to justify the cost of administration).

A court may also:

  • Reform the terms of a trust to correct mistakes or
  • Modify the terms of a trust to achieve the settlor’s tax objectives.

Although the beneficiaries’ consent is not required, a beneficiary or trustee may commence a proceeding seeking court approval or disapproval of such changes.

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ RIGHT OF BENEFICIARIES AND CREDITORS TO DISTRIBUTION
mee-fro-trusts:mandatory-versus-discreti.html:
A mandatory trust is one that gives the trustee no discretion regarding payments of income or principal to the beneficiary. In contrast, a discretionary trust gives the trustee complete discretion regarding such payments. As a result, the beneficiary of a fully discretionary trust cannot challenge the trustee’s actions, absent a clear abuse of discretion.

If the trustee exercises his discretion to pay, then the beneficiary’s creditors have the same rights as the beneficiary. But if the discretion to pay is not exercised, then the beneficiary’s interest cannot be reached by creditors.

crumb: CHAPTER V ▪ TRUST MODIFICATION AND TERMINATION
mee-fro-trusts:modification-or-terminati.html:
A noncharitable irrevocable trust can be terminated or modified by consent of all beneficiaries if (1) continuance is not necessary to achieve any material purpose or (2) the modification is not inconsistent with a material purpose. A material purpose typically exists if the trust has a:

  • Spendthrift provision (but may not be independently sufficient to show that the provision is a material purpose)
  • Discretionary distribution provision
  • Distribution provision keyed to the support of the beneficiary
  • Postponement-of-enjoyment provision (e.g., a beneficiary’s right is delayed until the beneficiary reaches a specific age) or
  • Future interest (e.g., a remainder interest).

However, a modification or termination that is inconsistent with a material purpose is permitted with the settlor’s consent. But even though the Noncharitable Irrevocable Trust

trustee need not consent, any party to the trust (settlor, trustee, or beneficiary) may commence a proceeding seeking court approval or disapproval of a proposed modification or termination.

If a trust is terminated by consent, then the trustee must distribute the trust property as agreed by the beneficiaries.

If the settlor is dead, a trust may still be modified in one of two situations:

  • All beneficiaries agree to a modification consistent with the material purpose of the trust or
  • An unforeseen event has frustrated the purpose of the trust.
Exam tip

If you see a fact pattern involving unforeseen events on the exam, be very clear about what the purpose of the trust is. Remember that a trustee CANNOT terminate or modify a trust through unilateral action.

mee-fro-trusts:powers-of-trustees-and-li.t: POWERS OF TRUSTEES AND LIABILITY OF THIRD PARTIES
crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ TRUST ADMINISTRATION AND TRUSTEE’S DUTIES
mee-fro-trusts:powers-of-trustees-and-li.html:
Before any duties are imposed, the trustee must accept the trusteeship. The trustee is then charged with safeguarding the trust property and otherwise acting as a fiduciary.

The trustee has the powers necessary to act as a reasonably prudent person in managing the trust (e.g., revoke, withdraw, modify), including the implied power to contract, sell, lease, or transfer the trust property.

A third party can potentially be held liable for her role in a breach of trust. Therefore, third parties must act in good faith and give valuable consideration when dealing with trustees. But third parties are not liable if they act without actual knowledge that such action constitutes a breach of trust.

mee-fro-trusts:private-express-trust.html: A private express trust clearly states the intention of the settlor to transfer property to a trustee for the benefit of one or more ascertainable beneficiaries. A private trust is created when a settlor with capacity to create a trust manifests a present intent to transfer ownership of the property to a trustee who has duties to perform for the benefit of one or more ascertainable beneficiaries for a valid purpose. No consideration is required.

crumb: CHAPTER V ▪ TRUST MODIFICATION AND TERMINATION
mee-fro-trusts:revocable-trusts.html:
A revocable trust is a trust that can be amended or terminated by the settlor. Under the common law, a trust is presumed to be irrevocable. But in most jurisdictions and under the Uniform Trust Code (UTC), a trust is revocable unless the trust documents say otherwise.

Exam tip

If the essay does not expressly state which rule applies, then discuss the traditional rule and the majority (UTC) rule but apply only the majority rule in your analysis.

A settlor can amend or revoke a revocable trust by substantially complying with a method provided within the terms of the trust. If no such terms are provided, then the trust can be amended or revoked by:

  • A later will or codicil that expressly refers to the trust or specifically devises property that otherwise would have passed according to the terms of the trust or
  • Any other method manifesting clear and convincing evidence of the settlor’s intent.

When a trust is created or funded by multiple settlors, a settlor’s ability to amend or revoke the trust depends on whether it contains community property.

  • Community property: The trust may be revoked by either spouse acting alone, but it can only be amended by joint action of both spouses.
  • Noncommunity property: Each settlor may revoke or amend the trust with regard to the portion of the trust property attributable to that settlor’s contribution.

When a revocable trust is revoked, the trustee must deliver the trust property as the settlor directs. But a trustee is not liable to the settlor or the settlor’s successors in interest for distributions made, and other actions taken, under the belief that the trust had not been amended or revoked.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ TRUST ADMINISTRATION AND TRUSTEE’S DUTIES
DUTY OF LOYALTY AND GOOD FAITH
mee-fro-trusts:self-dealing.html:
When a trustee personally engages in a transaction involving trust property, a conflict of interest arises between the trustee’s duties to the beneficiaries and the trustee’s personal interest.

The following are generally prohibited transactions with trust property:

  • Buying or selling trust assets to himself (even at fair market value)
  • Selling property of one trust to another trust that the trustee manages
  • Borrowing from or making loans to the trust
  • Using trust assets to secure a personal loan
  • Engaging in prohibited transactions with friends or relatives
  • Otherwise acting for personal gain through the trustee position

When self-dealing is at issue, there is an irrebuttable presumption that the trustee breached the duty of loyalty. This means that no further inquiry into the trustee’s reasonableness or good faith is needed because self-dealing is a per se breach.

Even when self-dealing is authorized (by the settlor, court order, or all beneficiaries), the transaction must still be reasonable and fair to avoid liability for breach.

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ RIGHT OF BENEFICIARIES AND CREDITORS TO DISTRIBUTION
mee-fro-trusts:spendthrift-trust.html:
A spendthrift trust expressly restricts the beneficiary’s power to voluntarily or involuntarily transfer her equitable interest. This restriction applies only while the property remains in the trust. It is inapplicable once property has been paid out to the beneficiary.

Creditors usually cannot reach a beneficiary’s trust interest if the governing instrument contains a spendthrift clause. However, the following classes of creditors can still reach a beneficiary’s assets:

  • Children and spouses entitled to support
  • Holders of federal or state tax liens and
  • In some jurisdictions, providers of necessaries (e.g., health care).

crumb: CHAPTER IV ▪ RIGHT OF BENEFICIARIES AND CREDITORS TO DISTRIBUTION
mee-fro-trusts:support-trust.html:
A support trust directs the trustee to pay income or principal as necessary—including maintaining the lifestyle to which the beneficiary is accustomed—to support the beneficiary.

Creditors generally cannot reach the assets of a support trust. However, a creditor who provided a necessity to the beneficiary can be paid directly by the trustee.

mee-fro-trusts:testamentary-trusts.html:
A testamentary trust arises when the terms of the trust are written in a will or in a document incorporated by reference in a will. A testamentary trust must comply with the Statute of Wills. If not, it may still be a constructive trust or resulting trust, depending on whether it is “secret” or “semi-secret.”

  • “Secret” trust: It looks like a testamentary gift but is created in reliance on the named beneficiary’s promise to hold and administer the property for another. A constructive trust is imposed on the property for the intended beneficiary.
  • “Semi-secret” trust: It occurs when a will directs that a gift be held in trust but the testator fails to name a beneficiary or specify the terms or purpose of the trust. A resulting trust is imposed on the property to be held for the testator’s heirs.

Although most jurisdictions still respect the common-law distinction between “secret” and “semi-secret” trusts, the modern trend and the Restatement (Third) of Trusts impose a constructive trust in favor of the intended beneficiaries (if known) in both “secret” and “semi-secret” trust situations.

mee-fro-trusts:third-parties.html:
A trustee is personally liable on contracts entered into and for tortious acts committed while acting as the trustee. The trustee can seek indemnity from the trust if the trustee was acting within the scope of her duties.

When a trustee improperly transfers property as a result of a breach of trust to a third party who is not a bona fide purchaser, a beneficiary or successor trustee may have the transaction set aside.

crumb: CHAPTER VII ▪ TRUST ADMINISTRATION AND TRUSTEE’S DUTIES
mee-fro-trusts:trust-investments.html:
The trustee must act as a prudent investor would act when investing his own property, and the trustee must exercise reasonable care, caution, and skill when investing and managing trust assets. A trustee’s investments are assessed by looking at the total performance of the trust (i.e., the portfolio approach), as opposed to individual investments. Factors considered in determining compliance include:

  • The trust’s distribution requirements
  • General economic conditions
  • The investment’s role in relation to the trust’s overall investment portfolio and
  • The trust’s need for liquidity, income regularity, and preservation or appreciation of capital.

A trustee’s duties regarding trust investments also include the following:

  • Duty to diversify assets: The trustee must adequately diversify trust investments to spread the risk of loss unless the administrative costs of doing so would outweigh the benefits.
  • Duty to make property productive: The trustee must pursue all possible claims, derive the maximum amount of income from investments, sell assets when appropriate, secure insurance, pay expenses, and act within a reasonable time in all matters.
  • Duty to be impartial: A trustee must be impartial in dealing with beneficiaries of a trust, but this does not mean that each beneficiary must be treated equally. Instead, this duty requires that a trustee:
    • Remain uninfluenced by favoritism or animosity
    • Balance the interests of present and future beneficiaries by investing property so that it produces a reasonable income while preserving the principal for the remaindermen and
    • Sell trust property within a reasonable time if a failure to diversify would be inconsistent with the total-performance portfolio approach.
Exam tip

If a fact pattern indicates that (1) the trust principal is appreciating but not generating a reasonable stream of income or (2) the trust is producing a good amount of income but the principal is depreciating, then your analysis should center on the duty of impartiality. In these situations, the trustee may be favoring one class of beneficiaries over another.

mee-fro-trusts:trust-property.html:
Trust property must be (1) identifiable, (2) segregated, and (3) described with reasonable certainty. The requirement of identifiable trust property (i.e., res) distinguishes a trust from a debt.

A trust also must be funded, meaning that it must contain some property that—at the time the trust was created—was (1) owned by the settlor and (2) transferred to the trust or the trustee. If a trust that was invalid for lack of assets is later funded, then a trust arises if the settlor remanifests the intent to create a trust.

Exam tip

Know the difference between trusts and debts. A debt can be paid using money from any source, while a trust necessarily involves a segregated source of funds.

mee-fro-trusts:types-of-express-trusts.html:
Inter Vivos Trusts

Inter vivos trusts are lifetime transfers in trust. A simple declaration of trust will usually suffice to create an inter vivos trust if the settlor is also the trustee. But if a third-party trustee is named, the declaration must be accompanied by a delivery in which the settlor parts with dominion and control over the trust property.

No writing is required to transfer personal property to a trust. But a writing is required to transfer real property, and a court will impose a constructive trust if the writing is lacking. Additionally, evidence outside of the written agreement (i.e., parol evidence) is admissible to show the settlor’s intent only if the agreement is ambiguous on its face.

Types of Inter Vivos Trusts

Pourover trust

A will provision that directs the distribution of property to a trust upon the happening of an event.

This is permissible—even if the trust instrument was not executed in accordance with the Statute of Wills—if the trust is identified in the will and its terms are set forth in a written instrument.

Totten trust

A designation given to a bank account in a depositor’s name as trustee for a named beneficiary (no separation of legal and equitable title).

This trust can be revoked by any lifetime act manifesting the depositor’s intent to revoke or by will.

Life-insurance trust

When proceeds from a life-insurance policy go to the trust upon the insured’s death. The trust owns the insurance policy, and the trust is irrevocable.

Living trust

This trust arises when a settlor names himself as trustee until death. However, the settlor can change the successor trustee and beneficiaries until his death.

Trust property is not protected from creditors or federal estate taxation.

mee-fro-trusts:valid-trust-purpose.html:
A trust can be created for any purpose that is (1) not illegal, restricted by law, or contrary to public policy, (2) possible to achieve, and (3) for the benefit of its beneficiaries.

If one of several trust terms violates public policy, then any alternative term will be honored. If no alternative term is provided, then the problematic term will be stricken. The trust will not fail unless removal of that term is fatal.
